title: Retroperitoneal ‘uterus-like mass’ of cervical origin
summary: This case report describes a rare retroperitoneal mass of cervical origin that presented as a uterus-like structure communicating with the endocervix and causing significant clinical symptoms including abdominal pain, bloating, and irregular vaginal bleeding with postcoital hemorrhage. The pathogenesis of this anomaly is proposed to involve metaplasia—the transformation of normal tissue into different cell types—which is promoted by elevated estrogen levels and further accelerated by the hyperoestrogenic state of pregnancy. The hormonal mechanisms underlying this condition highlight how estrogen-driven tissue remodeling can lead to ectopic tissue formation in unexpected anatomical locations. This case illustrates the complex interplay between hormonal regulation and aberrant tissue development, contributing to understanding of how metaplastic processes similar to those implicated in endometriosis can produce unusual gynecological pathology. The clinical presentation with severe bleeding symptoms underscores the importance of recognizing such rare congenital anomalies in the differential diagnosis of women with abnormal uterine bleeding and abdominal pain.

title: Hysterectomy, oophorectomy and risk of Non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma
summary:
# Summary

This large prospective study demonstrates that hysterectomy is associated with a substantially increased risk of non-Hodgkin's lymphoma (NHL), particularly when performed before age 55 and specifically for the diffuse large B-cell lymphoma subtype, with a 23-26% increased hazard ratio independent of oophorectomy status. The association is markedly modified by hormone therapy use, being confined to women who never used postoperative hormones and those with unopposed estrogen exposure. The proposed biological mechanism involves estrogen's immunosuppressive effects through binding to estrogen receptors (ER-β) found on B and T lymphocytes and in lymphoma cells; in premenopausal women, higher estrogen levels suppress pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-6, interleukin-8, and tumor necrosis factor-α, and hysterectomy-induced hormonal alterations combined with surgical trauma may disrupt this immune suppression and promote B-cell proliferation. The age-dependent effect suggests that the timing and duration of hormonal deprivation following hysterectomy influence NHL development, with earlier procedures conferring greater risk, likely reflecting longer exposure to altered immune regulation during the critical decades of remaining life expectancy.

title: Cell Chirality of Micropatterned Endometrial Microvascular Endothelial Cells
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated how extracellular matrix composition, stiffness, and soluble factors regulate the chirality (directional orientation) of endometrial microvascular endothelial cells (HEMECs) and vascular network organization. The researchers found that specific collagen combinations, particularly Collagen I alone or with Lumican, promoted left-oriented cell chirality and high attachment, while Fibronectin with Hyaluronic Acid induced clockwise orientation. Notably, cortisol emerged as a potent modulator of HEMEC chirality across multiple extracellular matrix substrates, exerting stronger effects than VEGF and reducing vascular branch length at physiologically relevant concentrations. Progesterone specifically increased branch number while decreasing branch length, demonstrating hormone-specific effects on vascular network architecture. The findings indicate that cellular-scale chirality correlates with vascular network parameters, suggesting that changes in endometrial cell orientation driven by hormonal fluctuations during the menstrual cycle may fundamentally govern endometrial vascularization and have implications for understanding endometrial pathologies including endometriosis.

title: Gut Microbial β-Glucuronidases Influence Endobiotic Homeostasis and Are Modulated by Diverse Therapeutics
summary:
# Summary

Gut microbial β-glucuronidase (GUS) enzymes regulate endobiotic homeostasis by reactivating conjugated forms of estradiol, estrone, serotonin, dopamine, and thyroxine in the intestinal lumen, with Loop 1 and FMN GUS classes showing the highest activity toward these substrates. Conventional mice with intact microbiota exhibit significantly elevated fecal levels of active endobiotics compared to germ-free mice, with sex-dependent differences in neurotransmitter and hormone profiles. Specific bacterial taxa, including Lachnospiraceae, Ruminococcus gnavus, and Roseburia hominis, drive reactivation of particular endobiotics, and their GUS protein abundances correlate with processing rates of hormone and neurotransmitter glucuronides. FDA-approved drugs containing piperazine or piperidine moieties inhibit microbial GUS activity and reduce circulating levels of active hormones and neurotransmitters, offering a mechanistic link between therapeutic drug efficacy and modulation of endobiotic reactivation. These findings illuminate how microbial enzyme activity influences systemic hormone and neurotransmitter homeostasis, with implications for hormone-dependent disorders including endometriosis where dysregulated estrogen reactivation promotes disease pathology.

title: Multi-Cellular Engineered Living Systems to Assess Reproductive Toxicology
summary: This study demonstrates that multi-cellular engineered living systems can effectively model reproductive toxicology and endometrial pathophysiology. Toxicants induce multiple adverse effects on reproduction through altered hormone production, decreased sperm function, heightened inflammation, epigenetic changes, oxidative stress, and immune dysfunction, ultimately leading to implantation complications and miscarriages. The researchers engineered follicles with theca and granulosa cells that produce steroids and hormonal signals to the pituitary, with optimized substrate stiffness and shear stress conditions enhancing oocyte maturation and hormone secretion. For endometrial modeling, physiologically relevant shear stress (0.0001–0.00025 Pa) recapitulated endometriosis pathophysiology, while PGE2 and progesterone induced decidualization in engineered endometrial tissues. The contraceptive levonorgestrel induced endometrial damage through loss of tissue integrity and vascular regression, whereas progesterone treatment inhibited extracellular matrix production and tissue stiffness. These engineered systems using decellularized extracellular matrix scaffolds provide powerful platforms to assess how toxicants and drugs disrupt reproductive hormone signaling and endometrial function.

title: Influence of gut microbiome on metabolic diseases: a new perspective based on microgravity
journal: Journal of Diabetes and Metabolic Disorders
summary: Microgravity exposure impairs multiple physiological systems including cardiovascular function, bone density, and metabolism through alterations to the gut microbiome composition and functionality. The exposure induces notable changes in microbial diversity that subsequently trigger metabolic dysfunction via dysregulated energy metabolism, cardiovascular disease, and bone metabolism disorders. Microgravity-induced microbiome alterations also lead to liver dysfunction and pregnancy complications, establishing the gut microbiota as a critical intermediary in gravity-dependent metabolic homeostasis. These findings suggest that gravitational forces fundamentally regulate the microbial ecosystem's capacity to maintain metabolic health, with implications for understanding microbial contributions to metabolic disease pathogenesis. The research highlights how environmental stressors like microgravity can perturb the host-microbiome axis, resulting in systemic metabolic dysregulation across multiple organ systems. This novel perspective repositions the gut microbiome as a mechanistic link between gravitational stress and metabolic disease development.

title: Progesterone and Allopregnanolone Neuroprotective Effects in the Wobbler Mouse Model of Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis
summary: This study demonstrates that progesterone exerts neuroprotective effects in a mouse model of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Progesterone reduces motoneuron damage and death while simultaneously activating protective pathways, including upregulation of antioxidant enzymes like Mn-dependent superoxide dismutase and downregulation of pro-death mitochondrial nitric oxide synthase, thereby preserving mitochondrial function and reducing oxidative stress. The hormone also enhances brain-derived neurotrophic factor production, promotes cholinergic motoneuron differentiation, increases oligodendrocyte density, and suppresses harmful glial responses by downregulating astrogliosis and microgliosis. Progesterone's local conversion to allopregnanolone in neural tissue amplifies these protective effects, independently reducing oxidative stress and glial pathology while improving motoneuron viability. These combined actions result in measurable clinical improvements, including enhanced motoneuron survival and increased muscle strength in motor behavior tests. The findings suggest that neuroactive steroids, particularly progesterone and its metabolite allopregnanolone, represent promising therapeutic candidates for treating amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and potentially other neurodegenerative conditions.

title: The role of RAD51 regulators and variants in primary ovarian insufficiency, endometriosis, and polycystic ovary syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study comprehensively examines how variants in RAD51 and its regulatory proteins contribute to three reproductive disorders: primary ovarian insufficiency (POI), endometriosis, and polycystic ovary syndrome. RAD51 and its regulators, including DMC1, RAD51B, SWS1, SPIDR, XRCC2, and BRCA2, are essential for homologous recombination and double-strand break repair during germ cell development, processes critical for proper oogenesis and chromosome segregation. Specific mutations in these genes—such as DMC1 M200V, RAD51 E68G, RAD51B c.92delT, and multiple SWS1 variants—impair DNA repair functions like homology search, strand exchange, and RAD51 loading, leading to reduced follicle counts, premature ovarian failure, and infertility. Additionally, variants in XRCC3, BRCA1, and CSB are associated with endometriosis, while BRCA1 variants also link to PCOS, suggesting that DNA repair pathway disruption broadly affects reproductive health. Current treatment approaches for POI involve hormone replacement with estradiol and progesterone, though identifying reliable biomarkers for ovarian reserve remains a clinical challenge.

title: Uterine and Ovarian Histopathology After Testosterone for Gender Affirmation: A Systematic Review
summary: Testosterone therapy for gender affirmation produces diverse effects on endometrial and ovarian histopathology in treated individuals. Nearly half of 521 patients studied exhibited endometrial atrophy, while 46.6% maintained proliferative endometrium, indicating variable endometrial responses to androgenic stimulation. Importantly, testosterone does not increase risk of unopposed estrogen-induced precancerous lesions; only 2.3% of patients developed endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia with one focal adenocarcinoma case, suggesting testosterone-induced amenorrhea may actually protect against estrogen-dependent endometrial pathology. Ovarian changes included polycystic-appearing morphology in 34.9% of patients, though malignant transformation was not observed and benign neoplasms were rare (0.7%). These findings indicate that testosterone suppresses normal menstrual cycling and dramatically alters endometrial and ovarian architecture, generally without promoting significant malignant transformation despite substantial histological remodeling.

title: The Impact of High Adiposity on Endometrial Progesterone Response and Metallothionein Regulation
journal: The Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism
summary: High adiposity impairs the normal progesterone-mediated response in the endometrium by suppressing metallothionein gene expression in epithelial cells, a protective mechanism normally activated by progesterone signaling. In obese women, reduced endometrial progesterone responsiveness prevents adequate upregulation of metallothionein genes, which normally protect against oxidative DNA damage through their antioxidant properties. This dysregulation results in increased oxidative stress and DNA damage accumulation in the endometrial stroma and epithelial compartments. Additionally, high adiposity downregulates genes essential for endometrial secretory phase function, further compromising endometrial receptivity and function. These findings suggest that obesity-associated impairment of the progesterone-metallothionein protective pathway contributes to altered endometrial pathology and may contribute to the reproductive dysfunction observed in obese women with endometriosis or infertility.

title: Soluble Markers of Viral Rebound and Post-Treatment HIV Control
journal: Current opinion in HIV and AIDS
summary:
# Summary

This review identifies multiple soluble plasma biomarkers that predict viral rebound and post-treatment HIV control following antiretroviral therapy interruption. Residual viremia and HIV blips exceeding 400 copies/ml during suppressive therapy associate with faster viral rebound, while HIV harbored in platelets correlates with low CD4+ counts and poor immune recovery. Soluble viral proteins including gp120 and Nef are detected in plasma and associate with elevated inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-10, TNF-α) and reduced CD4+ counts. The neutralization capacity of autologous antibodies during treatment and broadly neutralizing antibody responses predict post-treatment control, with IgG glycosylation patterns influencing both rebound timing and Fc-mediated immune functions. Elevated inflammatory markers (CXCL10, CXCL9, CD14) and bacterial translocation indicators (LBP, sCD14, sCD163) preceding detectable rebound suggest chronic immune activation as a driver of viral resurgence. Additionally, metabolic dysregulation linked to gut dysbiosis—including elevated trimethylamine N-oxide and lysophospholipids—accelerates rebound, while protective metabolites like L-glutamic acid delay it, establishing metabolic and immunological factors as key determinants of post-treatment HIV control.

title: Plasma estrogen levels and aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage in women
summary: This study investigates the relationship between plasma estrogen levels and aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage (aSAH) risk in women. While epidemiological evidence shows that post-menopausal women and those with shorter reproductive lifespans have higher incidence of cerebral aneurysm rupture, direct measurement of individual estrogen metabolites (estrone, estradiol, estriol) and sex hormone-binding globulin revealed no significant association with aSAH risk. The mechanism by which estrogens may protect against aneurysmal rupture involves estrogen receptor-mediated stimulation of nitric oxide production, which promotes vascular wall vasodilation and maintains vascular integrity. Cigarette smoking emerges as a critical risk factor, reducing circulating estrone and estradiol levels while simultaneously promoting arterial wall remodeling and weakening, thereby contributing to increased aneurysm rupture risk. Notably, the same smoking-related estrogen suppression that increases cerebral aneurysm risk paradoxically reduces risk of endometriosis and uterine fibroids. The findings suggest that reproductive hormone status influences cerebral aneurysm vulnerability through mechanisms beyond simple plasma estrogen concentration.

title: Sex Hormone: A Potential Target at Treating Female Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease?
journal: Journal of Clinical and Experimental Hepatology
summary: This comprehensive review examines sex hormones as potential therapeutic targets for metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) in females. The study identifies multiple hormonal pathways involved in MASLD pathophysiology, including estrogen, androgen, sex hormone-binding globulin, follicle-stimulating hormone, and prolactin, all of which show strong involvement in disease development. Sex hormone levels are tightly linked to MASLD susceptibility in women, and while males exhibit higher baseline MASLD prevalence, sex hormone modulation represents a promising treatment avenue specifically in female populations. The authors analyzed hormonal contributions in distinct female disease contexts, including polycystic ovarian syndrome and menopause, suggesting that targeted hormonal interventions could address MASLD in these high-risk groups. These findings highlight sex hormones as actionable molecular targets for developing sex-specific therapeutic strategies in female MASLD management.

title: Electroacupuncture enhances the mitophagy of granulosa cells in premature ovarian insufficiency model mice by inactivating the hippo-yes-associated protein/transcriptional co-activator with postsynaptic density protein, drosophila disc large tumor suppressor, and zonula occludens-1 protein binding motif pathway
summary: Electroacupuncture applied at the Guanyuan acupoint improves premature ovarian insufficiency in mice through multiple coordinated mechanisms involving granulosa cell protection and hormonal restoration. The treatment reduces elevated levels of follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone while elevating estrogen and anti-müllerian hormone, thereby restoring normal ovarian endocrine function. Electroacupuncture inhibits granulosa cell apoptosis and enhances mitophagy—the selective removal of damaged mitochondria—by activating the Hippo-YAP/TAZ signaling pathway, which directly regulates both apoptotic and autophagy processes. These pathway effects result in improved mitochondrial integrity and reduced cell death, protecting the ovarian reserve from degeneration. The involvement of the Hippo-YAP/TAZ pathway was confirmed by using verteporfin to inhibit this cascade, which reversed the beneficial effects of electroacupuncture on granulosa cell survival and mitochondrial quality control. These findings establish a mechanistic link between traditional acupuncture therapy and modern cellular biology, demonstrating that electroacupuncture restores ovarian function by preventing granulosa cell failure through enhanced mitochondrial homeostasis.

title: Impact of BRACTS exercises on muscular strength in eumenorrheic women
summary: This study examined how BRACTS exercises (bending, roll-ups, arm swings with loads, crunches, squats, and tandem walks) affect muscular strength in eumenorrheic women across different menstrual cycle phases. The research demonstrates that the BRACTS exercise protocol significantly improves hand grip strength and quadriceps strength throughout the follicular, mid-cycle, and luteal phases compared to a control group performing brisk walking. These improvements occur within the context of fluctuating sex steroid hormones, particularly estrogen and progesterone, which naturally vary across the menstrual cycle and influence muscular strength, muscle glycogen storage, and exercise performance. The magnitude of strength gains varied by menstrual cycle phase, with hand grip strength showing maximum improvements during the follicular phase (characterized by low estrogen and progesterone) and quadriceps and gastro-soleus strength peaking during the mid-cycle phase (marked by elevated estrogen). The findings suggest that targeted resistance and mobility exercises can enhance muscular strength independent of hormonal fluctuations, while also highlighting the complex interplay between sex steroid hormones, physical activity, and muscular performance across the menstrual cycle. These results have potential implications for exercise prescription in women with hormonal dysregulation, including conditions like endometriosis.

title: Role of sex hormone-binding globulin in the free hormone hypothesis and the relevance of free testosterone in androgen physiology
journal: Cellular and Molecular Life Sciences: CMLS
summary: This review examines the critical role of sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) and albumin in regulating free testosterone availability and bioactivity through the free hormone hypothesis. The equilibrium between free and total testosterone determines tissue exposure to androgens, and this balance is dynamically influenced by age, genetic factors, and various pathological conditions that alter SHBG levels. Free testosterone demonstrates significant associations with clinical outcomes including bone health and frailty, and its accurate determination is essential for proper diagnosis of male hypogonadism, preventing both under- and over-diagnosis when total testosterone levels are borderline or SHBG is altered. While direct immunoassays are unreliable, methods such as liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) coupled with equilibrium dialysis provide accurate free testosterone measurement, with the Vermeulen calculator offering a practical alternative despite inherent limitations. These findings underscore that measuring free testosterone rather than relying solely on total testosterone provides clinically meaningful information for understanding androgen physiology and diagnosing hormonal disorders. The dynamic regulation of free testosterone bioavailability represents a fundamental mechanism through which endocrine conditions modulate hormonal exposure at the tissue level.

title: Influencing factors of polyspermy during in vitro fertilization with antagonist protocol
summary:
# Summary

This study examined factors influencing polyspermy during in vitro fertilization using antagonist protocol ovulation induction. Elevated luteinizing hormone (LH) levels on the day of human chorionic gonadotropin injection emerged as an independent risk factor strongly associated with polyspermy occurrence. Polyspermic fertilization significantly reduced both egg utilization rates and the number of available embryos for transfer, compromising overall fertility outcomes. The quality of oocytes obtained through ovulation induction directly affects zona pellucida and cortical reactions, with immature eggs showing incomplete defensive mechanisms that increase polyspermy susceptibility. While sperm concentration, female age, and hormone levels including progesterone and estradiol showed moderate associations with polyspermy, the addition of appropriate LH levels during antagonist protocol stimulation can improve embryo outcomes. These findings suggest that careful monitoring and optimization of hormonal parameters, particularly LH levels, during controlled ovarian hyperstimulation is critical for minimizing polyspermy and improving in vitro fertilization success rates.

title: Hormone receptor profile of ectopic and eutopic endometrium in adenomyosis: a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis is characterized by elevated estrogen receptor alpha (ERα) expression in both ectopic lesions and eutopic endometrium, creating a state of local estrogen dominance that drives epithelial and stromal cell proliferation through estrogen-ERα signaling. During the proliferative phase, estrogen activates ERα and subsequently upregulates progesterone receptor (PR), but adenomyotic tissue exhibits progesterone resistance partly due to reduced PR-B isoform expression and elevated ERα levels that appear to override normal progesterone-mediated downregulation of these receptors. Estrogen also promotes epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, enhanced uterine contractility, and inflammation in adenomyosis, while compensatory mechanisms involving estrogen receptor beta (ERβ) and G protein-coupled estrogen receptor (GPER) appear dysregulated, with GPER specifically reduced in adenomyotic epithelium. Additional factors including androgen receptor expression and potential KRAS mutations contribute to the pathophysiology, yet despite adenomyosis being hormone-dependent, the condition remains therapeutically refractory to conventional hormonal treatments. This hormone receptor dysregulation—particularly the imbalance between proliferative ERα-estrogen signaling and deficient progesterone responsiveness—underlies the fundamental difficulty in managing adenomyosis medically.

title: Reproductive and sexual health concerns in gastrointestinal illness: a narrative review
summary:
# Summary

The menstrual cycle profoundly influences gastrointestinal function through hormonal and neurobiological mechanisms, with the luteal phase characterized by increased abdominal pain and diarrhea in both healthy women and those with gastrointestinal disorders. The hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis initiates this cyclical regulation: gonadotropin-releasing hormone from the hypothalamus stimulates luteinizing and follicle-stimulating hormones, which act on the ovaries to produce estrogen and progesterone. These ovarian hormones, which bind to sex hormone receptors present throughout the intestines, reduce gastrointestinal motility and modulate nociceptive processing and visceral sensitivity, explaining why women with irritable bowel syndrome and inflammatory bowel disease experience cyclical symptom fluctuations that can be ameliorated by oral contraceptive pills. Additionally, active inflammatory bowel disease disrupts this cyclical pattern while simultaneously causing significant reproductive complications including menstrual irregularities, reduced ovarian reserve as indicated by declining anti-Müllerian hormone levels, and infertility mediated through pelvic inflammation, adhesions, and surgical sequelae. Psychological factors including anxiety, depression, hypervigilance, and catastrophizing further amplify gastrointestinal symptom severity during the menstrual cycle, highlighting the complex interplay between hormonal, inflammatory, and neuropsychological mechanisms in patients with concurrent gastrointestinal and reproductive health concerns.

title: Unraveling the Clinical FSH Conundrum: Insights From the Small Ovarian Reserve Heifer Model
summary:
# Summary

This study uses a small ovarian reserve heifer model to investigate the paradoxical "FSH conundrum" in fertility treatment, where excessive FSH dosing paradoxically reduces rather than improves reproductive outcomes. FSH is essential for ovarian follicular development and activates a comprehensive gene expression program including upregulation of FSH and LH receptors, aromatase, estradiol receptors, and growth factors like BMP-15 and FGF proteins, while simultaneously inhibiting apoptotic genes and promoting oocyte competence and cumulus cell expansion. However, pharmacological FSH doses during assisted reproductive technology cause follicular hyperstimulation dysgenesis, characterized by premature cumulus expansion and oocyte maturation that impairs oocyte quality and fertilizability. Clinical data demonstrate that excessive FSH doses are inversely correlated with live birth rates, with the lowest total FSH doses (≤2000 IU) associated with the highest success rates, suggesting that milder stimulation protocols improve embryo survival. The findings reveal that artificially elevated FSH concentrations override the natural FSH-regulated selection process and disrupt coordinated gene expression patterns critical for normal follicular and oocyte development, indicating that optimizing FSH dosage rather than maximizing stimulation intensity is crucial for improving fertility outcomes.

title: Physical activities and breast cancer: a Mendelian randomization study
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study demonstrates that walking for pleasure significantly reduces the risk of estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer (ER+ BC), with an odds ratio of 0.302, identifying it as an effective preventive activity among various physical activity types. Physical activity exerts protective effects against breast cancer primarily through hormonal and metabolic pathways: it reduces circulating levels of endogenous estrogen, insulin, insulin-like growth factor (IGF-I), and free estrogen while simultaneously elevating sex hormone-binding globulin, thereby decreasing the bioavailability of estrogen that drives ER+ BC cell proliferation. Additionally, physical activity reduces obesity-related increases in free estrogen levels and enhances immune function through increased natural killer cells and lymphocytes, providing multiple protective mechanisms. In contrast, other physical activity types such as heavy DIY, light DIY, and strenuous sports showed no causal relationship with breast cancer risk, suggesting that moderate leisure activities like walking may be more beneficial than high-intensity exercise. The study highlights estrogen receptor status as a critical determinant of breast cancer heterogeneity and responsiveness to hormonal pathways modulated by physical activity, establishing reduced physical activity as a crucial cancer risk factor in women.

title: Association between the intake of potentially risky beverages and the occurrence of endometrial polyps: a case–control study
summary: This case-control study demonstrates that consumption of potentially risky beverages (PRBs)—particularly sugar-sweetened beverages and milk tea—is significantly associated with increased risk of endometrial polyps, with dose-dependent effects observed at both moderate and high intake levels. The pathogenic mechanisms linking PRB consumption to endometrial polyps involve multiple interconnected pathways: excessive sugar intake promotes obesity and insulin resistance, leading to elevated estrogen secretion from adipose tissue, which stimulates endometrial hyperplasia and polyp formation; concurrently, high-sugar diets trigger chronic low-grade inflammation and hyperglycemic conditions that increase proinflammatory cytokine production and disrupt immune function. Endometrial polyps themselves are characterized by hormonal imbalances and inflammatory responses that reduce endometrial receptivity and inhibit embryo implantation and development, ultimately compromising fertility. The study also identifies protective dietary factors, noting that consumption of plant protein and vitamin K reduces endometriosis incidence, while animal protein, heme iron, and high glycemic loads are associated with increased endometrial pathology. These findings highlight the critical role of dietary choices in modulating endometrial health through hormonal and inflammatory mechanisms relevant to both endometrial polyps and endometriosis.

title: The application of progesterone-primed ovarian stimulation protocol in patients with ovarian endometriosis combined with diminished ovarian reserve
summary: Ovarian endometriosis is associated with diminished ovarian reserve through multiple mechanisms, including cyclic bleeding and cyst formation that impair oocyte quality, reduce antral follicle counts, and lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels. The progesterone-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) protocol, utilizing medroxyprogesterone acetate, offers a superior alternative to traditional gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist downregulation in this patient population by suppressing premature luteinizing hormone surges while maintaining low estradiol to inhibit ectopic endometrial tissue growth and reduce local inflammation through decreased RANTES expression. The PPOS protocol achieves comparable pregnancy outcomes to standard downregulation while requiring significantly shorter gonadotropin treatment duration and lower total gonadotropin doses, thereby reducing patient burden and treatment costs. Mechanistically, medroxyprogesterone acetate acts at the hypothalamic level without causing pituitary suppression and alters microRNA expression patterns in granulosa cells, potentially improving oocyte competence. This protocol effectively addresses the dual challenge of managing endometriotic lesions while optimizing ovarian responsiveness in patients with concurrent diminished ovarian reserve, representing an evidence-based advancement in assisted reproductive technology for this challenging clinical population.

title: Association of ESR1 Xba1 (rs9340799) With Male Infertility: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

The ESR1 gene, located on chromosome 6q25 and encoding estrogen receptor alpha, plays a critical role in male reproductive function through regulation of spermatogenesis and spermiogenesis. This meta-analysis demonstrates that the Xba1 polymorphism (rs9340799) in ESR1 is associated with male infertility risk, with the G allele conferring protection against infertility overall and the AA genotype showing protective effects in specific populations. The protective genotypes correlate with improved semen parameters including higher sperm concentration and motility, while being associated with altered sex hormone-binding globulin, testosterone, and estradiol levels. ESR1 is expressed across reproductive tissues including the testes, pituitary, hypothalamus, and prostate, where estrogen receptor alpha mediates estrogen signaling essential for normal spermatogenesis and sperm maturation through regulation of nucleoproteins and spermiogenesis progression. Disruption of estrogen receptor alpha signaling—either through genetic variation or abnormal testosterone-to-estrogen ratios—impairs semen quality and fertility, whereas aromatase inhibitors that restore proper sex hormone balance can improve sperm parameters. This analysis underscores estrogen's essential but finely-balanced role in male reproduction alongside its known involvement in endometriosis and other estrogen-dependent conditions.

title: Peritoneal Endometriosis Impairs Ovarian Reserve and Increases Atresia in a Rat Model
summary: Peritoneal endometriosis significantly impairs ovarian reserve through disruption of folliculogenesis at multiple developmental stages, reducing primordial, primary, preantral, and late-antral follicles while simultaneously promoting follicular atresia in a rat model. The disease induces widespread apoptosis in ovarian structures, including increased TUNEL-positive late-antral follicles and apoptotic cells in the corpus luteum, accompanied by dysregulated apoptotic signaling characterized by elevated procaspase 3 and BCL-XS/BCL-2 and BCL-XS/BCL-XL ratios alongside decreased BCL-2 expression. Anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), a key indicator of ovarian reserve produced by granulosa cells, is significantly reduced in endometriosis, with in vitro studies showing that 12Z endometriotic cells suppress KITLG (KIT ligand) expression while increasing AMHR2 in granulosa cells, thereby disrupting critical signaling pathways necessary for follicle activation and maintenance. These molecular alterations directly compromise the KIT/KL pathway and mTOR signaling, which normally promote oocyte maturation and granulosa cell proliferation through GDF-9 and PI3K activation. The study demonstrates that peritoneal endometriosis-induced ovarian dysfunction substantially contributes to the infertility phenotype associated with the disease, offering mechanistic insights into how endometriosis compromises reproductive potential beyond anatomical factors.

title: Association of Basal Serum Androgen Concentration with Follicles Number on the Day of Triggering Final Oocyte Maturation in Low Responders According to the Bologna Criteria: A Prospective Cohort Study
summary: Androgens play a critical role in folliculogenesis by stimulating primordial follicle activation, promoting granulosa cell proliferation, and enhancing FSH receptor expression to improve ovarian sensitivity to gonadotropins. While experimental evidence demonstrates that androgens drive early follicular development and recruitment through androgen receptors expressed in granulosa cells, this prospective cohort study in low responders found that basal serum androgen concentrations—including testosterone, DHEAS, 17-OH-progesterone, and androstenedione—showed no significant associations with follicle number at final oocyte maturation triggering or with oocyte retrieval outcomes. Notably, basal androgen levels failed to predict live birth rates or serve as meaningful indicators of follicular development in this population. These findings suggest that despite the well-established biological importance of androgens in follicular development, circulating basal androgen concentrations have limited utility as predictive biomarkers for assisted reproductive outcomes in low responders according to Bologna criteria, potentially reflecting the complex relationship between systemic hormone levels and local ovarian androgen bioavailability.

title: Methimazole-Induced Hypothyroidism Increases the Content of Glycogen and Changes the Expression of LDH, GLUT4, and Aromatase in the Pregnant Uterus of Rabbits
summary:
# Summary

Methimazole-induced hypothyroidism alters critical metabolic and hormonal pathways in the pregnant uterus through dysregulation of glycogen metabolism and steroid hormone signaling. The study demonstrates that hypothyroidism increases uterine glycogen content while simultaneously reducing GLUT4 expression in pregnant rabbits, effects that vary between implantation and inter-implantation sites across different gestational timepoints. Hypothyroidism also upregulates lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) and aromatase expression, linking thyroid dysfunction to increased local estrogen synthesis and lactate-mediated immune regulation in the decidualized endometrium. These metabolic perturbations are clinically significant as hypothyroidism is associated with multiple adverse pregnancy outcomes including infertility, uterine hyperplasia and inflammation, spontaneous abortion, preeclampsia, and preterm birth. The findings suggest that thyroid hormone-mediated regulation of estrogen synthesis and glucose utilization is essential for maintaining normal uterine function during pregnancy, with implications for understanding endometrial dysfunction in thyroid disease and potentially in endometriosis, where aromatase overexpression and inflammatory dysregulation are hallmark features.

title: Influence of tobacco smoking and alcohol drinking on dysmenorrhoea: a cross-sectional analysis of data from the Taiwan Biobank
summary: This cross-sectional study from the Taiwan Biobank identifies tobacco smoking and alcohol consumption as significant modifiable risk factors for dysmenorrhoea, with concurrent use showing particularly strong associations. Tobacco exacerbates menstrual pain through nicotine-induced vasoconstriction that reduces uterine blood flow, thereby increasing prostaglandin production and inflammation, while smoking also disrupts oestrogen metabolism. Alcohol compounds these effects through multiple mechanisms including stimulation of vasopressin (which causes vasoconstriction), depletion of magnesium, enhancement of inflammatory responses, and direct effects on uterine muscle contractions. Protective factors include regular exercise and multiparity, which reduces dysmenorrhoea risk through mechanisms such as decreased prostaglandin levels following full-term pregnancy and potential neuronal changes in the uterus. Secondary dysmenorrhoea associated with conditions such as endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and adenomyosis accounts for significant disease burden through inflammation and obstruction of menstrual flow. The findings underscore that lifestyle modifications including smoking cessation and exercise could substantially reduce dysmenorrhoea symptom severity across different age groups.

title: Associations between endogenous sex hormones and multisite chronic musculoskeletal pain
summary:
# Summary

This study examined associations between endogenous sex hormones and chronic musculoskeletal pain (CMP) across multiple body sites in both sexes. Higher testosterone concentrations and the testosterone-to-sex hormone-binding globulin (T/SHBG) ratio were protective, consistently associated with fewer CMP sites and lower odds of pain across all anatomical locations in both men and women. Sex hormone-binding globulin showed similar protective associations, particularly for neck and shoulder pain. In contrast, estradiol concentrations demonstrated no significant association with CMP prevalence or site-specific pain. These findings suggest sex steroids, particularly testosterone and SHBG, play a role in the pathogenesis of chronic musculoskeletal pain, with higher bioavailable testosterone conferring a protective effect regardless of biological sex.

title: Temporary uterine artery blocking and uterine artery embolization in treating cesarean scar pregnancy
summary: Cesarean scar pregnancy (CSP) is a serious obstetric condition that causes severe postpartum bleeding, infertility, uterine rupture, and potentially life-threatening complications requiring hysterectomy if untreated. This study compared temporary uterine artery blocking (TUAB) with uterine artery embolization (UAE) as minimally invasive treatments for CSP, demonstrating that TUAB significantly outperformed UAE in both clinical and reproductive outcomes. Patients receiving TUAB experienced shorter hospital stays, reduced vaginal bleeding duration, faster menstrual recovery, and critically, lower complication rates (6.67% versus 23.33% with UAE). TUAB preserved ovarian function better than UAE, evidenced by elevated gonadotropins (luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone), larger ovarian diameter, higher anti-Müllerian hormone levels, and increased antral follicle counts—all indicators of superior ovarian reserve. Additionally, TUAB reduced postoperative complications including intrauterine adhesions, premature ovarian failure, amenorrhea, and decreased menstrual volume, while maintaining both uterine and ovarian function to meet patients' fertility needs. These findings establish TUAB as a superior laparoscopic treatment option for CSP, balancing hemorrhage control with preservation of reproductive capacity.

title: Highly purified-hMG versus rFSH in ovarian
summary: This study compared recombinant FSH (rFSH) and highly purified human menopausal gonadotropin (hp-hMG) for ovarian stimulation in assisted reproduction, with relevance to endometriosis management. Recombinant FSH demonstrated superior oocyte outcomes, producing significantly higher numbers of retrieved and metaphase II oocytes while maintaining higher maturity rates, despite generating lower peak estradiol levels. The hp-hMG formulation, which contains FSH, LH, and hCG, induced higher estradiol concentrations through continuous LH activity that stimulates aromatizable androgens in follicles, whereas rFSH (which lacks LH) achieved more efficient follicular recruitment and development. Baseline factors including elevated basal FSH and low antral follicle count negatively predicted oocyte yield, consistent with the diminished oocyte quantity and quality observed in endometriosis and advanced age. The protocol type (GnRH antagonist versus progestin-primed ovarian stimulation) did not significantly affect outcomes, though both prevent ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome. These findings suggest that rFSH-based stimulation may be particularly advantageous for endometriosis patients seeking to optimize oocyte competence while minimizing progesterone elevation that could compromise endometrial receptivity and implantation.

title: Menstrual cycle phase does not influence muscle protein synthesis or whole‐body myofibrillar proteolysis in response to resistance exercise
summary: This study investigated whether menstrual cycle phase influences the muscle protein synthetic and catabolic responses to resistance exercise in women. While resistance exercise robustly stimulated muscle protein synthesis, fluctuations in endogenous ovarian hormones across the menstrual cycle—including estrogen and progesterone—did not significantly affect myofibrillar protein synthesis or whole-body protein breakdown in response to exercise. The findings suggest that despite theoretical mechanistic pathways through which estrogen and progesterone could influence muscular adaptations, natural hormonal variations during the menstrual cycle do not meaningfully modulate the acute anabolic response to resistance training in healthy women. Although the follicular phase is characterized by elevated estrogen and may theoretically favor muscle regeneration, the data provide no empirical support for menstrual cycle phase-dependent differences in muscle protein metabolism following exercise. These results indicate that women can achieve equivalent muscle protein anabolic responses to resistance exercise regardless of menstrual cycle timing, contradicting earlier hypotheses that ovarian hormone fluctuations would create phase-specific advantages for muscle growth and strength development.

title: The proteomic landscape of trophoblasts unravels calcium-dependent syncytialization processes and beta-chorionic gonadotropin (ß-hCG) production
summary:
# Summary

This proteomic study elucidates the calcium-dependent mechanisms governing syncytialization of trophoblasts and their production of pregnancy hormones. The syncytiotrophoblast layer, formed through fusion of cytotrophoblasts mediated by syncytin-1, syncytin-2, and E-cadherin, is essential for the feto-maternal barrier, mineral exchange, and hormone production. Syncytialization upregulates key enzymes involved in sex steroid and glucocorticoid synthesis (CYP19A1, CYP11A1, HSD17β1, and HSD11β2), while dramatically increasing production of β-hCG, progesterone, and estradiol. Calcium plays a critical regulatory role through calcium-selective channels (TRPV6, TRPV2, TRPM7) in controlling ß-hCG secretion and syncytiotrophoblast marker expression; TRPV6 knockout causes skeletal dysplasia and reduced fetal growth, demonstrating calcium's importance for pregnancy maintenance. The cAMP-PKA signaling pathway mediates syncytialization by activating the transcription factor GCM1, which induces syncytin-1 expression and promotes marker protein expression, with forskolin-stimulated cAMP elevation inducing cell fusion and hormone production in BeWo trophoblast cells.

title: Infertility treatment using polysaccharides-based hydrogels: new strategies in tissue engineering and regenerative medicine
summary:
# Summary

This review examines polysaccharide-based hydrogels, including alginate, hyaluronic acid, and chitosan, as innovative biomaterial solutions for treating infertility through tissue engineering and regenerative medicine approaches. These hydrogels enhance fertility restoration by mimicking the extracellular matrix to support cell attachment, proliferation, and differentiation, while enabling controlled release of growth factors and reproductive hormones. In endometrial applications, polysaccharide-based hydrogels facilitate regeneration of damaged endometrial tissue caused by conditions such as Asherman's syndrome and intrauterine adhesions, promote uterine healing, and support embryo implantation in cases where traditional hormone therapies have proven insufficient. The hydrogels also show promise for ovarian tissue engineering and supporting spermatogenesis, addressing both female infertility (associated with menstrual disorders, ovulation dysfunction, polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis, and fallopian tube damage) and male infertility (linked to reduced sperm production, genetic alterations, and endocrine dysfunctions). Advanced technologies including 3D bioprinting, microfluidics, organoids, and bioreactors further enhance reproductive tissue engineering capabilities. Overall, polysaccharide-based hydrogels represent a promising strategy to overcome limitations of conventional fertility treatments and restore reproductive function across multiple etiologies of infertility.

title: Functions of Lysosomes in Mammalian Female Reproductive System
summary: Lysosomes are essential organelles that maintain mammalian female reproductive function through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Lysosomal acidity, maintained by vacuolar H+-ATPase and counter ion channels, creates an optimal environment for over 60 hydrolytic enzymes including cathepsins, lipases, and nucleases that facilitate breakdown of biomolecules and support intracellular trafficking, metabolic signaling, and hormonal responses. In the ovary, lysosomes regulate steroidogenesis by processing cholesterol from LDL endocytosis and control follicle dynamics through cathepsin D-mediated apoptosis during atresia and follicle rupture during ovulation. The hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis hormones—particularly LH and FSH—regulate lysosomal enzyme activity in granulosa cells, while prostaglandin F2α promotes luteal regression through increased lysosomal enzyme release. Defects in lysosomal function, exemplified by TRPML1 mutations causing mucolipidosis type IV, result in infertility, progesterone deficiency, and luteal cell degeneration, demonstrating that proper lysosomal biology is critical for reproductive success. Beyond the ovary, lysosomes support endometrial secretion and apoptosis during menstruation, embryo implantation through endocytosis regulation, and placental and mammary gland functions throughout pregnancy and lactation.

title: Using estrogen and progesterone to treat premenstrual dysphoric disorder, postnatal depression and menopausal depression
summary:
# Summary

Estrogen and progesterone regulate mood through their actions as neuroactive steroids that modulate neurotransmission, particularly by enhancing serotonin signaling and promoting neuroprotection and neurogenesis in the central nervous system. Cyclical fluctuations in these hormones and their metabolite allopregnanolone contribute to the pathophysiology of premenstrual dysphoric disorder, postnatal depression, and menopausal depression by destabilizing GABAergic neurotransmission and disrupting emotional regulation across different reproductive life stages. Multiple therapeutic approaches target these hormonal mechanisms, including selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, hormonal contraceptives (particularly those containing drospirenone or 17β-estradiol), transdermal estrogen, and novel allopregnanolone-based treatments such as brexanolone and sepranolone. Newer agents like ulipristal acetate, dutasteride, and zuranolone offer additional options for managing these mood disorders by either modulating progesterone signaling or providing sustained GABA-A receptor enhancement. The ESC/E(Z) gene network's regulation of hormonal responsiveness suggests that dysregulation within this pathway increases individual sensitivity to gonadal hormone fluctuations, accounting for heterogeneous clinical presentations. These findings underscore the importance of hormone-targeted treatment strategies tailored to the specific reproductive context and individual hormonal sensitivity profiles.

title: Ovarian stimulation by promoting basal follicular growth
summary:
# Summary

Basal follicular growth, the early stage of folliculogenesis from activated quiescent follicles to early antral follicles, is regulated by follicle-stimulating hormone in concert with local communication between oocytes and granulosa cells via gap junctions and autocrine/paracrine factors including growth differentiation factor-9, bone morphogenetic protein-15, and R-spondin2. The intracellular PI3K/AKT and mTOR pathways promote quiescent follicle activation, while the Hippo pathway inhibits this process, controlling the transition from the gonadotropin-independent basal growth phase to the gonadotropin-dependent cyclic recruitment phase. Multiple interventions targeting early antral folliculogenesis—including granulocyte colony-stimulating factor priming, bromocriptine rebound, carbohydrate metabolism intervention, and danazol priming—enhance embryo development and live birth rates in patients with poor ovarian response, though anti-Müllerian hormone concentrations serve as a biomarker for identifying infertility risk. Vascular endothelial growth factor promotes follicular angiogenesis and cell function, while natriuretic peptide type C enhances preantral and antral follicle growth through cGMP-mediated signaling that suppresses phosphodiesterase 3A activity in oocytes.

title: Testosterone mediates life‐history trade‐offs in female mammals
journal: Biological Reviews of the Cambridge Philosophical Society
summary:
# Summary

This review examines how testosterone mediates fundamental trade-offs between reproductive and competitive strategies in female mammals. The SRY gene activates prenatal testosterone production, with levels correlating to anogenital distance (AGD)—a biomarker linked to adulthood serum testosterone, ovarian follicle numbers, and premature ovarian insufficiency risk. Testosterone exhibits a narrow optimal range for female fertility: low levels cause oocyte apoptosis during folliculogenesis while elevated levels arrest follicular development and trigger polycystic ovary-like pathology, with testosterone also regulating menstrual cycle regularity and endometrial decidualization through androgen-mediated inflammation control. High prenatal testosterone exposure promotes competitive and aggressive phenotypes (mate competition, territorial defense, offspring protection) while reducing reproductive success and mating attractiveness, whereas low testosterone females show enhanced sexual receptivity and reproductive output—a pattern consistently observed across mice, rats, and gerbils. These findings demonstrate that testosterone orchestrates a life-history trade-off where females with lower developmental androgen exposure prioritize early reproduction and fertility, while higher testosterone females invest in dominance and competitive ability at the cost of reproductive performance.

title: Educational Case: Abnormal uterine bleeding
summary:
# Summary

Abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) represents a diverse clinical presentation with multiple structural and functional etiologies affecting reproductive health. Structural causes including endometrial polyps, adenomyosis, leiomyoma, and endometrial hyperplasia directly trigger abnormal bleeding and infertility through mechanical interference with the endometrium and intracavitary pathology. Functional causes such as ovulatory dysfunction, coagulopathy, and chronic endometritis disrupt normal hormonal regulation or endometrial integrity, with unopposed estrogen driving excessive endometrial proliferation in the absence of progesterone-mediated suppression. Systemic factors including thyroid dysfunction, hyperprolactinemia, and polycystic ovary syndrome impair ovulatory function through disrupted gonadotropin-releasing hormone signaling and androgen excess, while obesity and nulliparity emerge as significant risk factors across multiple endometrial pathologies. Normal endometrial shedding requires coordinated progesterone withdrawal, hypoxic signaling, and inflammatory cell infiltration, and disruption of these mechanisms underlies both abnormal bleeding and the infertility commonly associated with structural and functional uterine disease.

title: Effect of Treatment on Steroidome in Women with Multiple Sclerosis
summary: Multiple sclerosis manifestations are tightly linked to steroid fluctuations across the menstrual cycle, with disease exacerbations occurring during periods of low sex steroid levels and remission during pregnancy when steroids are elevated. Anti-MS medications alter the steroidome in distinct ways: anti-CD20 monoclonal antibodies (ofatumumab, ocrelizumab) and sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor inhibitors reduce circulating 17-hydroxy-pregnanes and androgen metabolites, while interferon-β1a paradoxically increases testosterone, dihydrotestosterone, and cortisol levels and upregulates steroidogenic enzymes like AKR1D1. These pharmacologically-induced steroid changes appear therapeutically beneficial because they promote neuroprotective steroids like pregnenolone and DHEA while reducing inflammatory mediators, with androgens suppressing B cell differentiation and stimulating anti-inflammatory Th2 responses and IL-10 secretion. The altered hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis in MS patients generates impaired cortisol production that compromises anti-inflammatory signaling, as circulating cortisol normally suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-2, IL-6, TNF, and interferons. Overall, the therapeutic efficacy of anti-MS drugs may partially depend on their capacity to rebalance the steroidome toward neuroprotective and immunomodulatory steroids that counteract chronic CNS inflammation.

title: The link between sex hormones and depression over a woman's lifespan (Review)
summary: Women experience major depressive disorder at approximately twice the rate of men, with this sex difference emerging after puberty and persisting throughout the lifespan due to fluctuations in estrogen and progesterone. The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis controls sex hormone production, with GnRH from the hypothalamus stimulating the anterior pituitary to release LH and FSH, which drive ovarian production of estradiol and progesterone that then feedback to inhibit the HPG axis. Estradiol protects against mood disorders through multiple mechanisms: it activates brain regions involved in emotional regulation such as the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex and amygdala, modulates the serotonergic system by upregulating 5-HT1 receptors and inhibiting monoamine oxidase, and enhances tryptophan hydroxylase activity for serotonin synthesis. Combined oral contraceptives suppress natural hormone production and significantly increase depression risk, particularly when initiated during adolescence, and are also associated with elevated suicidal risk in some populations. Progesterone's metabolite allopregnanolone contributes to premenstrual dysphoric disorder through its fluctuation during the luteal phase, though specific progestins like drospirenone show therapeutic benefit. The FDA-approved brexanolone represents the first hormonal treatment specifically targeting postpartum depression, which results from altered estrogen-mediated gene activation during the postpartum period.

title: An Expanding Large Acrochordon on the Labia Majora Requiring Surgical Intervention
summary:
# Summary

Acrochordons (skin tags) are benign proliferative lesions with multifactorial pathogenesis involving obesity, metabolic disorders, and hormonal dysregulation. The development of these lesions, particularly in women of reproductive age, is driven by elevated progesterone and estrogen levels that promote mesenchymal cell proliferation and fibrosis, while stromal cells express both estrogen and progesterone receptors, facilitating hormone-mediated growth. Mast cell infiltration promotes eosinophil recruitment, which induces excessive fibrosis in the fibroconnective tissue matrix, and human papillomavirus infection may contribute to lesion formation in a subset of cases. Giant acrochordons on the labia majora can cause substantial morbidity including discomfort, itching, ulceration, dyspareunia, and vaginal bleeding, necessitating surgical intervention. Complete surgical excision represents the definitive treatment and effectively resolves both the aesthetic concerns and clinical complications associated with these lesions. The marked reduction in acrochordon incidence in postmenopausal women underscores the critical role of reproductive hormones in their pathogenesis.

title: The role of androgens on experimental pain sensitivity: a systemic review and meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis examined the relationship between androgens and experimental pain sensitivity across multiple studies in both men and women. While androgens play critical roles in sexual differentiation and reproductive health through binding to androgen receptors and are regulated by the hypothalamus and pituitary gland, the overall evidence does not support a consistent effect of testosterone on experimental pain sensitivity in adults. Individual studies reported mixed findings, with some showing that higher testosterone levels correlate with lower pain ratings to heat and electrical stimuli, while numerous other studies found no significant associations across various pain modalities including pressure, ischemic, and mechanical stimuli. Similarly, testosterone did not consistently affect pain inhibitory modulation pathways such as conditioned pain modulation responses. The review also noted that related androgens DHEA and DHEA-S show contradictory effects on pain sensitivity, and that confounding factors such as menstrual cycle phase, contraceptive use, and hormone replacement therapy complicate interpretation of sex hormone effects on pain in women. These findings suggest that despite physiological roles of androgens in sexual differentiation and some reports of pain reduction with androgen treatment, testosterone levels do not reliably predict experimental pain responses.

title: Perspective Exploring Novel Associations of IL-18 Levels as a Mediator of the Causal Links between Major Depression and Reproductive Health
summary: This study reveals a bidirectional relationship between major depressive disorder (MDD) and female infertility mediated by interleukin-18 (IL-18), a key inflammatory cytokine. Using Mendelian randomization analysis, the researchers found that MDD causally increases female infertility risk (OR: 1.26), while paradoxically reducing IL-18 levels (β: −0.40), which itself exhibits a protective effect against infertility (OR: 0.92). IL-18 partially mediates the MDD-infertility pathway through multiple reproductive mechanisms, including regulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, control of decidualization, embryo implantation, and trophoblast invasion. The elevated IL-18 in reproductive tissues, particularly through NLRP3 inflammasome activation (which is elevated in endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome), promotes proinflammatory responses that disrupt the delicate immunological balance required for successful embryo acceptance. The findings suggest that depression and infertility form a vicious cycle, with screening and management of depressive symptoms recommended alongside anti-inflammatory interventions as adjunctive treatments for women seeking fertility care.

title: Exposure to gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist in early pregnancy leads to adverse pregnancy outcomes: a retrospective analysis
summary: This retrospective analysis reveals that exposure to gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists (GnRH-a) during early pregnancy significantly increases the risk of spontaneous abortion and ectopic pregnancy while reducing live birth rates. GnRH-a operates through dual mechanisms: initially, it binds to GnRH receptors and triggers a "flare-up" that transiently elevates FSH and LH, which subsequently undergo suppression to below-basal levels after 5-7 days of continuous administration, ultimately reducing estrogen and progesterone production. This hormonal suppression impairs progesterone secretion through ovarian GnRH receptors, leading to luteolysis and insufficient progesterone for pregnancy maintenance, while high initial levels of estrogen and progesterone disrupt pinopodes formation critical for the implantation window. Additionally, GnRH-a inhibits aromatase and neuroprotein-1 expression, reducing endometrial microvascularization and perfusion, which causes asynchronous embryo-endometrial development and implantation failure. The study demonstrates that although GnRH-a improves endometrial tolerance and embryo implantation through multiple mechanisms including enhanced sub-endometrial blood perfusion and trophoblast invasion when properly timed, inadvertent exposure during early pregnancy creates an adverse hormonal microenvironment that jeopardizes pregnancy viability, emphasizing the critical importance of avoiding GnRH-a administration during the peri-implantation period.

title: Reducing early pregnancy loss with vitamin D3: an analysis of serum 1,25-(OH)D3 modulation and miscarriage risk
journal: Journal of Clinical Biochemistry and Nutrition
summary:
# Summary

Vitamin D3 supplementation significantly reduces early pregnancy loss and threatened miscarriage risk by modulating immune responses and increasing serum 1,25-(OH)D3 levels. The protective mechanism operates primarily through rebalancing helper T cell (Th1/Th2) function, wherein vitamin D3 supplementation increases the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10 while simultaneously reducing pro-inflammatory mediators IL-2 and TNF-α in maternal tissues. Vitamin D3 deficiency is associated with increased early spontaneous and recurrent miscarriage risk, particularly in women with inadequate vitamin D during early pregnancy, possibly due to alterations in the decidual microenvironment. Daily supplementation with 400 IU of vitamin D3 elevated serum HCG concentrations and stabilized hormonal environments without affecting progesterone levels, thereby promoting pregnancy stability. The study demonstrates that serum 1,25-(OH)D3 concentration can serve as a predictive marker for fetal miscarriage risk, making vitamin D status assessment clinically relevant for pregnancy outcome optimization.

title: The Effects of Methylprednisolone and Hyaluronic Acid on the Endometrium in Experimentally Induced Asherman Syndrome Rat Models: A Prospective Laboratory Study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective laboratory study investigated therapeutic approaches to treat experimentally induced Asherman Syndrome in rat models, a condition characterized by intrauterine adhesions that cause infertility, amenorrhea, and menstrual irregularities. Methylprednisolone suppressed inflammation and fibroblast activity while increasing endometrial gland count, uterine wall thickness, lumen diameter, and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) expression. Hyaluronic acid primarily enhanced angiogenesis and wound healing by promoting vascularization, increasing lumen diameter, and elevating VEGF levels. The combined treatment of methylprednisolone and hyaluronic acid demonstrated superior efficacy compared to individual treatments, significantly reducing both inflammation and fibrosis while maximizing gland count and VEGF upregulation to facilitate endometrial regeneration. These findings suggest that the complementary mechanisms of methylprednisolone's anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic effects combined with hyaluronic acid's angiogenic and wound-healing properties represent a promising therapeutic strategy for treating Asherman Syndrome and preventing adhesion recurrence after hysteroscopic surgery.

title: Pregnancy in an SRY-Negative XX Developmental Sex Disorder Pig After Removing an Ovotestis
summary: This case study documents an XX SRY-negative developmental sex disorder pig with an ovotestis that successfully achieved pregnancy after surgical removal of the affected gonad. The pig's infertility resulted from hormonal imbalances created by the ovotestis, which produced insufficient testosterone and anti-Müllerian hormone to fully masculinize external genitalia but enough to suppress normal estrous cycles and inhibit ovarian follicular development. Several genes including SOX9, RSPO1, FOXL2, and SOX3 can mediate XX SRY-negative masculinization in the absence of the testis-determining SRY gene, with testosterone and dihydrotestosterone from Leydig cells driving male reproductive development while AMH from Sertoli cells triggers regression of female ductal structures. Unilateral gonadectomy successfully restored fertility by eliminating the testosterone-mediated inhibition of estrous cycles and embryonic implantation, allowing the animal to achieve normal ovulation and pregnancy. This novel case demonstrates that surgical removal of ectopic gonadal tissue is an effective treatment strategy for restoring reproductive function in individuals with sex determination disorders, with important implications for managing similar developmental abnormalities in both veterinary and potentially human clinical settings.

title: Cardaria draba subspecies Shalepensis exerts in vitro and in silico inhibition of α-glucosidase, TRP1, and DLD-1 proliferation
summary: Cardaria draba subspecies Chalepensis extract, enriched in phenolic compounds and flavonoids, demonstrates potent inhibitory activity against multiple metabolic and oncogenic targets through both in vitro and computational approaches. The extract strongly inhibits α-glucosidase and tyrosinase-related protein 1 (TRP1), with IC50 values indicating significant efficacy, while also reducing proliferation of DLD-1 colon cancer cells at 6.9 µg/mL. These inhibitory mechanisms are mechanistically linked to treating metabolic disorders and pigmentation pathology, as α-glucosidase inhibition addresses diabetes-related hyperglycemia and insulin resistance, while TRP1 inhibition targets hyperpigmentation complications associated with diabetic conditions. The extract additionally inhibits acetylcholinesterase, butyrylcholinesterase, α-amylase, and tyrosinase, providing a multi-targeted phytotherapeutic approach to metabolic dysregulation and chronic inflammation. By blocking TRP1-mediated melanogenesis and improving glucose metabolism, Cardaria draba offers potential therapeutic benefits against the interconnected pathways linking insulin imbalance, vascular dysfunction, and aberrant cell proliferation.

title: Prospective cohort of pre-diagnosis hormone exposure and post-diagnosis sex hormone levels with survival outcomes: Alberta Endometrial Cancer Cohort Study
summary: This prospective cohort study examined how pre-diagnosis hormonal exposures and post-diagnosis circulating sex hormone levels affect survival outcomes in endometrial cancer patients. Pre-diagnosis use of hormonal contraceptives and menopausal hormone therapy showed no significant associations with disease-free or overall survival, while combined estrogen-progestin therapy was not associated with increased cancer risk. However, post-diagnosis circulating estrogen levels emerged as independent predictors of poor prognosis: patients with the highest estrone (>123.86 pg/mL) and estradiol (>12.41 pg/mL) tertiles experienced significantly worse disease-free and overall survival compared to those with lower levels. The study proposes that endogenous estrogens contribute to cancer progression through DNA damage, increased cellular proliferation, and suppressed apoptosis, with particularly strong associations observed in post-menopausal women with type 1 and low-grade tumors. These findings suggest that post-diagnosis circulating hormone levels, rather than prior hormone exposures, are more informative biomarkers for predicting endometrial cancer survival outcomes.

title: A Cross-Sectional Study on Post-COVID-19 Menstrual Abnormalities in Women of Reproductive Age Group at a Tertiary Care Hospital
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study demonstrates that SARS-CoV-2 infection causes significant menstrual abnormalities in women of reproductive age through a mechanism involving viral binding to angiotensin-converting enzyme-2 (ACE-2), which is highly expressed in the ovary, fallopian tubes, and endometrium. Among participants with menstrual changes, COVID-19 infection induced early-onset irregularities characterized by prolonged menstruation, absent cycles, irregular patterns, heavier flow, and increased pain and cramps, with severity correlating to infection intensity. COVID-19 vaccination also triggered menstrual changes but with later onset, while specific vaccines (COVISHIELD and COVAXIN) showed differential effects on menstrual duration and pain. Body mass index and occupational stress emerged as significant modulators, with higher BMI associated with increased menstrual pain through disruption of estrogen and progesterone balance, and employed or student status linked to mood disturbances. Statistically significant differences were confirmed in menstrual frequency, flow volume, and pain intensity before and after COVID-19 infection, establishing menstrual cycle parameters as important health indicators that warrant clinical monitoring in post-COVID patients.

title: Discovery and development of steroidal enzyme inhibitors as anti-cancer drugs: state-of-the-art and future perspectives
journal: Journal of Enzyme Inhibition and Medicinal Chemistry
summary:
# Summary

Aromatase (CYP19A1) catalyzes the conversion of androgens to estrogens and is overexpressed in estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer, making it a validated therapeutic target for aromatase inhibitors including testolactone, formestane, and exemestane. The novel compound oxymestane-D1 represents an advanced steroidal inhibitor that not only blocks aromatase but also antagonizes estrogen receptors while activating androgen receptors, promoting apoptotic caspases to enhance anti-cancer activity. Steroid sulfatase (STS), which cleaves estrogen sulfates in hormone-dependent cancers including breast, prostate, and endometrial malignancies, is targeted by inhibitors such as E2MATE—notably tested in clinical trials for endometriosis pain management. The article comprehensively details steroidogenic enzyme inhibition strategies that disrupt biosynthetic pathways from cholesterol through multiple enzymatic steps involving CYP11A1, CYP17A1, and 5α-reductase, providing multiple intervention points to reduce circulating steroid levels in hormone-dependent malignancies. While steroidal aromatase inhibitors effectively treat ER-positive cancers, they are associated with adverse effects including hot flashes, vaginal dryness, and headaches, highlighting the ongoing need for improved therapeutic agents with enhanced selectivity and reduced side effect profiles.

title: Associations between parity, history of breastfeeding, and T cell profile of ovarian tumors
journal: Cancer epidemiology, biomarkers & prevention : a publication of the American Association for Cancer Research, cosponsored by the American Society of Preventive Oncology
summary:
# Summary

Pregnancy and breastfeeding produce long-term immunological changes that influence ovarian tumor T cell profiles. Breastfeeding is associated with reduced ovarian cancer risk and altered tumor immune microenvironments through sustained elevation of prolactin, which enhances anti-inflammatory cytokine production (interleukin-10) and promotes T cell activation and proliferation. Among parous women, breastfeeding duration correlates with increased infiltration of multiple T cell types including activated helper cells, cytotoxic T cells, and regulatory T cells, though tumor histotype modulates these associations—high-grade serous tumors show enhanced immune infiltration with breastfeeding, while clear cell tumors (which associate with endometriosis) show inverse relationships. Prolactin also reduces gonadotropin levels during breastfeeding, potentially limiting hormonal stimulation of high-grade serous carcinogenesis. These findings suggest breastfeeding-induced immune reprogramming, mediated partly through prolactin-driven shifts toward cytotoxic and activated T cell populations, may explain its protective effect against ovarian cancer despite evidence that regulatory T cells and T cell exhaustion markers typically associate with worse outcomes.

title: Development and functional evaluation of recombinant type III collagen intrauterine implant gel
summary:
# Summary

Recombinant type III collagen (rCol III) temperature-responsive hydrogels represent a novel therapeutic approach for treating intrauterine adhesions and promoting endometrial regeneration following surgical injury. The hydrogel formulation, composed of the biocompatible polymer HP407/HP188, transforms into a semi-solid gel at body temperature while maintaining injectability and forming a three-dimensional mesh structure that sustains collagen release at the injury site. The rCol III hydrogel promotes endometrial recovery through multiple interconnected mechanisms: it stimulates endometrial stem cell migration and angiogenesis, augments endometrial blood supply and gland recovery, while simultaneously suppressing fibrotic pathways by downregulating transforming growth factor beta 1 (TGF-β1), reducing collagen fiber deposition, and inhibiting inflammatory cytokines interleukin-6 and tumor necrosis factor alpha. At optimal concentrations (1 wt%), the collagen-containing gel significantly increased endometrial thickness, vascularized area, and gland number compared to adhesion controls, while reducing abnormal fibrin accumulation and scar formation. These findings demonstrate that rCol III hydrogels effectively address the multiple pathological consequences of endometrial injury by promoting regenerative processes while simultaneously antagonizing the inflammatory and fibrotic responses that perpetuate intrauterine adhesion formation.

title: Therapeutic Effects of Cynodon dactylon (C. dactylon) against
summary:
# Summary

Cynodon dactylon extract demonstrates therapeutic efficacy against polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) through multiple mechanisms of hormonal regulation and ovarian remodeling. The extract reduces elevated testosterone and estrogen levels while enhancing progesterone, thereby correcting the hormonal imbalances characteristic of PCOS and restoring regular estrous cycles. Histologically, C. dactylon treatment reduces theca layer thickness, decreases pathological cystic follicles, and increases secondary follicles and corpus luteum formation, alongside reductions in uterine weight and endometrial gland proliferation. The bioactive compounds within C. dactylon, including ar-tumerone, tumerone, and curlone, exert their effects by binding to key reproductive and metabolic receptors including anti-Müllerian hormone receptor-2, estrogen receptors, and Toll-like receptor 4. These molecular interactions counteract the pathogenic cascade in PCOS wherein insulin resistance drives hyperinsulinemia and androgen excess, which suppresses the GnRH pulse generator's sensitivity to progesterone regulation. The combined reduction in body weight, restoration of follicular development, and hormonal normalization achieved by C. dactylon extract suggest a promising botanical therapeutic comparable to metformin for PCOS management.

title: Kunxinning granules alleviate perimenopausal syndrome by supplementing estrogen deficiency
summary: Kunxinning Granules (KXN), a traditional herbal formulation, effectively treats perimenopausal syndrome by restoring estrogen deficiency through enhanced steroid hormone biosynthesis. Ovarian function decline during perimenopause reduces circulating estrogen levels, precipitating multiple symptoms including hot flashes, anxiety, depression, and osteoporosis. KXN reverses these pathological changes by increasing CYP19A1 expression and enzymatic activity across adrenal, uterine, and hypothalamic tissues, thereby promoting the conversion of androgens to estradiol and estrone. The therapeutic effects are mediated by key metabolites—astragaloside IV, icariin, and baohuoside I—which enhance aromatase activity and upregulate other steroid biosynthetic enzymes including HSD3B, CYP21A2, StAR, and HSD11B2.
