In an ovariectomized rat model, KXN treatment significantly restored serum estradiol and progesterone levels while suppressing elevated follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone, simultaneously improving uterine atrophy and endometrial thickness. These findings demonstrate that KXN's clinical efficacy in alleviating perimenopausal syndrome operates through direct hormonal rebalancing via the steroid hormone biosynthesis pathway.

title: Ovarian Endometriosis Accelerates Premature Ovarian Failure and Contributes to Osteoporosis and Cognitive Decline in Aging Mice
summary: Ovarian endometriosis (OEM) accelerates premature ovarian failure in aging mice through abnormal activation of primordial follicles, upregulation of mTOR and Hippo signaling pathways, and induction of ovarian fibrosis that depletes the ovarian reserve. The condition reduces anti-Müllerian hormone and estradiol while increasing follicle-stimulating hormone, disrupting estrous cycles and causing irregular menstrual patterns characteristic of early perimenopause. Beyond reproductive effects, OEM triggers systemic complications including elevated serum immunoglobulin G levels that contribute to osteoporosis through disrupted bone remodeling and cognitive decline, manifesting as anxiety and working memory impairment in aging mice. Mechanistically, OEM induces senescence-associated secretory phenotype markers, chromosomal instability, and heightened inflammatory responses that damage the ovarian microenvironment through mechanical stress and tissue injury. These findings suggest that OEM not only compromises fertility but significantly accelerates systemic aging phenotypes, with elevated FSH levels potentially increasing vulnerability to age-related conditions such as Alzheimer's disease in perimenopausal women.

title: Effects of Vitamin D3 Treatment on Polycystic Ovary Symptoms: A Prospective Double-Blind Two-Phase Randomized Controlled Clinical Trial
summary: This prospective randomized controlled trial demonstrates that vitamin D3 supplementation effectively improves polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) symptoms through multiple hormonal and metabolic mechanisms. Vitamin D deficiency is prevalent in PCOS patients and associates with metabolic disorders, ovulatory dysfunction, and androgen excess; vitamin D acts through its receptor (VDR) and 1α-hydroxylase to regulate sex hormone balance and affect ovarian, uterine, pituitary, and hypothalamic function. Vitamin D3 treatment (30,000 IU/week) significantly increased ovulation rates and serum 25(OH)D levels while reducing testosterone, cycle length, and cycle irregularity—effects that were particularly pronounced in hyperandrogenic patients with elevated LH/FSH ratios. Vitamin D supplementation improved ovarian morphology and menstrual cycle regularity in over half of treated patients and increased progesterone levels in those with high LH/FSH ratios, whereas placebo showed no significant changes in these parameters. The clinical benefits were subtype-dependent: patients with elevated androstenedione and high LH/FSH ratios showed the most dramatic improvements in testosterone reduction and ovulation rate, while vitamin D treatment increased sex hormone-binding globulin in this subgroup. These findings suggest that vitamin D3 supplementation could serve as either a standalone or adjunctive therapy for PCOS management, particularly in hormone-dysregulated phenotypes.

title: Amelioration of female menopausal syndrome by intravenous administration of autologous menstrual blood-derived stem cells
summary: Menopausal syndrome encompasses multiple symptom clusters including vasomotor disturbances, neuropsychological symptoms, sleep disturbances, urogenital dysfunction, and musculoskeletal complaints, all stemming from hormonal fluctuations during ovarian aging. While traditional hormone replacement therapy effectively manages these symptoms, its association with increased breast and reproductive cancer risk has spurred investigation of alternative therapeutic approaches. This study demonstrates that intravenous administration of autologous menstrual blood-derived stem cells (MenSCs) significantly ameliorates menopausal symptoms through anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory mechanisms, with higher cell doses (1 × 10⁸ cells) proving more effective than lower doses. The therapeutic effects correlate with restoration of hormonal balance, evidenced by increased estradiol levels and decreased follicle-stimulating hormone levels, particularly in postmenopausal patients. MenSCs accomplish this by secreting growth factors and cytokines that promote tissue regeneration and suppress inflammation, effectively restoring ovarian function without serious adverse events. These findings suggest MenSCs treatment offers a novel regenerative medicine approach to menopausal syndrome that avoids the cancer risks associated with conventional hormone replacement therapy.

title: Examining the effect of Kuntai capsule combined with HRT in the treatment of premature ovarian insufficiency: A systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis examines the therapeutic efficacy of Kuntai capsule, a Chinese herbal medicine, combined with hormone replacement therapy (HRT) for treating premature ovarian insufficiency (POI), a condition characterized by menstrual abnormalities, elevated follicle-stimulating hormone, and perimenopausal symptoms in women under 40 years old. The combined treatment significantly improved ovarian reserve function through multiple mechanisms, including increased antral follicle counts and anti-Müllerian hormone levels while reducing circulating FSH and luteinizing hormone, alongside elevated estradiol levels. At the molecular level, Kuntai capsule activates the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway to inhibit granulosa cell apoptosis and enhance sex hormone secretion, while the combination improved endometrial thickness, ovarian blood perfusion, ovulation rates, and pregnancy outcomes. Clinical benefits included alleviation of perimenopausal symptoms and menstrual disorders, with the combined approach reducing adverse effects associated with HRT alone, though untreated POI carries significant long-term health risks including osteoporosis, cardiovascular disease, and psychological complications.

title: Non-human primate: the new frontier model of female reproductive engineering
summary:
# Summary

Non-human primates (NHPs), particularly rhesus macaques, cynomolgus monkeys, common marmosets, and baboons, serve as superior preclinical models for reproductive engineering due to their biological similarity to humans, including comparable menstrual cycles (28–32 days), hormonal regulation involving estrogen, progesterone, and luteinizing hormone, and susceptibility to human-specific reproductive disorders such as endometriosis and preeclampsia. These models have been instrumental in advancing assisted reproductive technologies, including in vitro fertilization, intracytoplasmic sperm injection, and embryo transfer, as well as fertility preservation strategies such as ovarian tissue cryopreservation and transplantation, which restore fertility in individuals with age-related decline or chemotherapy-induced infertility. NHP research has also pioneered stem cell applications, enabling the derivation and reprogramming of embryonic stem cells and induced pluripotent stem cells into germline-like cells for investigating spermatogenesis and oogenesis, with potential therapeutic applications for gonadal dysgenesis. Additionally, tissue engineering approaches using artificial ovarian and uterine constructs are being developed in NHP models to treat conditions such as premature ovarian insufficiency, Asherman syndrome, and congenital uterine malformations. The maternal-fetal endocrine interactions studied in marmosets have revealed how maternal progesterone and cortisol influence fetal development and placental function, further validating NHPs as essential translational models for understanding hormonal regulation in female reproductive health.

title: A neural circuit for sex-dependent conditioned pain hypersensitivity in mice
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies a sex-dependent neural circuit underlying contextual pain hypersensitivity mediated by testosterone and androgen receptor signaling. In male mice, elevated free-testosterone activates androgen receptors and drives hyperactivity of glutamatergic neurons in the medial preoptic area (GlumPOA), which project to GABAergic neurons in the ventrolateral periaqueductal gray to enhance pain hypersensitivity following pain conditioning. Optogenetic manipulation directly demonstrates that GlumPOA neuronal activity is both necessary and sufficient for this pain phenotype, while GABAergic inhibition of GlumPOA neurons reverses hypersensitivity. Critically, the authors show testosterone rather than estrogen is required for this male-specific response, and that episodic memory encoded in the dorsal hippocampus CA1 region triggers testosterone-driven GlumPOA hyperactivity. Castration surgery abolishes the pain hypersensitivity and elevated free-testosterone levels, whereas testosterone propionate administration restores the phenotype even in females, establishing testosterone as the primary hormonal driver of this pain circuit.

title: Analysis of systemic effects of dioxin on human health through template-and-anchor modeling
summary:
# Summary

Dioxins, particularly 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD), are persistent environmental pollutants that disrupt female reproductive function through multiple mechanisms centered on hormonal dysregulation. The aryl hydrocarbon receptor mediates dioxin's effects on gene expression, including alterations in steroidogenesis, cholesterol biosynthesis, and lipoprotein transport pathways. Dioxin exposure suppresses the expression and activity of key steroidogenic enzymes (CYP11A1, CYP17A1, CYP19A1, and HSD17B1) while upregulating HSD3B1, resulting in reduced production of 17β-estradiol and progesterone in ovarian follicles and granulosa cells. These hormonal reductions disrupt the menstrual cycle, delay ovulation, and increase infertility risk, with epidemiological evidence from dioxin-exposed populations showing associations with prolonged menstrual cycles and extended time-to-pregnancy. Critically, dioxin exposure directly causes endometriosis alongside other reproductive toxicities including reduced fecundity, altered sex hormone dynamics, and developmental dysfunction, establishing dioxin as a significant environmental risk factor for endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Luteal phase oral dexamethasone administration alters the endometrial steroid milieu
summary: This study demonstrates that luteal phase dexamethasone administration effectively reverses endometrial glucocorticoid deficiency and reduces heavy menstrual bleeding by modifying the endometrial steroid milieu. Oral dexamethasone penetrates the endometrium and suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, leading to marked reductions in circulating cortisol, cortisone, 11-deoxycortisol, and androgens (androstenedione and testosterone). The mechanism appears to involve glucocorticoid receptor activation in endometrial immune cells, which modulates prostaglandin production and inflammatory responses that drive menstrual blood loss. Notably, dexamethasone did not alter estrogen or progesterone levels, suggesting its therapeutic effect is independent of classical progesterone signaling and instead targets the glucocorticoid-regulated inflammatory pathway. The findings support a novel therapeutic approach to heavy menstrual bleeding that addresses the underlying glucocorticoid deficiency previously associated with this condition, offering a mechanistically distinct alternative to existing hormonal treatments.

title: Treatment of unacceptable bleeding in long-term users of 52-mg levonorgestrel intrauterine device: a prospective observational study
summary: The 52-mg levonorgestrel intrauterine device (LNG-IUD) treats heavy menstrual bleeding through a well-characterized hormonal mechanism: levonorgestrel downregulates estrogen receptors in endometrial stromal cells, which diminishes endometrial proliferation and induces endometrial atrophy. However, this atrophy renders basal blood vessels vulnerable, paradoxically causing irregular vaginal bleeding in some long-term users. To address this complication, this prospective observational study evaluated oral estradiol supplementation, which promotes endometrial regeneration and significantly reduced bleeding days from a mean of 22.5 to 12.8 days per month, substantially improving patient satisfaction with the device. Although estradiol treatment proved effective, it produced notable side effects including vaginal discharge (60%), breast tenderness (28.6%), and mood swings (11.4%), along with less common complaints of headache, fatigue, gastrointestinal symptoms, and weight gain or edema. These findings support estradiol supplementation as a viable clinical strategy for managing unacceptable bleeding in LNG-IUD users while acknowledging the need to balance therapeutic benefits against potential adverse effects.

title: Effect of letrozole cotreatment in progestin-primed ovarian stimulation on IVF/ICSI outcomes in POSEIDON group 3 and 4 poor responders: a retrospective cohort study
summary: This study evaluated letrozole cotreatment combined with progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS) in poor ovarian responders, demonstrating that letrozole enhances ovarian response through multiple mechanisms including increased endogenous gonadotropin secretion and promotion of follicular development. The PPOS + letrozole protocol significantly increased oocyte number, MII oocyte maturation, and two-pronuclear embryos while reducing cycle cancellation rates compared to PPOS alone, with lower estradiol but higher luteinizing hormone levels on trigger day. Notably, the protocol benefited advanced-aged patients in the POSEIDON group 4 category, where it resulted in higher oocyte retrieval and maturation rates, though no advantage was observed in POSEIDON group 3 patients. The underlying mechanism involves androgens—elevated by letrozole through aromatase inhibition—which increase granulosa cell proliferation, reduce follicular atresia, and enhance antral follicle sensitivity to gonadotropins. The number of available embryos generated was positively associated with cumulative live birth rates, suggesting that letrozole cotreatment in PPOS protocols offers a practical strategy for improving IVF outcomes in carefully selected poor responder populations.

title: Treatment With a Nonaromatizable Androgen for Transgender Man With a Hormone-sensitive Ovarian Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a 51-year-old transgender man with estrogen receptor and progesterone receptor-positive endometrioid ovarian cancer who required an alternative gender-affirming hormone therapy strategy. Standard testosterone treatment was contraindicated because aromatization converts testosterone to estradiol, which stimulates hormone-sensitive ovarian cancer growth. The patient was switched to nandrolone decanoate, a nonaromatizable androgen that cannot be converted to estradiol, while also receiving medroxyprogesterone acetate to suppress residual serum estradiol levels through endocrine therapy. Nandrolone successfully maintained the patient's androgen effects, bone and muscle health, and quality of life while avoiding cancer stimulation, demonstrating that this agent represents a viable alternative masculinizing hormone for transgender men with hormone-sensitive malignancies. The case highlights the unique challenge of balancing gender-affirming care with cancer treatment in patients requiring both androgen replacement and estrogen suppression, and provides evidence that nandrolone may have broader clinical utility beyond its established uses in catabolic conditions.

title: Analysis of influencing factors on the rate of high-quality embryos induced by rectangular plan during in vitro fertilization
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated factors influencing high-quality embryo rates during in vitro fertilization using a rectangular stimulation protocol. The research identified elevated progesterone levels on the day of human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) injection as a significant independent risk factor that substantially reduces both oocyte quality and high-quality embryo rates, with prolonged ovarian stimulation time and high-dose gonadotropin administration further contributing to elevated progesterone accumulation. The mechanism involves follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone synergistically stimulating granulosa cells to increase progesterone biosynthesis during the late follicular phase. Long protocol stimulation improved fresh embryo transfer outcomes while reducing the need for freeze-all cycles through better synchronization. Female age, estradiol levels, and the number of mature follicles (≥14 mm diameter) also significantly associated with embryo quality outcomes, whereas male factors including sperm parameters, patient characteristics such as BMI and infertility duration, and baseline hormonal parameters showed no significant correlation with embryo quality.

title: Value analysis of serum β -HCG, AMH, and P/E2 for predicting freeze-thaw embryo transfer outcomes in patients with thin endometrium
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated serum biomarkers for predicting pregnancy outcomes in frozen-embryo transfer (FET) cycles, particularly in patients with thin endometrium. The analysis examined four key hormones—β-HCG, anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH), progesterone (P), and estradiol (E2)—finding that β-HCG, AMH, and E2 were significantly elevated in pregnant compared to non-pregnant patients, while progesterone and the P/E2 ratio were lower in successful pregnancies. Multiple factors independently associated with improved FET pregnancy rates included older maternal age (≥35 years), primary infertility status, and critically, endometrial characteristics, as endometrial thickness below 8 mm substantially reduced pregnancy rates, implantation rates, and live birth rates while increasing miscarriage risk. The study revealed that thin endometrium exhibits reduced vascular endothelial growth factor expression and impaired blood flow, mechanisms that compromise embryo implantation. Notably, maintaining an optimal P/E2 ratio emerged as important for endometrial receptivity and tolerance, as hormonal imbalances—such as elevated estrogen impairing corpus luteum function—can desynchronize endometrial maturation from embryonic development and weaken progesterone responsiveness.

title: The Role of Oxytocin Neurons in the Paraventricular Nucleus in Chronic-Sleep-Deprivation-Mediated Abnormal Cardiovascular Responses
summary:
# Summary

Chronic sleep deprivation induces paradoxical cardiovascular dysfunction characterized by decreased heart rate and increased blood pressure, accompanied by elevated cardiac autophagy and apoptosis, along with extensive blood transcriptomic alterations. The oxytocin neuronal system in the paraventricular nucleus (PVNOXT) plays a critical regulatory role in these responses, as optogenetic activation of PVNOXT causes heart rate suppression and potentially fatal cardiac arrest in sleep-deprived mice. Sleep deprivation suppresses PVNOXT postsynaptic activity while simultaneously upregulating 3-mercaptopyruvate sulfurtransferase (mPST), an enzyme crucial for cardiovascular homeostasis and hydrogen sulfide production. Long-term low-frequency stimulation of PVNOXT effectively reverses sleep deprivation-induced cardiovascular dysfunction by decreasing neuronal excitability and oxytocin release, which subsequently triggers feedback inhibition of mPST expression. These findings reveal a novel neuroendocrine mechanism linking sleep disruption to cardiovascular pathology through oxytocin–hydrogen sulfide interactions in the hypothalamus, with potential therapeutic implications for sleep-disorder-related cardiovascular disease risk.

title: Sex Differences in the Regulation of Interleukins in Chronic Pain: A Widely Recognized but Difficult-to-Tackle Factor
summary:
# Summary

Sex differences profoundly influence chronic pain susceptibility through sex hormone regulation of interleukin (IL)-mediated neuroinflammation. Women exhibit lower pain thresholds and higher incidence of chronic pain conditions compared to men, driven partly by differential IL expression and activation between sexes. Interleukins produced by neurons, glia, and immune cells trigger abnormal glial activation, immune cell infiltration, and neural activation, collectively contributing to neuroinflammation that alters nerve terminal excitability and pain transduction. Sex hormones—particularly estrogen and progesterone—regulate IL production and signaling through direct receptor interactions, with estrogen generally exerting analgesic effects through estrogen receptors (ERα and ERβ) and the non-classical receptor GPR30, while progesterone's effects are dose-dependent. Specific IL axes such as IL-23/IL-17A regulate female-specific mechanical pain, highlighting sex-specific immunological pathways in chronic pain pathophysiology. These findings underscore that effective chronic pain management requires consideration of sex-specific mechanisms involving the interplay between gonadal steroid hormones and IL-regulated neuroimmune responses.

title: The Temperature of the First Cleavage Impacts Preimplantation Development and Newborn Viability
summary:
# Summary

Incubation temperature during early embryonic development significantly impacts preimplantation progress and long-term viability through effects on DNA methylation patterns and cell division dynamics. Suboptimal temperatures of 35°C reduced embryo survival rates to 60% and increased DNA methylation variability in blastomeres, while 37°C maintained optimal 80% survival with more stable methylation patterns; in contrast, 39°C maintained similar in vitro survival but led to elevated pregnancy failure and reduced postnatal viability. Temperature stress altered DNA methylation levels—with cold stress increasing methylation and heat stress activating de novo methylation—disrupting the normal DNA demethylation required for embryonic genome activation and causing developmental arrest through suppression of zygotic transcription. Mechanistically, temperature imbalances impaired cellular metabolism and induced heterogeneous methylation distribution between blastomeres, manifesting as increased cleavage duration, reduced blastomere size, and elevated intraembryonic variability that persisted even after temperature normalization. These findings link endometriosis-associated temperature gradient alterations in the reproductive tract to potential embryo developmental dysfunction, suggesting that maintaining physiological temperature conditions is critical for proper epigenetic reprogramming and embryo developmental competence.

title: Validating At-Home Urinary Hormone Measurements in Postpartum and Perimenopause Fertility Transitions
summary: This study validated at-home urinary hormone monitoring devices (Mira and ClearBlue Fertility Monitor) for tracking estrone-3-glucuronide (E13G) and luteinizing hormone (LH) during postpartum and perimenopause transitions. The Mira monitor's quantitative measurements showed high correlation with the ClearBlue device's qualitative readings, enabling more detailed detection of dynamic hormone changes than standard ovulation prediction tests. Both postpartum and perimenopausal women exhibited significantly lower E13G levels compared to those with regular cycles, with postpartum amenorrhea characterized by ovarian quiescence and multiple non-ovulatory LH surges, while perimenopause displayed aberrant LH patterns potentially related to reduced ovarian sensitivity. The study identified that aberrant hormone patterns during these transitions—including transient estrogen rises and luteal out-of-phase follicular events—can be detected through quantitative monitoring, providing advantages for identifying true ovulation. These findings suggest that quantitative urine hormone assessment could have future clinical applications for menstrual cycle tracking and perimenopause prediction, though further validation is needed in women with irregular hormone patterns.

title: Lesion of the ventral or dorsal hippocampus in the rat delays puberty, follicular growth and secretion of sex steroid hormones
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that the hippocampus plays a critical role in regulating reproduction through connections with the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. Lesioning either the ventral or dorsal hippocampus in prepubertal rats caused delayed puberty, marked by postponed vaginal opening and first estrus, along with decreased numbers of healthy ovarian follicles and increased follicular atresia. These anatomical changes were accompanied by significant reductions in circulating estradiol and progesterone, decreased serum FSH and LH concentrations, and paradoxically elevated testosterone levels. The lesions also impaired hippocampal structural integrity, reducing dendritic spine density in CA1 and CA3 regions, and caused cognitive deficits in both short- and long-term memory. These findings reveal that the hippocampus exerts a stimulatory influence on gonadal function that is independent of classical hypothalamic control, establishing the hippocampus as a previously underappreciated neural regulator of reproductive development and hormone secretion.

title: Molecular and biological markers for assessing endometrial receptivity in infertile women: A narrative review
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial receptivity, essential for successful embryo implantation, is regulated through multiple molecular pathways involving hormonal signaling and structural markers. Key molecular players include integrin αvβ3 and osteopontin, whose calcium-dependent interaction facilitates embryo implantation, along with HOXA10, a homeobox gene that regulates endometrial cell proliferation and differentiation and controls integrin αvβ3 expression through estrogen and progesterone signaling. The cytokine LIF, operating through the gp130/STAT3 pathway, is critical for embryo implantation and pregnancy development, with deficiency preventing implantation despite normal ovulation and fertilization. Impaired endometrial receptivity—characterized by decreased integrin αvβ3 or HOXA10 expression in conditions like polycystic ovary syndrome, or reduced LIF levels in repeated implantation failure—can be ameliorated by treatments including metformin, aspirin, HSP65, and tacrolimus. Advanced diagnostic approaches such as endometrial receptivity testing (ERT) can identify endometrial window displacement in approximately 70% of patients with repeated implantation failure and enable personalized transfer protocols that significantly improve pregnancy outcomes, while endometrial microbiota composition emerges as another contributing factor to implantation success and pregnancy maintenance.

title: The interplay of obesity, microbiome dynamics, and innovative anti-obesity strategies in the context of endometrial cancer progression and therapeutic approaches
journal: Biochimica et biophysica acta. Reviews on cancer
summary:
# Summary

Obesity represents the strongest modifiable risk factor for endometrial cancer, primarily through metabolic and inflammatory mechanisms. Obesity increases estrogen bioavailability through three pathways: adipose tissue produces estrogen directly, hyperinsulinemia suppresses sex hormone-binding globulin synthesis and promotes androgen production, and peripheral aromatization of androgens to estrogen is enhanced in excess adipose tissue. Additionally, obesity-induced hyperinsulinemia and unopposed estrogen activate the PI3K/AKT/mTOR proliferation pathway while promoting IGF1 expression, collectively driving endometrial cell proliferation and blocking apoptosis. A pro-inflammatory state in obesity amplifies these effects, as elevated cytokines including leptin, interleukin-6, and TNF-α promote cell proliferation and invasion while suppressing apoptosis through NF-κB activation, while also upregulating aromatase to further increase local estrogen production. Notably, weight loss and bariatric surgery substantially reduce endometrial cancer risk by reversing these metabolic derangements, and gut dysbiosis associated with obesity contributes to perpetuating these pathogenic processes.

title: Efficacy of a Neuroimmune Therapy Including Pineal Methoxyindoles, Angiotensin 1-7, and Endocannabinoids in Cancer, Autoimmune, and Neurodegenerative Diseases
summary: This study demonstrates that a comprehensive neuroimmune therapy combining pineal methoxyindoles (melatonin and related compounds), angiotensin 1-7, and endocannabinoids effectively addresses multiple disease pathologies through coordinated immune and inflammatory modulation. Melatonin and pineal-derived compounds suppress tumor growth and promote apoptosis while improving sleep and mood through antidepressant mechanisms, while simultaneously stimulating ACE2 expression. Angiotensin 1-7, produced downstream of ACE2 activation, counteracts the pro-inflammatory and tumor-promoting effects of angiotensin II by inhibiting IL-17 secretion and promoting anti-tumoral IL-2 production, thereby regulating both immune responses and cell proliferation. The endocannabinoid system provides additional anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects through reciprocal interactions with the pineal gland. In clinical application, this integrated neuroimmune regimen halted cancer progression in 68% of patients while improving mood, sleep, and reducing anxiety, pain, and fatigue, and demonstrated particular efficacy in autoimmune conditions including multiple sclerosis, Hashimoto's thyroiditis, hypertension, heart failure, and type 2 diabetes. The therapeutic success reflects a coordinated restoration of neuroendocrine-immune balance that simultaneously suppresses pathogenic inflammation and promotes organ protection across diverse disease states.

title: Use of Hormone Replacement Therapy in Special Circumstances
summary:
# Summary

Hormone replacement therapy (HRT) effectively manages postmenopausal symptoms, but its use requires careful consideration of disease-specific risks and benefits. Progestins are essential components of HRT regimens as they prevent endometrial hyperplasia and malignancy, though combined estrogen-progestin therapy carries elevated breast cancer risk compared to estrogen monotherapy, particularly with synthetic progestins. Endometriosis presents a unique clinical challenge as an estrogen-dependent condition where estrogen-only HRT risks disease reactivation and malignant transformation of residual tissue, whereas continuous combined therapy with progestins mitigates these risks. The timing of HRT initiation significantly influences cardiovascular and cognitive outcomes: therapy commenced within ten years of menopause or before age 60 reduces coronary artery disease incidence and atherosclerosis progression, while initiation in older women increases dementia and Alzheimer disease risk. In Turner syndrome and primary ovarian insufficiency, HRT provides critical benefits including puberty induction, bone mineral density improvement, and endothelial function restoration, with micronized progesterone emerging as the safest progestin option across various clinical contexts.

title: Effects of integrated exercise approach on total testosterone levels in eumenorrheic women: a randomized controlled trial
summary: This randomized controlled trial demonstrates that integrated exercise produces transient increases in total testosterone levels in eumenorrheic women, with peak elevation occurring 15 minutes post-exercise followed by decline within 24 hours. The testosterone response varies across the menstrual cycle, reaching maximum levels during the mid-cycle phase when exercise stimulus is applied. In women, testosterone is synthesized by both the ovaries and adrenal glands and plays important physiological roles including contributions to muscle mass, strength, cognitive function, libido, energy levels, and musculoskeletal health through mechanisms involving protein synthesis, satellite cell proliferation, and muscle fiber hypertrophy. The integrated exercise protocol combined resistance training (squats, tandem walks, arm swings with loads, crunches) with flexibility and mobility work (bending, roll-ups), demonstrating that structured multi-modal exercise can effectively modulate testosterone levels. These findings suggest that while acute exercise bouts transiently elevate testosterone in women, chronic exercise training may produce more sustained resting testosterone elevations, with the magnitude of response influenced by exercise type, intensity, and menstrual cycle timing.

title: Healthcare provider communication and current contraceptive use among transgender men and gender-diverse people: results from an online, cross-sectional survey in the United States
summary: This cross-sectional study demonstrates that active healthcare provider communication significantly influences contraceptive use and method selection among transgender men and gender-diverse people in the United States. Provider-patient discussions about contraception were strongly associated with current contraceptive use overall, with the strongest association observed for long-acting reversible contraception (LARC) adoption. Patient-initiated conversations about contraception were particularly effective, correlating with increased contraceptive use for both pregnancy prevention and menstrual symptom management. Conversely, inadequate counseling and poor provider communication led to misaligned method selection or contraceptive avoidance, while deterrence of patient self-advocacy prevented informed decision-making about contraceptive needs. Patient-centered, shared decision-making approaches that respect gender identity and incorporate individual preferences improved both patient outcomes and long-term contraceptive adherence. These findings underscore the critical role of respectful, knowledgeable provider communication in enabling transgender men and gender-diverse individuals to achieve their reproductive health goals, whether through hormonal suppression via testosterone or other contraceptive strategies.

title: Hyperthyroidism Associated with Gestational Trophoblastic Neoplasia: Systematic Literature Review and Pathways Analysis
summary: Gestational trophoblastic neoplasia, particularly choriocarcinoma and molar pregnancies, frequently causes hyperthyroidism through multiple interconnected mechanisms involving human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) and estrogen signaling. hCG, produced by syncytiotrophoblastic cells, directly stimulates thyroid function and demonstrates cross-reactivity with thyroid-stimulating hormone due to shared α-subunits, with basic isoforms of hCG facilitating enhanced thyrotropic activity. Additionally, trophoblast cells convert dehydroisoandrosterone sulfate to estradiol, which upregulates thyroxine-binding globulin; this increased binding globulin reduces free thyroid hormone levels, triggering compensatory TSH elevation and thyroid stimulation. Among gestational trophoblastic disease cases analyzed, hyperthyroidism occurred in 20–64% of molar pregnancies and was particularly prominent in choriocarcinoma patients, with lung metastases being the most common site of dissemination. Treatment of the underlying neoplasia with chemotherapy or surgical resection typically results in normalization of thyroid function within 2–3 weeks, demonstrating the direct causative relationship between trophoblastic disease and thyroid hormone dysregulation.

title: Estrogen deficiency in the menopause and the role of hormone therapy: integrating the findings of basic science research with clinical trials
summary:
# Summary

This article integrates basic science and clinical findings on estrogen's role during menopause and the mechanisms underlying hormone therapy efficacy. The study details how ovarian failure during perimenopause causes a dramatic reduction in estradiol production, leading to the cessation of menstrual cycles and triggering vasomotor symptoms like hot flashes, which are effectively treated by estrogen replacement. Estrogen acts through multiple receptor pathways—estrogen receptor alpha (ERα) promotes cell proliferation in reproductive tissues and is expressed predominantly in the uterus, liver, and mammary glands, while estrogen receptor beta (ERβ) provides protective effects including cardiovascular and neuroprotection with antiproliferative properties. The article highlights a critical endometrial pathology connection: unopposed estrogen stimulates excessive endometrial proliferation and hyperplasia, a risk amplified by obesity-related adipose tissue estrogen production, but this hyperplasia is prevented by progesterone and typically regresses with menopause when estrogen levels decline. Notably, abnormal expression of estrogen receptors (particularly ERα and GPER1) is associated with endometrial pathologies including endometriosis, suggesting dysregulated estrogen signaling contributes to disease development independent of circulating hormone levels.

title: Breast cancer incidence after hormonal treatment for infertility
summary:
# Summary: Breast Cancer Incidence After Hormonal Treatment for Infertility

Hormonal fertility medications stimulate ovarian activity, leading to elevated levels of progesterone and estrogen, which promote breast tissue proliferation and enhance cell division that could theoretically increase breast cancer risk. However, meta-analytic evidence demonstrates no significant increased breast cancer risk associated with hormonal infertility treatments overall, including clomiphene citrate, controlled ovarian stimulation, and multiple IVF cycles. The biological mechanism underlying potential risk involves prolonged exposure to high hormone levels promoting abnormal cell division in breast tissue, yet this theoretical pathway does not consistently translate to increased cancer incidence in clinical practice. Notably, while some studies report higher breast cancer risk after assisted reproduction, pooled analyses show risk ratios at or below unity, with one analysis even suggesting a protective effect (HR=0.91). Confounding factors such as nulliparity and infertility itself—independent risk factors for breast cancer—complicate interpretation of treatment effects. The evidence suggests that genetic factors including BRCA mutations and family history do not substantially modify breast cancer risk from fertility treatments, supporting the overall safety profile of these interventions for infertile women.

title: Photoperiod-driven testicular DNA methylation in gonadotropin and sex steroid receptor promoters in Siberian hamsters
journal: Journal of Comparative Physiology. A, Neuroethology, Sensory, Neural, and Behavioral Physiology
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that photoperiod—the seasonal change in day length—regulates reproductive function in Siberian hamsters through epigenetic modifications of gene promoters controlling the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. Short photoperiod conditions induce DNA methylation in promoter regions of critical reproductive genes including androgen receptor (Ar), estrogen receptors (Esr1 and Esr2), kisspeptin1 receptor (Kiss1r), and follicle-stimulating hormone receptor (Fshr), leading to increased expression of DNA methyltransferases (Dnmt3a and Dnmt3b) and overall testicular DNA methylation. These epigenetic changes suppress the signaling cascade initiated by kisspeptin receptor stimulation of gonadotropin-releasing hormone, ultimately reducing follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone secretion, testosterone production, and spermatogenesis, resulting in gonadal regression and decreased testicular mass characteristic of winter-like reproductive involution. Conversely, long photoperiod conditions promote gametogenesis and steroidogenesis while inducing DNA methylation in alternative gene promoters (Bmal1, Pgr, and Tshr) necessary for maintaining active reproductive function. These findings establish DNA methylation as a critical epigenetic mechanism linking environmental photoperiodic cues to seasonal reproductive physiology through coordinated regulation of multiple reproductive hormone receptors.

title: Menstrual disturbance associated with COVID-19 vaccines: A comprehensive systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that COVID-19 vaccination is associated with mild, dose-dependent increases in menstrual cycle length, with a 19% greater risk compared to unvaccinated individuals. The menstrual disturbance manifests primarily after the first vaccination dose (0.34 days increase) and is more pronounced after the second dose (0.62 days increase), with effects resolving by the second menstrual cycle post-vaccination. All major vaccine platforms—Pfizer-BioNTech, Moderna, AstraZeneca, and Janssen—show similar associations with cycle elongation, along with modest increased risks of post-menopausal bleeding (7%) and intermenstrual bleeding, though heavy menstrual flow changes are not statistically significant. The proposed mechanism involves vaccine-induced spike protein binding to ACE2 receptors in reproductive tissues, triggering CD4+ T cell activation and release of pro-inflammatory cytokines (interferon-gamma, IL-1, IL-2, IL-6, TNF-alpha) that suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis through corticotropin-releasing hormone and glucocorticoid-mediated inhibition of gonadotropin-releasing hormone. While the observed menstrual alterations are real, the authors emphasize they are clinically unmeaningful in magnitude and transient, representing temporary disruptions to hormonal regulation rather than long-term reproductive harm.

title: Case Report on Endometriosis Associated Peritoneal Endometrioid Carcinoma
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents a rare instance of peritoneal endometrioid carcinoma arising from endometriosis, highlighting the malignant transformation pathway in endometriosis-associated neoplasms that develop in 0.7–2.5% of affected women. A 43-year-old woman presenting with menorrhagia, dysmenorrhea, and dyspareunia was found to have endometriotic cysts that had undergone malignant transformation, with histopathology confirming endometrioid adenocarcinoma in peritoneal tissue. The case illustrates a critical hormonal mechanism: aromatase enzyme, abnormally expressed in endometriotic tissue, converts androgens to estrogen, which stimulates prostaglandin E2 production and further amplifies aromatase activity, creating a feed-forward loop promoting tumor growth. This estrogen-dependent mechanism is compounded by obesity-related hyper-estrogenism, which can accelerate malignant transformation through atypical endometriosis intermediary lesions. The sigmoid colon and rectum are identified as the most common extraovarian sites for endometriosis-associated malignancies, and the authors emphasize that clinicians should maintain vigilance for malignant transformation in endometriosis patients, particularly those with concurrent metabolic risk factors.

title: Menopause, Perimenopause, and Migraine: Understanding the Intersections and Implications for Treatment
summary:
# Summary

Female sex hormones regulate the menstrual cycle and profoundly influence migraine occurrence and severity, with women experiencing migraine three times more frequently than men. Estrogen modulates multiple migraine pathways including serotonergic neurotransmission, endogenous opioidergic signaling, and vascular changes, while progesterone provides protective effects through inhibition of neurogenic edema, mast cell activation, and trigeminal nociception. The "estrogen withdrawal" hypothesis explains migraine exacerbation, wherein declining estradiol levels—particularly during menstruation, perimenopause, and oral contraceptive placebo weeks—trigger attacks by increasing neuronal excitability, promoting vasodilation, and elevating calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) levels in the trigeminal system. Approximately 30% of women experience peak migraine symptoms during perimenopause when estrogen fluctuations are most pronounced, though migraine without aura typically improves after menopause when estrogen stabilizes at lower levels. Treatment approaches include CGRP inhibitors for chronic migraine during menopause and progestin-only contraceptives that modestly reduce attack frequency, while combined hormonal contraceptives are contraindicated in migraine with aura due to increased stroke risk.

title: Large animal models in gynecology: status and future perspectives
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis affects approximately 10-15% of women globally and represents a significant clinical burden, causing both physical pain and emotional distress while frequently associating with menstrual disorders and infertility. The condition involves dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and hormone-dependent mechanisms, as estrogen and progesterone normally regulate menstruation through cyclic endometrial exfoliation. Animal models, particularly rhesus monkeys and sheep, have demonstrated the capacity to develop endometriosis-like lesions and reveal how prenatal testosterone exposure disrupts neuroendocrine feedback, leading to elevated luteinizing hormone levels and impaired reproductive function. Additional reproductive pathologies including ovarian lesions, luteal phase dysfunction, and mycoplasma-induced inflammation similarly compromise fertility through mechanisms involving granulosa cell apoptosis, impaired endometrial receptivity, and chronic inflammatory states. These findings underscore that infertility results from multifactorial etiologies encompassing hormonal imbalances, genetic mutations, infectious complications, and environmental exposures, collectively highlighting the need for continued large animal model research to elucidate endometriosis pathophysiology and develop targeted therapeutic interventions.

title: Epigenetic remodeling by sex hormone receptors and implications for gender affirming hormone therapy
summary:
# Summary

Sex hormones mediate sex differences in immune system development through signal transduction by sex hormone receptors, which remodel the regulatory chromatin landscape via DNA methylation, histone modifications, and three-dimensional chromatin restructuring. Estrogen, primarily expressed in females, promotes immune function and drives stronger immune responses through estrogen receptor α (encoded by ESR1) and β (encoded by ESR2), which act as transcriptional regulators capable of activating or repressing target genes and inducing posttranslational protein modifications. Conversely, testosterone exhibits immunosuppressive effects and reduces immune reactivity, including diminished responses to influenza vaccination and more severe COVID-19 outcomes in males due to elevated TMPRSS2 expression facilitating viral entry. These hormonal sex differences result in females exhibiting elevated susceptibility to autoimmune and inflammatory diseases, including rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, and psoriasis, where estrogen enhances IL-17 expression and amplifies inflammatory responses. The androgen receptor, encoded on the X chromosome, regulates gene expression through genomic signaling and non-genomic kinase cascades influenced by coregulators, driving epigenetic heterogeneity particularly in prostate cancer. ESR1 and ESR2 polymorphisms significantly influence individual responses to infection and inflammatory conditions, establishing the critical role of epigenetic regulation by sex hormone receptors in determining sex-biased immunity and disease susceptibility.

title: Correlation between anthropometric and biological parameters of the offspring and parental plasma 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels: a cross-sectional study conducted at Oran, Algeria
summary: This cross-sectional study conducted in Algeria examined how parental vitamin D status, measured by circulating 25-hydroxyvitamin D (25(OH)D) concentrations, correlates with offspring anthropometric and biological parameters. The active hormone form, calcitriol, plays critical roles in reproductive physiology by stimulating endometrial decidualization and synthesis of estradiol and progesterone, while also regulating placental hormones including human chorionic gonadotropin and human placental lactogen. Maternal 25(OH)D levels showed strong positive correlations with multiple newborn parameters including height, femur length, weight, and Apgar scores at both one and five minutes, whereas paternal vitamin D status showed no significant association with offspring measurements. Parental vitamin D deficiency was associated with suboptimal newborn 25(OH)D concentrations and reduced anthropometric parameters, with maternal deficiency demonstrating particular impact on neonatal health outcomes. Risk factors for parental 25(OH)D deficiency included dark skin pigmentation, insufficient sun exposure, anemia in mothers, and recent COVID-19 history. These findings underscore vitamin D's essential role in supporting healthy pregnancy and proper fetal development, with maternal vitamin D status being a significant determinant of neonatal well-being.

title: Androgens modulate endometrial function
summary:
# Androgens Modulate Endometrial Function and Reproductive Outcomes

Androgens, including testosterone and dihydrotestosterone, play complex and context-dependent roles in endometrial physiology and pathology through androgen receptor signaling in both endometrial epithelial and stromal cells. Androgen signaling enhances decidualization by upregulating key markers such as prolactin, IGFBP1, and FOXO1, while also protecting decidualized cells from apoptosis through upregulation of antioxidant enzymes like superoxide dismutase 2. However, elevated plasma androgens and hyperandrogenemia are associated with adverse reproductive outcomes including infertility, pregnancy loss, and recurrent miscarriage, partly through reduced endometrial receptivity and downregulation of implantation-related genes like Wilms tumor suppressor. In endometriosis, abnormally high testosterone concentrations in lesions combined with elevated androgen receptor expression and 5α-reductase activity in ectopic endometrium contribute to disease pathogenesis, and androgen receptor polymorphisms may further influence susceptibility. Similarly, polycystic ovary syndrome patients exhibit elevated endometrial androgen receptor expression associated with their hyperandrogenic state, contributing to infertility. These findings indicate that while androgens are essential for normal endometrial remodeling and early pregnancy establishment, excessive androgen signaling disrupts endometrial receptivity and promotes endometriosis and other reproductive pathologies.

title: Comparative efficacy of single vs. double autologous platelet-rich plasma ovarian injections for improving ovarian response in poor ovarian responders
summary: Autologous platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injections significantly improve ovarian function in poor ovarian responders through growth factors including platelet-derived growth factor, transforming growth factor-beta, and vascular endothelial growth factor, which promote tissue regeneration, angiogenesis, and cellular proliferation while exhibiting anti-inflammatory properties. Both single and double PRP injections substantially increased key ovarian reserve markers, including anti-Müllerian hormone levels, antral follicle count, peak estradiol, and the number of mature follicles, with comparable efficacy between the two dosing regimens. Treatment resulted in significant improvements in oocyte retrieval and embryo quality, with increases in retrieved oocytes, normally fertilized zygotes, usable cleavage embryos, and high-quality embryos across both injection protocols. The mechanism involves activation of dormant primordial follicles and expansion of the ovulatory follicle pool, creating a favorable microenvironment for follicular development and oocyte maturation. These findings demonstrate that PRP represents a promising therapeutic approach for enhancing fertility outcomes in poor ovarian responders, with single injections providing comparable benefits to double injections while reducing treatment burden.

title: A prolactin-targeting antibody to prevent stress-induced peripheral nociceptor sensitization and female postoperative pain
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that stress-induced elevation of prolactin (PRL), a female-predominant neurohormone controlled by estrogen and stress, sensitizes peripheral nociceptors and exacerbates postoperative pain hypersensitivity in females through receptor-isoform-specific mechanisms. Mechanical injury downregulates the protective long isoform of the prolactin receptor (PRLR-L) while leaving the short isoform (PRLR-S) intact, thereby shifting signaling toward pronociceptive PRLR-S pathways that increase nociceptor excitability and pain sensitivity. Notably, this female-specific pain response does not occur in males, as prolactin fails to sensitize male nociceptors regardless of receptor expression. The authors developed PL200,019, a monoclonal antibody that sequesters human prolactin, and demonstrated that prolactin sequestration or pharmacological/genetic inhibition of prolactin signaling prevents nociceptor sensitization and blocks stress-induced mechanical hypersensitivity and postoperative pain in female mice. These findings establish prolactin as a critical link between psychological stress and postoperative pain in females, with potential therapeutic applications for women undergoing surgery.

title: Kisspeptins Regulating Fertility: Potential Future Therapeutic Approach in Infertility Treatment
summary: Kisspeptins, peptides encoded by the KISS1 gene, function as potent stimulators of gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) secretion through binding to the KISS1R receptor, thereby playing a critical role in regulating the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and fertility. Defects in kisspeptin signaling are associated with multiple forms of infertility including idiopathic hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, primary ovarian insufficiency, and hyperprolactinemia-induced amenorrhea, while exogenous kisspeptin administration has demonstrated therapeutic potential in restoring gonadotropin secretion and fertility in affected patients. Locally within the ovary, kisspeptins are expressed in granulosa and theca cells, where they promote ovarian reserves, steroidogenesis, and follicular development while preventing follicular atresia. In endometriosis, women exhibit reduced KISS1 and KISS1R expression in eutopic endometrium despite elevated serum kisspeptin levels, and kisspeptins inhibit matrix metalloproteinase activity and ectopic endometrial migration and implantation through their antimetastatic properties. This complex dual regulation—where diminished local kisspeptin signaling in eutopic endometrium facilitates ectopic tissue implantation while elevated circulating kisspeptins reflect dysregulation—suggests that kisspeptin-based therapies may offer novel approaches for treating both infertility and endometriosis-associated reproductive dysfunction.

title: Women’s Pain Management Across the Lifespan—A Narrative Review of Hormonal, Physiological, and Psychosocial Perspectives
summary:
# Summary

Hormonal fluctuations throughout the female lifespan profoundly influence pain perception through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Estrogen exhibits complex dual effects—it can increase pain thresholds by enhancing endogenous opioid function and modulating serotonin and dopamine pathways, yet also promotes inflammation and increases nociceptive receptor expression, thereby amplifying pain sensitivity. Progesterone provides protective effects by enhancing GABAergic signaling and reducing immune responses, though its withdrawal triggers inflammatory responses that heighten pain sensitivity, particularly during the luteal menstrual phase. Endometriosis exemplifies hormone-dependent chronic pain, where increased aromatase activity drives local estrogen production, promoting inflammation and neurogenic inflammation through vascular endothelial growth factor and nerve growth factor overexpression. Postmenopausal women face compounded pain vulnerability as declining estrogen reduces endogenous opioid availability, impairs pain modulation, and accelerates degenerative conditions including osteoarthritis and intervertebral disc degeneration, while menopausal vasomotor symptoms disrupt sleep and further amplify pain perception. Psychosocial factors including anxiety and emotional distress substantially interact with these hormonal mechanisms to amplify pain signals, underscoring the necessity for personalized, multifactorial pain management approaches across reproductive stages.

title: ​​Delayed Diagnosis of Adult Granulosa Cell Tumor: Emphasizing the Importance of Comprehensive Ovarian Mass Evaluation and Standardized Laboratory Reporting
summary:
# Summary

Granulosa cell tumors (GCTs) are rare ovarian neoplasms that produce high levels of estrogen, inhibin, and anti-müllerian hormone (AMH), creating a hyperestrogenic state that increases susceptibility to endometriosis, uterine leiomyomas, endometrial cancer, and breast cancer. Diagnosis relies on detecting elevated serum markers—inhibin A, inhibin B, and AMH—combined with imaging and molecular testing for the characteristic FOXL2 C402G mutation, found in over 95% of adult GCTs. Histopathological confirmation typically shows immunopositivity for SF-1, calretinin, and inhibin, with FOXL2 staining providing additional diagnostic specificity. The article emphasizes that current American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) laboratory panels inadequately include these tumor markers, leading to delayed diagnoses, and advocates for standardized comprehensive evaluation protocols that integrate serum hormone markers, imaging, and molecular testing alongside FSH measurement to detect the suppression of follicle-stimulating hormone by elevated inhibin levels.

title: Blood Pressure, Cardiometabolic Traits, and Cardiovascular Events in Women With Uterine Fibroids: A Genetic Correlation and Mendelian Randomization Study
journal: Journal of the American Heart Association: Cardiovascular and Cerebrovascular Disease
summary: This genetic correlation and Mendelian randomization study demonstrates that uterine fibroids share substantial genetic overlap with multiple cardiometabolic traits in women. Body mass index, waist-to-hip ratio, and triglycerides were identified as causal risk factors for uterine fibroids through Mendelian randomization analysis, while higher sex hormone-binding globulin levels were protective. Uterine fibroids showed significant genetic correlations with both systolic and diastolic blood pressure, type 2 diabetes, and dyslipidemia, suggesting shared biological mechanisms including central obesity, visceral fat accumulation, and altered lipid metabolism. The study identified smooth muscle cell remodeling and impaired sex steroid bioavailability as key pathophysiological pathways linking these conditions, with visceral adiposity promoting estrogen production while reducing hepatic SHBG synthesis. Interestingly, a novel genetic correlation was reported between uterine fibroids and migraine, potentially mediated by vascular remodeling and hormonal factors. However, genetic evidence for associations between uterine fibroids and major cardiovascular events such as myocardial infarction or stroke was limited, suggesting that cardiometabolic risk factors rather than fibroids themselves directly drive cardiovascular disease in affected women.

title: Mention to Prof. Andrew V. Schally
summary: This review by Prof. Andrew V. Schally demonstrates that the growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH) system functions far beyond its classical role in stimulating pituitary growth hormone secretion, operating through autocrine and paracrine mechanisms to regulate cell proliferation, apoptosis, inflammation, and tissue repair. GHRH agonists such as MR-409 show therapeutic promise across multiple disorders by promoting myocardial function, reducing oxidative stress and inflammation, enhancing neurological recovery after stroke, and ameliorating muscle atrophy. In contrast, GHRH antagonists exhibit anticancer properties with low toxicity across diverse malignancies including lung, prostate, breast, and gastrointestinal cancers through tumor growth inhibition and immune modulation. The GHRH/GHRH-R axis is identified as a key regulator of gonadal function and reproductive health, with potential therapeutic applications in treating infertility, endometriosis, and hormone-related reproductive cancers. GHRH agonists additionally stimulate bone marrow-derived stem cells to enhance angiogenesis and inhibit vascular calcification, while both agonists and antagonists modulate immune cells and endothelial function with anti-inflammatory effects. These findings collectively establish GHRH axis modulation as a pleiotropic therapeutic strategy spanning cardiovascular, neurological, metabolic, oncological, and reproductive disorders.

title: Efficacy of high-dose estrogen and progesterone sequential assisted hysteroscopic cold knife separation versus hysteroscopic cold knife separation alone in the treatment of infertility or amenorrhea caused by severe intrauterine adhesion: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Intrauterine adhesions result from endometrial damage caused by trauma or infection, leading to abnormal repair mechanisms involving excessive TGF-β-mediated extracellular matrix deposition, which manifests clinically as amenorrhea, infertility, dysmenorrhea, and decreased menstrual volume. Hysteroscopic cold knife separation surgery minimizes thermal damage to surrounding tissues compared to laser approaches and effectively reduces adhesive tissue; however, surgical intervention alone achieved only 79.27% effectiveness with a 29.27% re-adhesion rate. High-dose sequential estrogen and progesterone therapy administered post-operatively significantly enhanced outcomes by promoting endometrial regeneration, improving the intrauterine environment, and preventing adhesion reformation, resulting in a combined treatment approach with 94.07% effectiveness, 11.02% re-adhesion rate, and substantially higher pregnancy rates compared to surgery alone. The combination therapy also increased endometrial thickness and volume while lowering bleeding scores, with multivariate analysis confirming that combined treatment was an independent protective factor against recurrent intrauterine adhesions. These findings establish sequential hormonal supplementation with estradiol valerate at 4 mg daily as a critical adjuvant strategy that optimizes surgical outcomes and restores reproductive function in severe intrauterine adhesion management.

title: The impact of sex on blood pressure
journal: Current Opinion in Nephrology and Hypertension
summary:
# Summary

Estrogen and progesterone exert protective effects on blood pressure regulation through multiple mechanisms, primarily by inhibiting the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) and suppressing vasoconstrictor expression, whereas androgens activate this pathway. The loss of estrogen's cardioprotective effects following menopause leads to sympathetic nervous system dominance, vascular stiffness, and increased cardiovascular risk. Notably, gynecological disorders including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and uterine fibroids are associated with elevated hypertension risk, suggesting shared hormonal pathways. The formulation of hormone therapy matters critically: while oral estrogens paradoxically activate the RAAS and increase blood pressure, transdermal estrogens produce neutral or hypotensive effects. Hypertension in women carries disproportionate consequences, including increased risk for acute coronary syndrome, heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, and microvascular damage. Therapeutic strategies including Mediterranean diet components, aerobic exercise, and dietary modifications with potassium and reduced sodium effectively mitigate hypertension risk in women.

title: hUMSC-derived exosomes alleviate follicular interstitial cell autophagy by let-7a-5p/AMPK/mTOR axis in POI rats
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that human umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cell-derived exosomes (hUMSC-Exos) effectively treat premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) induced by cyclophosphamide in rats by suppressing excessive autophagy in theca interstitial cells (TICs), the key hormone-producing follicular cells. The therapeutic mechanism operates through the let-7a-5p/AMPK/mTOR signaling axis, where the microRNA let-7a-5p carried by hUMSC-Exos targets and inhibits AMPK, thereby suppressing the mTOR-regulated autophagy pathway that cyclophosphamide had aberrantly activated. Following hUMSC-Exos treatment, POI rats showed restoration of ovarian morphology and weight, restoration of reproductive hormones (estradiol and anti-Mullerian hormone), recovery of functional follicles, and improved reproductive capacity. The study confirms that hUMSC-Exos successfully accumulated in ovarian tissue and TICs after systemic administration, providing a promising cell-free regenerative therapy for chemotherapy-induced POI by correcting the dysregulated autophagy that underlies ovarian tissue damage and hormone depletion.

title: Ovarian Hormones and Addictive Behaviour Vulnerability: Insights From Preclinical Studies
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian hormones, particularly estradiol, significantly enhance vulnerability to psychoactive substance addiction in females across multiple drug classes including cocaine, methamphetamine, nicotine, ketamine, and tetrahydrocannabinol, though heroin shows a more complex pattern where estradiol reduces consumption during the acquisition phase while progesterone facilitates it during extinction. Females demonstrate heightened sensitivity to drug reward and greater motivation for substance-seeking throughout various phases of addiction (acquisition, maintenance, extinction, and reinstatement), with hormonal fluctuations across reproductive cycles directly modulating consumption patterns and relapse vulnerability. The molecular mechanisms underlying these sex differences involve estrogen receptor signaling pathways that modulate dopaminergic and catecholaminergic neurotransmission in reward-related brain regions, with stress-induced relapse particularly pronounced in females when paired with stressors like yohimbine and caffeine. Progesterone exhibits a protective effect against cocaine intake during reinstatement, suggesting potential therapeutic applications for hormone-based interventions in treating substance use disorder in women, though the hormone's role varies across drug types and addiction phases.

title: Associations of Gestation Length and Offspring Birthweight for Gestational Age with Menopausal Symptoms and Age of Natural Menopause at Midlife among Women Enrolled in a Pre-birth Longitudinal Cohort
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated whether gestational factors and offspring birth size are associated with menopausal symptoms and age at natural menopause in midlife women. The research found that shorter gestation length and preterm birth history were associated with increased risk of heart discomfort symptoms, while higher offspring birthweight for gestational age was linked to greater risk of hot flashes, sweating, and physical exhaustion. Additionally, women with endometriosis or infertility history showed elevated risk for menopausal symptoms, and a history of small-for-gestational-age (SGA) infant delivery was protective against hot flashes. The study's mechanistic framework suggests that shared underlying factors—including maternal stress hormones, inflammation during pregnancy, oxidative stress, endothelial dysfunction, and maternal metabolic conditions like obesity and gestational diabetes—may explain the associations between adverse birth outcomes and menopausal experiences, though gestation length itself showed minimal correlation with overall menopausal symptom burden or age at menopause onset.

title: Short-term moderate caloric restriction in the rhesus macaque attenuates markers of ovarian aging in select populations
summary: Moderate caloric restriction (CR) attenuates markers of ovarian aging in rhesus macaques, a primate model with reproductive anatomy and age-related ovarian changes similar to human females. CR preserved ovarian reserve by maintaining higher numbers of primordial and multilayer follicles in aging animals, particularly in those with irregular menstrual cycling, while preventing the age-related accumulation of collagen and loss of hyaluronic acid in the ovarian microenvironment—changes that normally drive ovarian fibrosis and decreased fertility. These findings suggest that CR slows physiologic aging through multiple mechanisms including reduced body weight, improved metabolism, and decreased insulin resistance, ultimately delaying reproductive aging and potentially improving reproductive longevity. Notably, the benefits of CR appeared contingent on menstrual status during aging rather than chronological age alone, with irregularly cycling older animals showing the most dramatic preservation of follicle populations compared to controls. The timing of CR intervention proved critical, as juvenile-onset CR in nonhuman primates did not improve survival or reproductive outcomes, unlike in rodents where early implementation enhanced longevity. These data establish CR as a potential intervention to counteract age-related ovarian decline in primates and highlight the importance of intervention timing in determining reproductive health outcomes.

title: Endometrial Organoids and Their Role in Modeling Human Infertility
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial organoids have emerged as powerful three-dimensional models that closely mimic the cellular composition, hormonal responsiveness, and function of the human endometrium, making them valuable platforms for studying infertility mechanisms and implantation dynamics. These organoids can be cultivated using defined growth factors including FGF10, R-spondin-1, and inhibitors of BMP and TGF-β signaling (Noggin and A83-01), while progesterone regulates key receptivity markers through downregulation of estrogen receptor-alpha and promotion of anti-inflammatory actions. Research reveals that infertile organoids exhibit dysregulated cell cycle activity during the secretory phase, inadequate epithelial differentiation, and altered hormonal responsiveness compared to fertile controls, with endometriosis organoids specifically showing progesterone receptor-B downregulation linked to epigenetic changes in methylation patterns. Organoid transplantation demonstrates therapeutic potential for treating endometrial dysfunction and fibrotic lesions in models of Asherman syndrome and adenomyosis, with mechanisms including enhanced vascularization through VEGF expression and improved cellular metabolism. Additionally, endometrial organoids serve as critical toxicology and drug screening platforms, revealing that bisphenol A induces oxidative stress and apoptosis, while candidate therapeutics like dacomitinib show promise for treating endometrial cancer models.

title: Hormone Replacement Therapy and Cardiovascular Health in Postmenopausal Women
summary:
# Summary

Postmenopausal women experience significantly elevated cardiovascular disease risk compared to premenopausal women, a difference largely attributable to the dramatic decline in estradiol and progesterone following menopause. Estrogen exerts cardioprotective effects by binding to estrogen receptors on endothelial cells and vascular smooth muscle, promoting vasodilation, decreasing blood pressure, and regulating steroid hormone-responsive genes and signaling pathways critical for vascular function. The loss of ovarian hormone production in menopause alters the testosterone-to-estradiol ratio, with elevated testosterone and reduced estradiol correlating with increased incidence of coronary artery disease, heart failure, and hypertension. Menopausal hormone therapy effectively alleviates vasomotor symptoms such as hot flushes and night sweats, which themselves are associated with cardiovascular risk factors and adverse outcomes including arterial stiffness and vascular dysfunction. Early ovarian insufficiency or bilateral oophorectomy produces particularly abrupt hormone loss and correspondingly elevated risks for hypertension, coronary artery disease, heart failure, stroke, and thromboembolism. These findings underscore the critical role of sex hormone homeostasis in maintaining vascular health across the lifespan.

title: Cardiovascular Disease Risk in Women with Menopause
summary:
# Summary

Menopause represents a critical transition that substantially elevates cardiovascular disease risk in women, primarily through the loss of estrogen's cardioprotective effects. Estrogen deficiency during menopause triggers multiple adverse pathways including endothelial dysfunction, arterial stiffness, and deterioration of the lipid profile characterized by increased total and LDL cholesterol, elevated triglycerides, and decreased HDL cholesterol. The severity of cardiovascular risk is amplified by menopause-associated metabolic changes such as visceral fat accumulation and metabolic syndrome progression, alongside menopausal vasomotor symptoms including hot flashes, which correlate with unfavorable lipid profiles, insulin resistance, reduced endothelial function, and increased carotid atherosclerosis. Additional mechanistic pathways contributing to cardiovascular disease risk include progesterone-mediated impairment of sleep quality, depressive symptoms, and emerging biomarkers such as elevated inflammatory markers (hs-CRP), altered adiponectin levels, and dysregulated microRNAs that promote systemic inflammation and endothelial dysfunction. Early-onset menopause or surgical menopause without estrogen replacement therapy substantially elevates coronary heart disease and heart failure risk, while reproductive history factors such as gestational diabetes, gestational hypertension, and early menarche further predispose women to menopausal cardiovascular complications.

title: Causal relationship between physical activity and common gynecologic conditions: a two-sample Mendelian randomization study
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study demonstrates that physical activity patterns causally influence risk for multiple gynecologic conditions through exercise modality-specific mechanisms. Walking for pleasure, strenuous sports, and other forms of exercise substantially reduce risk of estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer, endometrial cancer, and endometriosis, with strenuous sports showing the most dramatic protective effects (OR = 0.06 for endometrial cancer and OR = 0.17 for endometriosis). Conversely, sedentary behaviors and leisure screen time increase cancer and ovarian cyst risk, establishing physical inactivity as a significant modifiable risk factor. The study reveals a paradoxical association between walking and polycystic ovary syndrome risk while showing strenuous exercise protects against PCOS, suggesting complex hormonal mechanisms underlying these relationships. These findings underscore that specific exercise modalities provide differential protection against hormone-related gynecologic pathologies, positioning physical activity as a crucial preventive intervention for these common disorders.

title: Sex Hormone Levels and Pain Thresholds in the Luteal Phase of Healthy Women
summary: This study examined how gonadal hormones modulate pain sensitivity during the luteal phase of the menstrual cycle in healthy women. Lower serum FSH concentrations were associated with greater cold pain thresholds, while higher LH levels correlated with increased ischemic pain tolerance, suggesting that gonadotropins differentially regulate nociception through distinct mechanisms. The mechanistic basis likely involves FSH-mediated activation of fatty acid amide hydrolase, which degrades endocannabinoids and thereby reduces pain-suppressing signaling, alongside progesterone's interaction with TRPV1 receptors to enhance pain sensitivity. Notably, psychological factors including anxiety, depression, sleep quality, and pain catastrophization also emerged as significant predictors of pain thresholds independent of hormonal effects, indicating a multifactorial model of pain perception during the luteal phase. These findings support the clinical observation that females experience heightened pain sensitivity and postoperative pain during the luteal phase, with hormonal fluctuations serving as one of several biological contributors to this phenomenon. The absence of correlation between sex hormones and mechanical pain thresholds suggests that hormone-pain relationships are modality-specific rather than globally affecting all pain pathways.

title: The Placental Steroid Hypothesis of Human Brain Evolution
summary:
# Summary

This article proposes the placental steroid hypothesis, suggesting that the placenta's production of sex steroid hormones—particularly testosterone and estradiol—has been fundamental to human brain evolution and development. The placenta regulates sex steroid levels during prenatal life by producing progesterone, which serves as a substrate for DHEA and subsequent androgen production, with the enzyme aromatase converting androgens to estrogens in both placental and brain tissue. These prenatal sex steroids regulate multiple aspects of cognition, physiology, and fertility through their effects on hypothalamic function and cortical development, with androgens promoting cortical progenitor proliferation while estrogens facilitate synaptic formation and stabilization through BDNF expression and Notch signaling upregulation. The enhanced prenatal steroidogenesis and placental aromatase expression unique to humans enable a balance between androgen-mediated neuronal proliferation and estrogen-regulated synapse formation, with aromatase acting as a critical rate-limiting enzyme. The article further notes that this hormonal system is sex-dependent, with male fetuses showing higher placental DHEA and aromatase levels, and links dysregulation of this system to neurodevelopmental conditions such as microcephaly and autism through altered estrogen-response-element signaling in recently evolved brain development genes.

title: Human Cervix Chip: A Preclinical Model for Studying the Role of the Cervical Mucosa and Microbiome in Female Reproductive Health
