Notably, stimulation of more than six acupoints yielded superior results, and both manual and electroacupuncture approaches demonstrated clinical benefit, establishing acupuncture as an evidence-supported intervention for LUFS-related infertility with superior tolerability compared to conventional ovulation-induction drugs.

title: Assessing and Addressing Cardiovascular and Obstetric Risks in Patients Undergoing Assisted Reproductive Technology: A Scientific Statement From the American Heart Association
summary:
# Summary

This American Heart Association scientific statement examines cardiovascular and obstetric complications associated with assisted reproductive technology (ART). During ART, exogenous gonadotropins trigger excessive follicular recruitment and luteinization, leading the corpus luteum to produce supraphysiologic amounts of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), which increases vascular permeability and causes perifollicular neovascularization. In severe cases, this cascade results in ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS), characterized by massive intravascular-to-extravascular fluid shifts that produce intravascular hypovolemia and potentially life-threatening complications including hypotension, arrhythmias, syncope, and thromboembolic events. Beyond OHSS, supraphysiologic estrogen levels during ART substantially elevate risk for venous and arterial thromboembolism, with fresh embryo transfer conferring additional risk compared to frozen transfer. ART is also associated with increased risks of preeclampsia, gestational diabetes, and preterm birth, complications partly mediated by VEGF dysregulation and epigenetic changes. The statement emphasizes that underlying infertility causes such as endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome themselves increase cardiovascular disease risk, and recommends risk-reduction strategies including single embryo transfer, low-dose gonadotropin protocols, and prophylactic anticoagulation for high-risk patients.

title: Estrogen and Metabolism: Navigating Hormonal Transitions from Perimenopause to Postmenopause
summary:
# Summary

During perimenopause and the menopausal transition, declining estrogen levels fundamentally disrupt glucose homeostasis and lipid metabolism through multiple mechanisms mediated by estrogen receptor signaling (particularly ESR1 and ESR2). Estrogen normally regulates insulin production, enhances hepatic insulin sensitivity, supports pancreatic β-cell survival, and actively suppresses de novo lipogenesis through modulation of malonyl-CoA availability; its decline precipitates insulin resistance, metabolic disorders, and characteristic lipid abnormalities including elevated apolipoprotein B, LDL cholesterol, and triglycerides. Additionally, the hormonal transition causes unfavorable body composition changes—central adiposity shifts, decreased lean body mass, and reduced energy expenditure—that compound cardiometabolic and cardiovascular disease risk. Lifestyle interventions including regular physical activity (≥6000 steps daily), adequate protein intake (1–1.2 g/kg/day), polyunsaturated fatty acids, and phytoestrogen-rich foods (soybeans, green tea, papaya) provide clinically effective management of metabolic complications and vasomotor symptoms. When lifestyle modifications prove insufficient, menopausal hormone therapy with low-dose transdermal estradiol formulations offers robust benefits for vasomotor symptoms and bone protection while minimizing thrombotic risk compared to standard-dose oral formulations.

title: Diminished Ovarian Reserve: A Narrative Review of Etiologies and Possible Therapeutic Approaches
summary: Diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) encompasses multiple etiologies with distinct molecular mechanisms that converge on follicle loss and reduced reproductive capacity. Chemotherapy agents such as cyclophosphamide and cisplatin directly damage primordial follicles through granulosa cell apoptosis and DNA damage, while radiation therapy causes additional harm via vascular damage and oxidative stress, with total body irradiation and whole abdominal irradiation particularly prone to inducing premature ovarian insufficiency. Endometriosis-related factors significantly contribute to DOR, as ovarian endometriomas reduce AMH levels, accelerate their decline, and suppress oocyte retrieval through mechanisms involving fibrosis, enhanced follicle recruitment and atresia, and elevated free iron concentrations. Genetic factors including BRCA mutations and FMR1 premutations impair DNA repair capacity and trigger oocyte apoptosis, while endometriotic cystectomy paradoxically damages ovarian reserve despite treating the disease by removing surrounding primordial follicles. Emerging therapeutic strategies using stem cell transplantation—including human amnion-derived mesenchymal stem cells, menstrual-derived stem cells, and bone marrow-derived stem cells—demonstrate promise in reversing chemotherapy-induced ovarian injury by promoting vascularization, improving hormonal profiles, and restoring follicle numbers and oocyte quality in preclinical models.

title: Natural Pregnancy Following Oocyte Retrieval in a Luteal-Phase Progestin-Primed Ovarian Stimulation (PPOS) Protocol With Concurrent Blastocyst Cryopreservation: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a 38-year-old woman with endometriosis and secondary infertility due to low ovarian reserve and male factor infertility who achieved natural pregnancy concurrent with luteal-phase progestin-primed ovarian stimulation (PPOS). The PPOS protocol, which uses oral progestin (Provera) to suppress endogenous gonadotropins while allowing follicular development, was employed to avoid the need for injectable gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists or antagonists. The patient underwent oocyte retrieval with concurrent blastocyst cryopreservation following hCG-induced ovulation, while simultaneously becoming naturally pregnant—a rare occurrence diagnosed through elevated serum hCG and progesterone levels combined with ultrasound visualization of a corpus luteum. The PPOS approach proved beneficial for this endometriosis patient, offering an oral alternative to conventional pituitary suppression methods while maintaining the opportunity for spontaneous pregnancy. This case demonstrates that PPOS is a viable and patient-friendly stimulation strategy in endometriosis-associated infertility, with the unexpected natural pregnancy outcome highlighting that conception remains possible even during fertility treatment protocols.

title: The Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Ovarian Axis, Ovarian Disorders, and Brain Aging
summary:
# Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Ovarian Axis and Cognitive Health

The hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis maintains reproductive function through a tightly regulated cascade: GnRH triggers FSH and LH release, FSH stimulates granulosa cells to produce estradiol and promote oocyte maturation, while LH triggers ovulation and progesterone synthesis by the corpus luteum; both estradiol and progesterone provide negative feedback inhibition of gonadotropins to maintain hormonal balance. Endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and primary ovarian insufficiency represent distinct ovarian pathologies that dysregulate this axis—endometriosis increases local estradiol production and induces follicular atresia through inflammation, PCOS features intrinsic theca cell activation producing excessive androgens that impair FSH signaling, and POI is characterized by elevated FSH with low estradiol and antimüllerian hormone. Beyond reproductive effects, these ovarian disorders share a common link to accelerated brain aging and cognitive decline through chronic inflammation and blood-brain barrier disruption; estradiol's neuroprotective capacity and direct crossing of the blood-brain barrier is lost during menopause or in POI, and inflammatory mediators downregulate tight junction proteins, allowing neuroinflammatory infiltration that damages the hippocampus and predisposes to dementia. Women with endometriosis, PCOS, and POI all show two-fold increased dementia risk compared to controls, while hormone replacement therapy offers potential cognitive protection, highlighting the critical role of ovarian hormones in maintaining both reproductive and neurological health across the lifespan.

title: New potential in the treatment of moderate to severe intrauterine adhesions: influencing factors for menstrual improvement
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies multiple therapeutic approaches and influencing factors for treating moderate to severe intrauterine adhesions (IUA) and improving menstrual function. The research demonstrates that surgical techniques using cold knife microsurgery combined with mechanical barriers, hyaluronic acid gel, and platelet-rich plasma represent an optimized first-line strategy, as these interventions synergistically reduce inflammation, suppress fibroblast generation, and promote endometrial regeneration while minimizing thermal injury. Estrogen therapy—delivered via transdermal or transvaginal routes to bypass hepatic metabolism and reduce thrombotic risk—works alongside growth factors and mesenchymal stem cell mechanisms to enhance angiogenesis and cell proliferation while inhibiting apoptosis in damaged endometrial tissue. Beyond surgical and pharmacological interventions, psychological support through mindfulness-based stress reduction and partner involvement, combined with disease-specific follow-up monitoring, significantly improves treatment outcomes and menstrual restoration rates by reducing psychological stress that otherwise interferes with hormonal regulation and endometrial repair.

title: The clinical value of acupuncture combined with Wenjing decoction in treating primary dysmenorrhea in women—a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis demonstrates that Wenjing decoction combined with acupuncture effectively treats primary dysmenorrhea by modulating key hormonal and vascular mechanisms. The combined treatment significantly reduces pain scores and improves clinical effectiveness compared to standard Western medicine, while simultaneously improving uterine artery blood flow by decreasing both resistance and pulsatility indices. The therapeutic mechanism involves normalization of prostaglandin metabolism—specifically reducing elevated PGF2α levels that trigger excessive uterine contractions and ischemic pain, while increasing PGE2. Primary dysmenorrhea is shown to involve multiple pathophysiological pathways including dysregulated sex hormones (estradiol and progesterone), elevated oxytocin and vasopressin, altered immune function (reduced CD4/CD8 ratios), and inflammatory factors. By restoring uterine perfusion and rebalancing hormonal and immune parameters, the acupuncture-herbal medicine combination offers superior pain relief compared to NSAIDs and oral contraceptives alone, supporting integration of traditional Chinese medicine approaches in dysmenorrhea management.

title: World Congress on Osteoporosis, Osteoarthritis and Musculoskeletal Diseases (WCO-IOF-ESCEO 2025)
summary:
# Summary

This conference presentation addresses the complex relationship between chronic kidney disease and bone health, focusing on mineral and bone disorder (CKD-MBD) as a major complication affecting fracture risk and cardiovascular outcomes. Chronic kidney disease causes phosphorus retention, which triggers compensatory elevations in fibroblast growth factor-23 and parathyroid hormone, ultimately leading to renal osteodystrophy—a secondary form of osteoporosis that manifests as high bone turnover, osteomalacia, or adynamic bone disease. Multiple pathological mechanisms contribute to bone fragility in CKD patients, including uremia, inflammation, abnormal bone remodeling, and reduced bone mineral density, while simultaneously promoting vascular calcification through effects on smooth muscle cells—a process further accelerated by uremic toxins and inflammation. Bone turnover markers such as bone-specific alkaline phosphatase and tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase provide non-invasive assessment tools, and emerging therapeutic strategies include early intermittent parathyroid hormone administration to mitigate phosphorus retention and prevent FGF-23 elevation, alongside newer anabolic agents like abaloparatide and biosimilar denosumab. The presentation underscores the interconnected nature of bone fragility, driven by age-related immune dysfunction, estrogen deficiency, and systemic metabolic dysregulation, with emerging evidence suggesting cross-organ communication through myokines, osteokines, and adipokines.

title: Review of hormonal replacement therapy options for the treatments of menopausal symptoms
summary: Menopause results from loss of ovarian follicles due to oocyte apoptosis and impaired ovarian responsiveness to pituitary hormones, causing vasomotor symptoms, mood disturbances, sleep disruption, and long-term metabolic complications including osteoporosis and cardiovascular disease. Hormone replacement therapy effectively alleviates acute menopausal symptoms including hot flashes, night sweats, and urogenital atrophy while preventing bone loss and reducing fracture risk. For women retaining their uterus, progestogens or micronized progesterone must accompany estrogen therapy to prevent endometrial hyperplasia and cancer, with micronized progesterone offering superior vasomotor symptom reduction and sleep improvement. Estrogen monotherapy in postmenopausal women reduces mortality and risk of multiple cancers, congestive heart failure, thromboembolism, and dementia, though oral formulations elevate triglycerides and inflammatory markers compared to transdermal delivery. Transdermal estrogen administration avoids unfavorable hepatic effects of oral preparations while maintaining benefits on lipid profiles and sexual function. These findings support hormone replacement therapy as an evidence-based treatment addressing both acute menopausal symptoms and long-term health outcomes in appropriately selected candidates.

title: Mesenchymal stem cells and platelet rich plasma in the treatment of premature ovarian insufficiency: a scoping review
summary:
# Summary

Premature ovarian failure (POF) is characterized by amenorrhea, hypergonadotropic hypogonadism, and estrogen deficiency before age 40, resulting in infertility and long-term complications including osteoporosis. Multiple etiological factors contribute to POF, including chemotherapy, autoimmune disorders, infections, genetic abnormalities such as Turner and Fragile X syndromes, and premature ovarian aging. Treatment approaches range from hormone replacement therapy and estrogen-based therapies to emerging regenerative strategies; mesenchymal stem cells and platelet-rich plasma show promise by stimulating cellular processes and activating multipotent stem cells to promote ovarian follicle growth and prevent follicular atresia. Adjunctive mitochondrial nutrients such as resveratrol, coenzyme Q10, and melatonin have been studied to improve oocyte quality, while in vitro activation and in vitro fertilization with donor oocytes remain established reproductive options. This scoping review highlights the multifactorial nature of POF and the expanding therapeutic arsenal combining hormonal, cellular, and regenerative medicine approaches to address both symptom management and fertility preservation.

title: Anabolic–Androgenic Steroids Induced Cardiomyopathy: A Narrative Review of the Literature
summary: Chronic supraphysiologic use of anabolic-androgenic steroids (AAS) induces severe cardiomyopathy and multiple cardiovascular complications through multiple interconnected mechanisms. AAS promotes cardiac injury and adverse remodeling primarily via androgen receptor overactivation, oxidative stress with increased reactive oxygen species production, renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system dysregulation, and pro-apoptotic signaling in myocardial and vascular tissues. These pathways collectively trigger hypertension, dyslipidemia with elevated low-density lipoprotein and reduced high-density lipoprotein, myocardial fibrosis and hypertrophy, atherosclerosis, and a prothrombotic state characterized by vascular dysfunction and increased arterial stiffness. AAS abuse is increasingly associated with acute cardiovascular events in young athletes, including cardiomyopathy, atrial fibrillation, myocardial infarction, stroke, and systemic embolism. While AAS has legitimate therapeutic uses for male hypogonadism, osteoporosis, and aplastic anemia, the considerable cardiovascular and systemic toxicity associated with supraphysiologic dosing represents a significant public health concern.

title: Obesity/Overweight as a Meaningful Modifier of Associations Between Gene Polymorphisms Affecting the Sex Hormone-Binding Globulin Content and Uterine Myoma
summary: This study demonstrates that body mass index (BMI) acts as a critical modifier of genetic susceptibility to uterine myoma (fibroids) through sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) regulation. In normal-weight women (BMI < 25), the PRMT6 rs17496332 G allele confers protection against myoma by reducing transcriptional activity and altering transcription factor binding, while in overweight/obese women (BMI ≥ 25), the BAIAP2L1 rs3779195 A allele increases myoma risk through effects on liver and adipose tissue gene expression. Higher BMI dramatically decreases SHBG levels while simultaneously increasing circulating estrogen and androgen levels, creating a hormonal milieu that favors myoma development, as excessive adipose tissue converts androgens to estrogens. The identified polymorphisms influence multiple biological pathways including transcriptional regulation, metabolism, and cell proliferation through complex protein-protein interactions involving transcription factors such as SP1, MYC, and EP300. These findings reveal BMI-dependent genetic heterogeneity in myoma pathogenesis, suggesting that personalized risk stratification and intervention strategies must account for both genetic background and metabolic status to effectively prevent or manage uterine fibroids.

title: Advances in biomonitoring technologies for women’s health
summary: Sex and gender biases in global healthcare systems have created significant health gaps affecting women, with nearly 10.8 million women in the USA lacking medical insurance and 1.5 billion women and girls worldwide without access to healthcare facilities. Emerging wearable biomonitoring technologies and artificial intelligence-enhanced diagnostics offer promising solutions to address these disparities by enabling early detection and management of critical women's health conditions including breast cancer, gynecological cancers, fertility problems, and pregnancy complications. These wearable devices facilitate continuous monitoring of key reproductive hormones—including estradiol, progesterone, and cortisol—that regulate menstrual cycles and affect conditions such as endometriosis, which is characterized by chronic menstrual pain and abnormal hormone levels. For endometriosis treatment, GnRH agonists suppress FSH, LH, and estrogen levels but incur side effects like bone density reduction, highlighting the need for improved therapeutic approaches informed by biomonitoring data. Intravaginal temperature tracking devices and novel electrochemical sensors detecting multiple biomarkers like Cystatin C enable personalized assessment of reproductive health and pregnancy complications such as gestational diabetes mellitus. Collectively, these advanced biomonitoring technologies represent a paradigm shift toward precision medicine for women's health, addressing the research funding gaps that have historically left women's conditions understudied.

title: Impact of Tamoxifen on Endometrial Thickness in Women With Thin Endometrium Following Estrogen Therapy During Frozen–Thawed Embryo Transfer
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial thickness is a critical determinant of endometrial receptivity and implantation success in frozen-thawed embryo transfer cycles, with optimal outcomes occurring at 8.7–14.5 mm. While standard estrogen supplementation improves endometrial growth, patients with refractory thin endometrium may benefit from tamoxifen, a selective estrogen receptor modulator that enhances endometrial thickness more effectively than estrogen alone. Tamoxifen acts through multiple mechanisms, including stimulation of the GPR30 receptor, which activates steroidogenic factor 1 and induces local aromatase expression, thereby promoting endometrial proliferation and upregulating proliferation markers like Ki-67 and insulin-like growth factor 1. Three case studies demonstrated that combining tamoxifen with hormone replacement therapy successfully improved endometrial thickness in patients resistant to high-dose estrogen. Although tamoxifen carries risks including endometrial hyperplasia and cancer with prolonged use, short-term adjunctive therapy appears promising for patients with persistent thin endometrium during assisted reproductive treatment. Alternative strategies for refractory cases include intrauterine platelet-rich plasma infusion, vaginal sildenafil with estradiol valerate, and pentoxifylline to enhance uterine perfusion and endometrial growth.

title: Insulin-like growth factor 1 and estrogen from cumulus cell culture increases the success of in vitro fertilization in cattle
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) and estrogen produced by cumulus cells significantly enhance in vitro fertilization success in cattle by improving embryo development and reducing cell death. Six-day-old cumulus cell cultures produced optimal levels of IGF-1 and estrogen, which function as mitogenic and antiapoptotic agents by inhibiting the p53 protein and activating cell cycle progression through cyclin-dependent kinases. When cumulus cell culture media containing these factors were supplemented into embryo culture systems, particularly those with progesterone absorption, embryo cleavage rates increased from 27.48% to 53.61%, morula formation rose from 5.73% to 27.59%, and apoptosis rates decreased dramatically from 70.58% to 31.01%. IGF-1 additionally stimulates granulosa cell proliferation and steroidogenesis while promoting blastocyst formation, effects that are modulated by IGF-binding proteins. These findings establish cumulus cell-derived hormones as critical mediators of oocyte quality and embryo developmental competence, offering a practical approach to improving assisted reproductive technology outcomes in cattle.

title: The effect of iodine supplementation on oocyte apoptosis and proliferation in women with diminished ovarian reserve: a pilot study
summary:
# Iodine Supplementation Improves Oocyte Quality in Diminished Ovarian Reserve

This pilot study demonstrates that iodine supplementation significantly enhances oocyte quality and cumulus cell viability in women with diminished ovarian reserve by reducing apoptosis and upregulating cell proliferation markers. Iodine supplementation increased metaphase II oocytes and live cumulus cells while dramatically reducing early apoptosis (19.3% to 13.7%) and late apoptosis (44.7% to 9.0%), with total apoptosis declining from 64.0% to 22.7%. The therapeutic mechanism involves upregulation of PCNA, a proliferation and DNA repair protein, which correlated strongly with increased live cells and inversely with apoptotic markers. Iodine exerts its effects both indirectly through thyroid hormone synthesis—which regulates follicular development—and directly through its absorption by ovarian tissues and cumulus cells, where it promotes antioxidant and anti-apoptotic properties. These findings suggest iodine supplementation could represent a promising micronutrient-based strategy for improving fertility outcomes in women with diminished ovarian reserve, though the study was limited in scope and warrants further validation in larger cohorts.

title: Outcomes of ovulation induction in obese women with infertility after bariatric surgery
summary: Obesity impairs fertility through multiple hormonal and metabolic mechanisms that bariatric surgery can effectively reverse. Excess adipose tissue increases estrogen production via aromatase activity, which suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and reduces circulating luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone levels, leading to menstrual irregularities and compromised ovulation. At the oocyte level, obesity-induced oxidative stress impairs mitochondrial function and promotes aneuploidy, while also delaying the endometrial window of implantation, reducing receptivity. Bariatric surgery reverses these effects by restoring luteal phase progesterone and improving corpus luteal function, thereby increasing clinical pregnancy rates despite persistent postoperative obesity. The study demonstrates that surgical weight loss particularly benefits women with infertility undergoing ovulation induction, with group 3 patients achieving notably higher cumulative clinical pregnancy rates, suggesting that metabolic improvements from bariatric surgery enhance fertility outcomes independent of final BMI.

title: Impact of gonadotropin selection on risk of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome in predicted high responders: a Menopur in Gonadotropin-releasing hormone Antagonist Single Embryo Transfer–High Responder trial analysis
summary:
# Summary

In high-responder patients undergoing assisted reproductive technology, human menopausal gonadotropin (HP-hMG) significantly reduces ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) risk compared to recombinant follicle-stimulating hormone (rFSH) alone, despite higher total gonadotropin doses. This protective effect of HP-hMG stems from its combined FSH and LH activity, which selectively promotes growth of dominant follicles while suppressing smaller follicle development through altered androgen metabolism, thereby lowering peak estradiol levels and oocyte yield—the primary drivers of OHSS incidence. Early OHSS risk associates with younger age, lower body mass index, shorter infertility duration, elevated baseline estradiol, and high oocyte yields, with rFSH-treated patients showing substantially higher early OHSS rates (31.5% versus 10.0% in patients with >25 oocytes retrieved). Additionally, GnRH agonist triggering reduces late OHSS severity compared to hCG trigger but increases mild OHSS, and patients with endometriosis demonstrated paradoxically reduced OHSS development, suggesting disease-specific effects on ovarian responsiveness.

title: Brain‐Derived Neurotrophic Factor (BDNF) and Sex Differences in Metabolic Regulation
summary: Brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) emerges as a critical regulator of metabolic processes that operates with marked sex differences, particularly through its expression in hypothalamic regions including the ventromedial and dorsomedial hypothalamus. BDNF is widely expressed across multiple tissues—brain, kidney, ovaries, skeletal muscle, and prostate—where it supports neuronal growth, learning, and memory while simultaneously influencing food intake regulation through modulation of appetite-controlling neurons (AgRP and POMC) and integration of metabolic signals from leptin, insulin, and ghrelin. Genetic disruption or reduced expression of BDNF or its receptor TrkB directly causes obesity, hyperphagia, and diabetes, whereas BDNF elevation improves hyperglycemia, suggesting a protective metabolic role. In males, obesity associates with hypogonadism and low testosterone, while women show higher obesity prevalence but greater cardiovascular complications, with both sexes exhibiting distinct BDNF-dependent metabolic phenotypes. Specific deletion of Bdnf in steroidogenic factor-1 (SF1) neurons or ventromedial hypothalamic regions produces hyperphagic behavior and obesity, indicating that BDNF signaling in these specialized neuronal populations is essential for normal energy homeostasis and metabolic regulation in a sex-specific manner.

title: Associations Between Earlier Menarche and Fibroid Severity in Individuals Seeking Hysterectomy
summary: This study demonstrates a strong association between earlier menarche and increased uterine fibroid severity in individuals undergoing hysterectomy. Earlier age at menarche was significantly associated with multiple markers of fibroid burden, including increased symptom severity, greater uterine weight (>250 g), and younger age at hysterectomy. The mechanistic basis for these observations involves prolonged estrogen exposure, as the hormonal dependence of fibroids means that individuals experiencing menarche at earlier ages accumulate greater cumulative estrogen exposure over their reproductive years, potentially accelerating tumorigenesis. Early menarche was also linked to increased physical fibroid burden (number of fibroids) and symptom manifestations such as heavy menstrual bleeding. These findings suggest that menarcheal timing represents an important clinical variable in understanding fibroid severity and may help identify individuals at higher risk for more aggressive fibroid disease requiring surgical intervention.

title: Legume-based green concentrate alleviates negative energy balance and enhances performance, metabolic health, and profitability in postpartum Holstein Friesian cows
summary:
# Summary

Negative energy balance (NEB) in postpartum Holstein Friesian dairy cows severely compromises milk yield, metabolic health, and reproductive function by triggering excessive mobilization of body reserves and elevated beta-hydroxybutyrate (BHB) levels, which inhibit synthesis of critical reproductive hormones including IGF-1 and estrogen. Supplementation with legume-based green concentrate (GC), particularly GC30 containing high proportions of Gliricidia sepium and Indigofera zollingeriana, effectively alleviates NEB by providing enhanced crude protein, energy, and essential amino acids including leucine. GC treatments significantly increased ruminal propionate and butyrate production, improved feed conversion efficiency, increased milk yield and fat content, and substantially reduced circulating and milk BHB levels, thereby restoring energy availability for hormone synthesis. By improving nutrient availability, GC feeding elevated both IGF-1 and estrogen concentrations, signaling restored reproductive readiness and enhanced potential for timely resumption of ovarian cyclicity in postpartum cows. Additionally, GC supplementation reduced feed costs while increasing daily profitability by approximately $11/day compared to control diets. These findings demonstrate that legume-based green concentrate effectively corrects the metabolic and reproductive dysfunction associated with postpartum NEB while simultaneously improving economic returns in dairy production.

title: Life behaviors, educational attainment, and risk for endometriosis: a Mendelian randomization study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study identifies lifestyle and sleep factors as causally associated with endometriosis risk through hormonal mechanisms. Long sleep duration strongly reduces endometriosis risk by stabilizing estrogen levels and promoting ovarian health, while smoking cessation shows a protective effect through re-establishment of estrogen balance. Conversely, sleep disruption impairs ovarian function and reduces estradiol production, potentially by altering melatonin secretion, a key regulator of reproductive hormones. The findings suggest that sufficient sleep duration and smoking cessation may be protective interventions for endometriosis by maintaining proper hormonal regulation, particularly estrogen homeostasis. Although insomnia symptoms showed association with endometriosis, the evidence indicates sleep disturbances may be secondary to endometriosis-related pain rather than a primary causative factor. Overall, the study demonstrates that behavioral modifications affecting sleep quality and smoking status can influence endometriosis risk through hormonal pathways involving estrogen regulation and ovarian function.

title: A Case of Progestogen Hypersensitivity: Systemic Allergic Response
summary: This case report describes progestogen hypersensitivity (PH), a rare systemic allergic reaction to progesterone that can occur through both Type I and Type IV hypersensitivity mechanisms. Progesterone, normally produced by the corpus luteum during the luteal phase and by the placenta during pregnancy, acts on extranuclear progesterone receptors found on mast cells, triggering activation of ERK and MAPK kinases that precipitate mast cell degranulation and release of histamine, prostaglandins, and leukotrienes. These mediators cause acute allergic symptoms including urticaria, angioedema, and potentially anaphylaxis, with symptoms characteristically peaking during the luteal phase when progesterone levels are highest. While PH is typically associated with prior exogenous progestin exposure from oral contraceptives, it can also develop from endogenous progesterone in patients without prior sensitization, possibly through the immunogenic properties of synthetic progestin side chains or through cross-reactivity with endogenous steroid precursors. Treatment approaches include immediate management of allergic symptoms, followed by progesterone desensitization, continuous low-dose oral contraceptives, or for patients not desiring pregnancy, GnRH agonists, selective estrogen receptor modulators, or oophorectomy; omalizumab, which binds IgE and downregulates mast cells and basophils, has also demonstrated success in treating PH-related anaphylaxis.

title: Endometrial cancer: from clinical reality to molecular treatment
summary:
# Endometrial Cancer: Molecular Mechanisms and Hormonal Regulation

Endometrial cancer develops through multiple molecular pathways involving both genetic alterations and hormonal imbalances. TP53 mutations and loss of PTEN function prominently drive endometrial carcinogenesis by activating the PI3K-AKT signaling axis, which promotes cell proliferation and survival. Inherited genetic syndromes including Lynch syndrome, Cowden syndrome, and polymerase proofreading-associated polyposis substantially increase endometrial cancer risk through defects in DNA mismatch repair and replication fidelity. Hormonally, unopposed estrogen exposure—exacerbated by obesity, tamoxifen use, and endocrine disruption from compounds like bisphenol A—drives endometrial hyperplasia and cancer development, while progesterone acts protectively by upregulating antiproliferative factors like FOXO1 and IGFBP-1 and achieving a 60% response rate as primary treatment. MicroRNAs such as miR-18a and miR-222 regulate estrogen receptor alpha expression, providing an additional layer of post-transcriptional control over cancer progression. Polycystic ovary syndrome and other conditions disrupting the estrogen-progesterone balance represent significant modifiable risk factors for endometrial cancer development.

title: Caloric Restriction, the Menstrual Cycle, and Sleep in Women Without Obesity
journal: The Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism
summary: Caloric restriction and decreased energy availability independently disrupt sleep architecture in non-obese women, reducing sleep efficiency and increasing wake after sleep onset and awakenings, with effects most pronounced during the late luteal phase of the menstrual cycle. The neuropeptide orexin, which promotes wakefulness, shows significant positive correlations with sleep fragmentation and number of awakenings, suggesting a mechanistic link between energy deficit and arousal regulation. Menstrual cycle hormones, specifically rising progesterone metabolites (pregnanediol-3-glucuronide) and estrogen metabolites (estrone-3-glucuronide), are associated with increased nighttime awakenings and reduced sleep efficiency, indicating cyclical hormonal influences on sleep quality. These findings demonstrate that the interaction between dietary restriction, menstrual cycle phase, and neuroendocrine signaling substantially impairs sleep continuity in women, with orexin activity potentially serving as a mediator of energy availability-dependent sleep disruption. The late luteal phase emerges as a particularly vulnerable window for sleep degradation during caloric restriction, suggesting that reproductive cycle timing should be considered when assessing sleep disturbances related to metabolic stress.

title: The cutting-edge progress of novel biomedicines in ovulatory dysfunction therapy
summary: Ovulatory dysfunction represents a major cause of infertility and menstrual disorders in women, with multiple underlying etiologies including polycystic ovary syndrome, hyperprolactinemia, hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction, and metabolic conditions such as diabetes and obesity. The physiological process of ovulation involves coordinated hormonal signaling, wherein luteinizing hormone activates epidermal growth factor-like factors to promote cumulus expansion and matrix metalloproteinase secretion, while progesterone regulates inflammation and angiogenesis during the periovulatory period. Novel therapeutic approaches target multiple pathways to restore ovulatory function: antioxidants and clomiphene reduce reactive oxygen species and inflammatory cytokines, mesenchymal stem cell transplantation enhances follicular survival through secretion of growth factors including vascular endothelial growth factor and insulin-like growth factor-1, metformin improves insulin sensitivity and suppresses ovarian fibrosis through NF-κB inhibition, and glucagon-like peptide-1 agonists regulate metabolic homeostasis. Advanced drug delivery systems including hydrogels, nanoparticles, and extracellular vesicles enable controlled and targeted delivery of therapeutic agents to improve bioavailability and clinical efficacy in treating ovulatory dysfunction.

title: MON-197 Discordant Serum and Urine hCG in a Postmenopausal Woman: Diagnostic Pitfalls and the Role of Assay Interference
summary: This case report describes a diagnostic challenge in a postmenopausal woman with a history of endometriosis treated by hysterectomy who presented with discordant serum and urine human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) levels. The key finding is that elevated serum hCG in postmenopausal patients may result from benign sources, particularly ectopic hCG production by the pituitary gland rather than malignancy. The study highlights that heterophilic antibodies and cross-reactivity with other glycoproteins can cause false elevations in immunoassays, explaining discrepancies between serum and urine hCG measurements. Additionally, variability in hCG isoforms present in urine versus serum may contribute to different detection thresholds across assay types. The authors emphasize that hormonal therapy to suppress pituitary-derived hCG represents a potential management strategy when assay interference is identified as the underlying cause. This case underscores the critical importance of recognizing assay-related pitfalls in hCG interpretation to avoid unnecessary investigations for occult malignancy in postmenopausal women with prior endometriosis history.

title: Understanding migraine throughout a woman’s life and the role of calcitonin gene-related peptide: A narrative review
summary:
# Summary

Migraine prevalence is three to four-fold higher in women than men, with this sex difference emerging at puberty and driven by complex interactions between estrogen and calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) signaling. Estrogen fluctuations, particularly the sharp decline before menstruation and during the menopausal transition, increase migraine susceptibility by enhancing CGRP signaling, whereas stable and elevated estrogen levels suppress CGRP signaling and provide protective effects. Women have higher baseline plasma CGRP levels than men, and the two systems are deeply intertwined—estrogen directly regulates CGRP expression and receptor signaling while modulating related pathways such as oxytocin release, which itself inhibits CGRP. CGRP-targeting therapeutics including monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab, eptinezumab) and receptor antagonists (atogepant) effectively treat migraine by blocking this key signaling pathway. Additional hormonal factors including prolactin dysregulation and lower testosterone levels in affected men further influence migraine risk, suggesting that sex hormone regulation of CGRP represents a fundamental mechanism underlying the higher female burden of this disabling condition. This framework explains why migraine attacks cluster around menstruation, improve during pregnancy with estrogen stabilization, and worsen during postpartum periods and menopause.

title: Distinct effects of progesterone and cholesterol on lipid membranes: insights from biophysical experiments and molecular dynamics simulations
summary:
# Summary

This biophysical study reveals that progesterone and cholesterol exert opposing effects on lipid membrane organization, with distinct mechanistic implications for cellular signaling. While cholesterol promotes lipid packing and stabilizes phase-separated membrane domains, progesterone disrupts phase separation, reduces line tension, and increases lipid lateral diffusion, ultimately causing membrane thinning and enhanced permeability. These structural alterations by progesterone appear to facilitate rapid non-genomic signaling pathways, positioning progesterone as a dynamic regulator of membrane organization distinct from the ordering effects of cholesterol. The findings demonstrate that steroid hormones modulate membrane biophysics through non-specific interactions with lipid bilayers, complementing their classical genomic effects mediated through intracellular receptors. These membrane-level mechanisms may underlie progesterone's anti-inflammatory and anti-tumorigenic properties, and have important implications for understanding how synthetic progestins like medroxyprogesterone acetate influence cellular membrane function and potentially contribute to therapeutic outcomes. The opposing effects of these steroids on membrane fluidity and organization suggest that careful consideration of membrane-perturbing properties is essential for rational drug design and clinical application of steroid-based therapies.

title: Atrial Mass in Pregnancy: A Rare Case of Intracardiac Ectopic Liver
journal: Annals of Thoracic Surgery Short Reports
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of ectopic liver tissue in the right atrium of a pregnant woman, highlighting the role of hormonal changes in promoting this unusual condition. During pregnancy, elevated estrogen and progesterone levels upregulate hepatic synthetic function and promote regenerative and dysplastic changes in hepatocellular tissue, which may facilitate the growth or manifestation of ectopic liver deposits. The patient's ectopic hepatic mass was identified in the third trimester and was successfully treated through surgical resection four months postpartum. Risk factors for intracardiac ectopic liver include obesity and potentially endometriosis, conditions that may increase susceptibility to aberrant hepatic tissue development. The case demonstrates that pregnancy-associated hormonal changes can exacerbate or unmask normally quiescent ectopic tissues, and that surgical intervention remains the definitive treatment for symptomatic intracardiac masses of this nature.

title: Melatonin in the Treatment of Female Infertility: Update on Biological and Clinical Findings
summary:
# Summary

Melatonin, a pleiotropic molecule secreted by the pineal gland and multiple peripheral tissues, improves female fertility through interconnected antioxidant and immunomodulatory mechanisms. By binding to MT1 and MT2 receptors on granulosa cells and oocytes, melatonin scavenges reactive oxygen species, supports mitochondrial function, promotes intracellular antioxidant enzyme production, and prevents pro-apoptotic pathways, thereby enhancing oocyte quantity and quality while slowing age-related ovarian decay. At the uterine level, melatonin increases receptivity and enhances implantation by preventing immune rejection of the embryo while improving placental health through local synthesis by placental melatonin synthases. Beyond its reproductive benefits, melatonin alleviates symptoms of endometriosis and adenomyosis—conditions strongly associated with female infertility—and improves clinical outcomes including fertilization rates, blastocyst development, and pregnancy rates. The compound also demonstrates efficacy in treating endocrine-related fertility disorders such as polycystic ovary syndrome and premature ovarian failure, making it a promising multimodal therapeutic agent for restoring fertility in women with hormonal and inflammatory reproductive pathologies.

title: Clinical and Histopathological Correlates of Endometrial Proliferative Lesions in Perimenopausal Women: A Retrospective Study with Internal Validation of a Risk Model
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study examined endometrial proliferative lesions in perimenopausal women, revealing how hormonal imbalances drive pathological progression. Estrogen stimulates endometrial epithelial growth, while progesterone regulates endometrial thickness; during perimenopause, declining progesterone creates estrogen dominance that promotes endometrial proliferation and hyperplasia. The study found that unopposed estrogen causes endometrial hyperplasia without atypia (74.6% of cases, low malignant potential) or with atypia/endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia (20.0% of cases, premalignant), which can progress to estrogen-dependent Type I endometrial cancer characterized by PTEN, PIK3CA, KRAS, and CTNNB1 mutations. Metabolic factors significantly influenced disease severity: diabetes independently increased risk of advanced pathology 2.75-fold, obesity and hypertension showed dose-dependent associations with histopathological severity, while long-term oral contraceptive use and prior non-atypical hyperplasia were protective. Endometrial thickness correlated strongly with histopathological severity and emerged as a key clinical marker, with combined assessment of thickness and metabolic comorbidities identifying women at highest risk for advanced lesions.

title: Understanding Peritoneal Fluid Estrogen and Progesterone Concentrations Permits Individualization of Medical Treatment of Endometriosis-Associated Pain with Lower Doses, Especially in Adolescents Not Requiring Contraception
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that endometriosis-associated pain can be effectively managed through hormonal therapies that reduce peritoneal fluid estrogen and progesterone concentrations by suppressing ovulation. Oral contraceptives, progestogen monotherapy, and GnRH agonists/antagonists all inhibit ovarian function, decreasing steroid hormone production and thereby alleviating pain in both superficial and ovarian endometriosis lesions. The research reveals that individual endometriosis lesions exhibit heterogeneous biochemical properties including variable aromatase activity and progesterone resistance, which explains the variable therapeutic response to different treatments. By measuring peritoneal fluid hormone concentrations, clinicians can individualize medical therapy with lower doses, particularly benefiting adolescents who do not require contraception. The findings establish that the hypoestrogenic effect rather than progesterone action is the primary mechanism by which these therapies reduce endometriosis pain, with superficial lesions being especially responsive to lowered estrogen concentrations in peritoneal fluid.

title: Vitamin D Supplementation and Testosterone Levels in Breast Cancer Survivors
summary: This study examines the complex relationship between vitamin D supplementation, testosterone levels, and cancer outcomes in breast cancer survivors. Vitamin D functions as a multifaceted regulator of immune modulation, cell proliferation, and hormone synthesis, with circulating 25(OH)D levels influencing sex hormone-binding globulin and consequently testosterone bioavailability. In breast cancer survivors, elevated testosterone levels associate with increased risk of disease recurrence, distant metastasis, and breast cancer-specific mortality, particularly since aromatase inhibitor therapy can paradoxically increase testosterone, which may be converted to estrogen in postmenopausal women. Contrary to initial hypotheses, vitamin D supplementation did not significantly affect testosterone levels in the study population; instead, baseline testosterone levels and body mass index emerged as the strongest predictors of testosterone trajectories. However, the findings suggest that women with lower baseline testosterone showed significant increases across both treatment groups, while those with higher baseline levels demonstrated slight reductions, indicating a homeostatic regulation pattern. These results highlight the importance of individualizing hormone management strategies in breast cancer survivors, considering that alternative approaches such as low glycemic index diets and moderate-intensity physical activity may more effectively reduce androgens through improved insulin sensitivity.

title: Reconsidering Hormone Replacement Therapy: Current Insights on Utilisation in Premenopausal and Menopausal Women: An Overview
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines hormone replacement therapy (HRT) efficacy and safety across menopausal stages, with particular attention to hormonal mechanisms underlying menopausal symptoms and long-term health outcomes. Estrogen deficiency during perimenopause and menopause, characterized by declining estradiol, inhibin B, and increased FSH, directly causes vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes, night sweats), urogenital symptoms (vaginal dryness), and mood disturbances—all effectively treated by HRT. Beyond symptom relief, HRT improves metabolic health by enhancing insulin sensitivity, lipid profiles (increasing HDL, decreasing LDL), reducing abdominal fat and HOMA-IR, and preventing type 2 diabetes progression, while also preserving bone mineral density and preventing osteoporosis-related fractures. However, combined estrogen-progestin therapy must be carefully balanced: while progestin prevents endometrial hyperplasia and reduces estradiol receptor density through antimitotic effects, estrogen monotherapy carries increased risks of endometrial proliferation; additionally, HRT use is associated with elevated breast cancer, cardiovascular disease, and stroke risk in certain postmenopausal populations. The review underscores that surgical menopause creates particularly acute estrogen decline leading to rapid endothelial dysfunction and myocardial infarction risk, highlighting the clinical importance of individualized HRT regimens that weigh symptom control and metabolic benefits against cardiovascular and cancer risks.

title: Reproductive Fitness and the Links to Chronic Disease and Systemic Aging
summary:
# Summary

Female reproductive aging represents one of the earliest manifestations of systemic aging in humans, with declining oocyte quality and ovarian reserve depletion occurring decades before other age-related conditions emerge. The hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis regulates this process through gonadotropin-releasing hormone stimulation of follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone, which in turn promote estrogen and progesterone production by ovarian follicles; these hormones maintain cardiovascular, cognitive, metabolic, and bone health throughout life. Critical reproductive transitions, including puberty, pregnancy, and menopause, act as physiological inflection points that shape long-term systemic health, with early puberty (before age 12) linked to elevated risks of type 2 diabetes, metabolic syndrome, polycystic ovary syndrome, and cardiovascular disease. Menopause emerges as a powerful driver of systemic aging through cessation of ovarian hormone production, leading to unfavorable lipid shifts, accelerated vascular aging, and increased vulnerability to metabolic and neurological diseases. Beyond normal reproductive transitions, reproductive disorders such as infertility, primary ovarian insufficiency, polycystic ovary syndrome, and endometriosis are linked to substantially elevated risks of late-life morbidities and premature mortality. These findings underscore how reproductive health across the lifespan fundamentally determines susceptibility to chronic disease and aging trajectories.

title: Endometrial Carcinoma and Associated Secondary Neoplasia: The Role of Clinical Features, Pathology, and Comorbidities in a University-Affiliated Clinical Center from Western Romania
summary:
# Summary

This clinical study examines endometrial carcinoma (EC) and its associated secondary malignancies, with particular emphasis on metabolic and hormonal risk factors. Obesity, type 2 diabetes, and hypertension—key components of metabolic syndrome—represent major EC risk factors, operating primarily through elevated estrogen levels and altered insulin signaling. Adipose tissue produces excess estrogen from androgens, while hyperinsulinemia simultaneously promotes endometrial cell proliferation and inhibits apoptosis, collectively driving estrogen-dependent carcinogenesis. The study identified endometrioid adenocarcinoma as the predominant EC histotype and documented frequent synchronous or secondary malignancies, including invasive ductal breast carcinoma, colorectal adenocarcinoma, and ovarian tumors, sharing common metabolic pathways that facilitate their development. Lynch syndrome and DNA mismatch repair gene mutations emerged as significant hereditary risk factors, particularly in cases with concurrent colorectal cancer, while protective factors including regular physical activity and reduced glycemic load may mitigate EC risk.

title: Organ-on-a-Chip Models of the Female Reproductive System: Current Progress and Future Perspectives
summary:
# Summary

Organ-on-a-chip (OOC) and microphysiological systems have emerged as transformative platforms for modeling the female reproductive system, overcoming significant limitations of traditional 2D cell cultures and animal models that fail to recapitulate complex tissue architecture and disease progression patterns such as endometriosis. These advanced systems enable precise simulation of hormonal regulation—including estrogen and progesterone signaling that controls the menstrual cycle—alongside embryo-endometrium interactions and follicular development under dynamic conditions. Specific applications include ovarian chips that simulate follicular maturation and steroidogenesis, endometrial chips that recapitulate morphological features and menstrual cycling, and fallopian tube chips that investigate ovum transport and ciliary function through periodic fluid flow. Material selection is critical, as poly(dimethylsiloxane) absorbs steroid hormones limiting reproductive studies, whereas hydrogel-based systems and decellularized extracellular matrix scaffolds better maintain cell viability and hormone bioavailability. Multi-organ-chip technology further advances understanding by enabling simultaneous study of interconnected organs and their coordinated physiological responses, while tumor-on-chip models facilitate personalized drug screening for gynecologic cancers including epithelial ovarian cancer and endometriosis-related complications.

title: Case Report: E2 luteal phase priming in a stop GnRH agonist combined with GnRH antagonist using a delta follitropin protocol in a poor responder: clinical case
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a novel assisted reproduction protocol combining luteal phase estradiol priming, GnRH agonist discontinuation, and follitropin delta administration in a poor-responder patient with endometriosis presenting with dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and low back pain. Estradiol plays multiple critical roles in this approach: it suppresses FSH secretion through negative feedback on the pituitary, prevents premature FSH surges during the luteal-follicular transition, and enhances granulosa cell receptivity and FSH receptor expression. The combined strategy leverages GnRH agonists' ability to downregulate pituitary receptors and suppress gonadotropin release while preventing premature LH surges and luteinization, and incorporates follitropin delta, which demonstrates superior ovarian response compared to standard follitropin alfa through enhanced systemic exposure and improved ovarian receptor binding. The integrated protocol successfully improved ovarian response and follicular recruitment in this poor-responder case, suggesting that synchronized estradiol priming with optimized gonadotropin stimulation may enhance outcomes in patients with endometriosis who traditionally exhibit reduced ovarian responsiveness to standard protocols.

title: Molecular pathways affecting reproductive efficiency in seasonal breeders: prospects and implications for improving fertility in donkeys
summary:
# Summary

The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis regulates reproductive functions in seasonal breeders like donkeys, primarily controlled by melatonin signaling that responds to photoperiod changes. Decreasing melatonin during long-day periods activates the HPG axis through effects on KNDy neurons and GnRH secretion, ultimately stimulating gonadotropin release and reproductive activity, while sustained high melatonin during short days suppresses this cascade. Multiple stressors disrupt fertility through distinct mechanisms: oxidative stress and endocrine-disrupting chemicals inhibit steroid synthesis and gonadal function; chronic stress elevates cortisol and reduces gonadal hormone production and spermatogenesis; and nutritional insufficiency triggers AMPK-mediated suppression of GnRH through low leptin signaling. The article proposes that reproductive success in donkeys can be improved through integrated interventions combining photoperiod manipulation, melatonin and antioxidant treatments, and nutritional supplementation to counteract these inhibitory pathways.

title: Metabolic impact of endogenously produced estrogens by adipose tissue in females and males across the lifespan
summary:
# Summary

White adipose tissue (WAT) serves as both a major metabolic regulator and an endocrine organ that synthesizes estrogens through the enzyme aromatase, which converts circulating androgens to estrone and estradiol. During reproductive years, gonadal estradiol prevents visceral adiposity accumulation and promotes protective subcutaneous fat deposition in the hips and thighs, while post-menopausal women experience a metabolic shift where WAT becomes the predominant source of estrogen production as circulating estrogen levels decline. This hormonal transition triggers fat redistribution from subcutaneous to visceral depots and increases metabolic risk through multiple mechanisms: decreased adiponectin and increased pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-6), which collectively impair insulin signaling and hepatic function. Obesity further exacerbates dysfunction through hypertrophic adipocyte expansion, macrophage infiltration, and hypoxia-induced stress, leading to excessive free fatty acid release that interferes with insulin signaling pathways. Together, these age- and obesity-related changes in WAT physiology explain why post-menopausal women face a five-fold increased diabetes risk compared to pre-menopausal women, with estrogen replacement therapy potentially providing protective metabolic benefits.

title: Hormonal Contraceptive Formulations and Breast Cancer Risk in Adolescents and Premenopausal Women
summary:
# Summary

This large-scale study examined breast cancer risk associated with various hormonal contraceptive formulations in adolescents and premenopausal women, revealing important differences based on progestin type, dose, and formulation. Overall hormonal contraceptive use was associated with increased breast cancer risk (HR 1.24), with progestin-only formulations showing higher risk than combined formulations (HR 1.21 vs. 1.12). Desogestrel-containing products, particularly progestin-only formulations and low-dose estrogen combinations, demonstrated the highest breast cancer risk, while levonorgestrel-based methods and drospirenone showed lower risk profiles. The increased risk appears mechanistically related to progestins stimulating breast cancer cell proliferation through progesterone receptors, with desogestrel exhibiting higher progesterone receptor affinity but markedly lower androgen receptor binding compared to other progestins; androgen receptor signaling provides protective antiproliferative and proapoptotic effects. Risk escalated progressively with duration of use, from 11% increased risk with less than one year of use to 34% increased risk with 5-10 years of use. These findings suggest that while hormonal contraceptives remain valuable for preventing unintended pregnancies and reducing ovarian and endometrial cancer risk, formulation choice should consider individual breast cancer risk profiles.

title: Unexpected estradiol decline during ovarian stimulation monitoring affects cumulative live birth
summary:
# Summary

Unexpected declines in estradiol (E2) during controlled ovarian stimulation (COS) significantly impair reproductive outcomes in assisted reproductive technology. Estradiol, produced by granulosa cells in response to follicle-stimulating hormone, is essential for follicular development, and its decline during monitoring—particularly between days 8-13 of stimulation—is associated with poorer ovarian response, reduced oocyte and embryo yields, and diminished cumulative live birth rates. The adverse effects of E2 decline on reproductive success are primarily mediated through decreased embryo availability, with mature oocyte yield and embryo number mediating 75-78% and 72-76% of the cumulative live birth decline, respectively. Notably, increasing gonadotropin dosage does not effectively reverse the negative outcomes associated with E2 decline, and the relationship between E2 decline magnitude and cumulative live birth shows a U-shaped dose-response pattern. Patients experiencing E2 decline tend to be older, have higher BMI, lower antral follicle counts, and greater prevalence of polycystic ovary syndrome, while agonist protocols demonstrate superior cumulative live birth rates compared to antagonist protocols when E2 decline occurs. These findings establish unexpected E2 decline during COS monitoring as a robust biomarker for compromised treatment outcomes despite compensatory management strategies.

title: Potential of growth factors and steroid hormones from cell cultures to improve in vitro fertilization in bovine
summary:
# Summary

This bovine in vitro fertilization study demonstrates that insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) and estrogen are critical regulators of reproductive function, acting synergistically to enhance fertilization outcomes. IGF-1, produced by the liver and ovaries in response to growth hormone stimulation, exerts its effects through the IGF1 receptor, activating the PI3K-Akt and MAPK/ERK signaling pathways to promote protein synthesis, energy metabolism, and inhibit apoptosis in developing oocytes. The growth hormone-IGF-1 axis operates through a well-characterized regulatory cascade in which growth hormone-releasing hormone stimulates growth hormone secretion from the anterior pituitary, triggering hepatic IGF-1 production via JAK/STAT pathway activation and subsequent binding of STAT5 to the IGF-1 gene promoter. In culture conditions, IGF-1 enhances cumulus cell growth, oocyte nuclear maturation, and embryo cleavage rates while preventing embryo death, whereas estrogen and progesterone support granulosa cell health and reproductive development. Complementary growth factors such as epidermal growth factor further optimize outcomes by activating MAPK/ERK pathways and promoting cumulus expansion and oocyte nuclear maturation. These findings establish that strategic supplementation with growth factors and steroid hormones can significantly improve bovine embryo development in vitro by simultaneously activating multiple signaling cascades essential for successful reproduction.

title: Association of Regular and Weekend Warrior Physical Activity Patterns with Gynecologic Cancer Risk: A Cross-Sectional Study of NHANES 2007-2018
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study of NHANES data demonstrates that regular physical activity significantly reduces gynecologic cancer risk, particularly cervical and endometrial cancers, through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Regular activity patterns achieve reductions in cervical cancer risk through decreased HPV infection susceptibility and reduced adiposity, while weekend-only activity shows no protective benefit. The protective effects of physical activity operate via hormonal regulation—exercise reduces circulating free estrogens and progestogens while improving metabolic profiles—and via metabolic pathways including decreased obesity and insulin-like growth factor 1, both of which are implicated in ectopic endometrial cell growth and carcinogenic potential. A dose-response relationship was observed, with women achieving 51–1130 minutes of weekly activity and those in the highest physical activity tertile (≥72 MET-hours/week) experiencing the greatest risk reduction. Early-life vigorous activity additionally confers protection through reproductive timeline modifications, including delayed menarche and earlier menopause, which shorten lifetime menstrual exposure. These findings underscore physical activity as a modifiable risk factor for gynecologic malignancies, with effects mediated by hormonal, metabolic, and infectious disease pathways relevant to endometrial and ovarian pathology.

title: Establishment of an optimized chemotherapy-induced mouse model for premature ovarian failure: protocol and findings
summary: This study establishes an optimized chemotherapy-induced mouse model of premature ovarian failure (POF) using a combination of cyclophosphamide (CTX) and busulfan (Bu). Both chemotherapeutic agents induce POF through multiple mechanisms: CTX forms DNA crosslinks triggering apoptosis in rapidly dividing oocytes, while its metabolites excessively activate primordial follicles leading to atresia; Bu directly depletes primordial reserves through apoptosis in oocytes and granulosa cells, and both agents cause follicular destruction via oxidative damage. The optimal dosage of CTX 100 mg/kg plus Bu 20 mg/kg administered intraperitoneally reliably induced POF within three weeks, producing the characteristic endocrine signature of elevated FSH with suppressed anti-Müllerian hormone and estradiol, along with severe depletion of all follicle types and oocyte/granulosa cell degeneration. Importantly, this dose regimen maintained ovarian stromal viability while establishing a stable POF phenotype with no folliculogenesis or hormonal recovery observed, closely mimicking the clinical presentation of chemotherapy-induced POF in cancer survivors. The study identifies the CHEK2 pathway as a key regulator of oocyte elimination, suggesting that selective pathway inhibition may preserve fertility, while establishing a reliable preclinical model for evaluating interventions against chemotherapy-induced reproductive toxicity.

title: Premature Ovarian Insufficiency and Diminished Ovarian Reserve: From Diagnosis to Current Management and Treatment
summary:
# Summary

This article examines premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) and diminished ovarian reserve (DOR), conditions characterized by depletion of the primordial follicle pool, with anti-Müllerian hormone serving as a key diagnostic marker of this depletion. Multiple etiologies contribute to these conditions, including iatrogenic causes such as chemotherapy (particularly alkylating agents) and pelvic radiotherapy that directly damage ovarian tissue, as well as endometrioma surgery which ablates healthy ovarian tissue. Genetic factors including FMR1 mutations, BRCA1/2 mutations, Turner syndrome, and polymorphisms in follicle development genes (BMP15, GDF9) significantly impact ovarian reserve, while autoimmune conditions and viral infections (mumps, coxsackie B) can trigger oophoritis and subsequent POI. Environmental exposures including smoking, endocrine-disrupting chemicals (bisphenol A, phthalates, vinclozolin), and estrogen receptor-binding compounds further compromise ovarian function. Management strategies include hormone replacement therapy to mitigate consequences of estrogen deficiency such as hot flushes, bone loss, and cardiovascular disease, while fertility preservation options such as oocyte and ovarian tissue cryopreservation offer reproductive alternatives for affected women.

title: Vitamin D Receptor Polymorphisms and Immunological Effects of Vitamin D in Hashimoto’s Thyroiditis
summary: Vitamin D exerts critical immunomodulatory effects by activating the vitamin D receptor (VDR), a nuclear transcription factor that regulates genes controlling immune cell function, apoptosis, and proliferation across T lymphocytes, B lymphocytes, and dendritic cells. Vitamin D deficiency is strongly associated with autoimmune thyroid diseases, particularly Hashimoto's thyroiditis, which is characterized by diffuse lymphocytic infiltration, thyroid tissue destruction, and elevated anti-thyroid peroxidase and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies that correlate with disease severity and hypothyroidism progression. Low vitamin D levels directly correlate with increased thyroid-stimulating hormone levels and heightened autoimmune thyroid antibody titers, while genetic variations in VDR polymorphisms—including the TaqI polymorphism and BB genotype—influence VDR expression levels, cytokine secretion patterns, and the effectiveness of vitamin D supplementation in managing the condition. The active form of vitamin D regulates calcium and phosphorus homeostasis while simultaneously modulating immune tolerance, and VDR polymorphisms can affect interferon-gamma production and overall immune responses, suggesting that genetic predisposition combined with vitamin D deficiency creates a permissive environment for autoimmune thyroid disease development. These findings underscore vitamin D's dual roles in skeletal health and immune regulation, establishing vitamin D supplementation as a potentially important therapeutic consideration in Hashimoto's thyroiditis management, particularly when guided by individual VDR genotype status.

title: The Evolution of In Vitro Fertilization Practices in Saudi Arabia: Historical Perspectives and Future Directions
summary: In vitro fertilization (IVF) has become an effective treatment for multiple infertility conditions including endometriosis, male factor infertility, blocked or damaged fallopian tubes, and unexplained infertility. The IVF process involves ovarian stimulation with fertility medications to promote multiple egg development, followed by egg retrieval and fertilization via conventional insemination or intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI), with progesterone subsequently supporting embryo implantation. Success of IVF cycles depends on multiple factors including maternal age—with success rates declining notably after age 40—as well as endometrium quality and embryo quality. While IVF offers substantial benefits, it carries significant risks including ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome, multiple pregnancies, ectopic pregnancy, miscarriage, infections, and bleeding. The article traces the historical development of IVF protocols, including the discovery that specific nutrients (pyruvate, lactate, and glucose) are required for embryo development at different stages. Modern refinements such as preimplantation genetic testing now allow prevention of genetic abnormalities, representing a major advancement in assisted reproductive technology.

title: Management of Obesity in Menopausal Women: Implications for Metabolic Health and Minimally Invasive Surgery
summary:
# Summary

Menopause triggers a cascade of metabolic dysfunction centered on declining estrogen, which reduces resting energy expenditure, increases appetite, and promotes central fat accumulation while diminishing lean muscle mass. This hormonal shift creates a vicious cycle: excess adiposity perpetuates estrogen production through adipose tissue aromatization, yet simultaneously generates proinflammatory cytokines and dysregulates appetite-suppressing hormones like leptin, accelerating insulin resistance and elevating risks for type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and hormone-sensitive malignancies. Obesity in menopausal women significantly complicates minimally invasive surgical interventions by increasing technical difficulty, operative time, and postoperative complications including venous thromboembolism and infection, though robotic surgery may partially mitigate these challenges. Management strategies span lifestyle modifications—including Mediterranean diet, exercise to reduce visceral adiposity, and adequate protein intake—alongside pharmacological approaches including menopausal hormone therapy with transdermal estrogen formulations and emerging GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide, tirzepatide) that demonstrate superior efficacy in weight reduction and metabolic parameter improvement compared to traditional agents. Bariatric surgery remains the most effective long-term intervention for severe obesity, representing an important consideration for women requiring gynecological surgical procedures.

title: Age, lifestyle, and disease affect ciliary transport in the human fallopian tube
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that ciliary beat frequency (CBF) in the human fallopian tube is a critical determinant of oocyte and embryo transport, but is significantly modulated by multiple age-related, lifestyle, and disease factors. Age exhibits a biphasic effect on CBF, with a decline in women under 45 years followed by an increase around age 45 and subsequent plateau, likely driven by changing oestradiol levels and progesterone-to-oestrogen ratios. Lifestyle factors negatively impact tubal function: elevated body mass index and obesity reduce CBF through leptin-mediated mechanisms and impaired endocrine signaling, while smoking paradoxically increases CBF yet still impairs oocyte pickup and increases ectopic pregnancy risk through additional mechanisms affecting smooth muscle contraction and cilia numbers. Hormonal regulation of CBF involves both direct effects—where progesterone inhibits CBF by 40-50% while oestradiol enhances it by 25%—and indirect effects through cytokines (interleukin-6, prostaglandins, angiotensin-II) and elevated testosterone in polycystic ovarian syndrome. Disease conditions including tubal endometriosis, ovarian cysts, uterine myomas, and certain malignancies significantly reduce CBF, while ovarian hormone-dependent ciliary formation and function are essential for tubal transport efficiency. These findings highlight that ciliary dysfunction represents a multifactorial reproductive pathology affecting fertility outcomes.

title: Synaptic mitochondrial respiration differs between the prefrontal cortex and hippocampus in female, but not male, mice: no effect of chronic stress history
summary: This study reveals sex-specific differences in synaptic mitochondrial respiration between brain regions that persist despite chronic stress exposure. Chronic repeated predation stress induced anxiety-like behavior and hyperactivity in both male and female mice, yet did not alter the baseline sex differences in mitochondrial respiration patterns. The prefrontal cortex exhibited higher synaptic mitochondrial respiration rates than the hippocampus in females across multiple measures (basal respiration, maximal respiration, ATP production, and spare capacity), while this regional distinction was absent in males. Females displayed elevated hippocampal expression of estrogen receptor beta (ESR2) and uncoupling protein 2 (UCP2) compared to males, and chronic stress further upregulated hippocampal ESR2 in both sexes, potentially serving a neuroprotective role. These findings suggest that estrogen-mediated regulation of mitochondrial dynamics and energy metabolism creates inherent sex differences in brain bioenergetics that are resistant to stress-induced changes, highlighting the importance of considering biological sex in understanding stress-related neurobiological outcomes.

title: Breastfeeding was associated with lower risks of maternal postpartum hypertension and diabetes mellitus: A systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that breastfeeding substantially reduces maternal postpartum risk of both type 2 diabetes and hypertension, with the protective effect increasing with duration—particularly at six months or longer. Lactation improves glucose metabolism, reduces insulin resistance, and lowers HbA1c levels, while also improving lipid profiles and reducing hypercholesterolemia, collectively supporting long-term cardiovascular health. The protective mechanisms involve hormonal changes during lactation, including alterations in oxytocin, prolactin, cortisol, estrogen, and progesterone, which influence blood pressure regulation and glucose homeostasis. Additionally, lactation appears to reverse metabolic adaptations from pregnancy and enhance insulin sensitivity, counteracting pathways involving the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, oxidative stress, and inflammation that contribute to diabetes and hypertension. Importantly, women with gestational diabetes mellitus experience significant benefits from breastfeeding, with even modest durations showing meaningful risk reduction. The findings underscore breastfeeding as a cost-effective maternal health intervention with evidence-based protective effects against postpartum metabolic and cardiovascular complications.

title: Investigation of the mechanism of Yijing Decoction in the treatment of premature ovarian insufficiency: A network pharmacology study
summary:
# Summary

Premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) is a major consequence of chemotherapy-induced gonadal toxicity in young cancer patients, with alkylating agents such as cyclophosphamide and busulfan causing follicle depletion by inhibiting primordial follicle proliferation and inducing oocyte loss. Network pharmacology analysis reveals that the traditional Chinese medicine formula Yijing Decoction ameliorates POI through multiple interconnected mechanisms: upregulating reproductive hormones (follicle-stimulating hormone, luteinizing hormone, estradiol, and anti-Müllerian hormone), increasing primordial, primary, and secondary follicle counts, and improving embryo implantation rates. The therapeutic action operates primarily through activation of the CDK1–PCNA–CCND1 axis and associated cell cycle regulatory proteins (CDK4, E2F1, CD34), which promote ovarian granulosa cell proliferation while simultaneously inhibiting apoptosis and promoting angiogenesis to sustain ovarian function.
