Notably, treatment with Yijing Decoction exhibits lower toxicity and fewer adverse effects compared to conventional therapies, positioning this herbal approach as a promising adjunctive strategy for managing chemotherapy-induced ovarian damage.

title: A role of the uterine cervix in the pathogenesis of adenomyosis and endometriosis?
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Summary

This article proposes that the uterine cervix plays a critical role in the pathogenesis of both adenomyosis and endometriosis through mechanical and microbiological mechanisms. Anatomical factors including excessive uterine flexion and internal cervical os stiffness obstruct menstrual outflow, intensifying uterine contractions that damage the endometrial-myometrial junction and promote either intrauterine invasion (adenomyosis) or retrograde flow into the peritoneal cavity (endometriosis). Hormonally, elevated local estrogen in adenomyotic tissue reduces cervical mucus barrier function by decreasing viscosity and immune cell content while increasing mucin pore diameter, facilitating ascent of vaginal pathogens and the establishment of dysbiotic, non-lactobacilli-dominated microbiota. This disrupted cervical barrier allows bacterial colonization of the endometrium, leading to chronic endometritis and compromised fertility through both increased infection rates and structural damage such as fallopian tube closure. The cervix thus emerges as a central anatomical and immunological hub linking mechanical menstrual obstruction, hormonal dysregulation, and microbial dysbiosis to adenomyosis and endometriosis development.

title: Hormonal imbalance in umbilical vein cord blood of pregnant women with endometriosis: a propensity score-matched analysis
summary:
# Summary

This propensity score-matched study reveals that pregnant women with endometriosis have significantly altered fetal hormonal exposure, characterized by elevated estradiol and reduced androgens in umbilical cord blood of female newborns. Female offspring of women with endometriosis showed markedly higher estradiol-to-testosterone, estradiol-to-androstenedione, estradiol-to-DHEA, and estradiol-to-DHEAS ratios compared to controls, establishing a high-estrogen, low-androgen intrauterine environment. The elevated in utero estradiol enhances placental 11β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase 2 (11β-HSD2) activity, leading to increased cortisol inactivation and 11-deoxycortisol accumulation, with the latter correlating with newborn anthropometric measures. These findings support the fetal programming hypothesis for endometriosis, suggesting that altered hormonal exposure in utero—analogous to documented effects of prenatal diethylstilbestrol exposure—may increase daughters' susceptibility to developing endometriosis later in life through epigenetic mechanisms and reproductive axis dysregulation. The study provides the first direct evidence that daughters of women with endometriosis experience heightened prenatal estrogenic imprinting, offering a mechanistic explanation for the observed intergenerational transmission of endometriosis risk.

title: Neuroendocrine–Immune Axis in Endometriosis: A Review on How the Nervous System Goes Beyond Pain Perception
summary:
# Neuroendocrine–Immune Axis in Endometriosis: Key Findings

Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent disorder characterized by the presence of endometrial tissue outside the uterus, whose pathophysiology is fundamentally shaped by dysregulation of the neuroendocrine–immune axis. Psychological stress activates this imbalance, leading to altered hormone secretion and subsequently increased pelvic pain and infertility, while chronically elevated stress levels promote inflammatory complications. The nervous system contributes beyond pain perception through multiple mechanisms: increased nerve fiber density in ectopic lesions correlates with dysmenorrhea severity, while neuropeptides such as substance P, neuropeptide Y, and vasoactive intestinal peptide are elevated in affected endometrium and promote neurogenic inflammation via TRPV1-positive neurons and inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-1β). Hormone therapy reduces nerve fiber density and symptom severity, while emerging therapeutic targets include growth hormone-releasing hormone antagonists and somatostatin analogs, which inhibit ectopic cell proliferation and migration through multiple pathways. Brain-derived neurotrophic factor, elevated in both plasma and eutopic endometrium of patients and promoted by estrogen, contributes to increased pain risk, while interventions such as GnRH agonists, progestins, and melatonin reduce its signaling to provide symptom relief.

title: Bidirectional Mendelian Randomization Analysis of the Causal Relationship Between Uterine Fibroids and Breast Cancer in East Asian Women
summary: This bidirectional Mendelian randomization study reveals complex causal relationships between uterine fibroids and breast cancer in East Asian women, mediated primarily through estrogen and progesterone signaling pathways. Both conditions share elevated lifetime estrogen exposure as a central risk factor, influenced by early menarche, late menopause, hormone replacement therapy, and obesity. Genetic analysis identified estrogen receptor genes (ESR1) and follicle-stimulating hormone genes (FSHB) as key contributors, with the SNP rs6557160 showing significant associations with estrogen and progesterone levels. The study demonstrated bidirectional causality: genetically predicted uterine fibroids showed a suggestive causal effect on breast cancer risk (OR 1.33-1.50), while genetically predicted breast cancer exhibited a stronger causal association with uterine fibroids risk (OR 1.19-2.16). Additional pathways including WNT signaling and fibroblast growth factor receptor 2 signaling were implicated in cancer cell proliferation and metastasis, while shared environmental and genetic risk factors such as race, obesity, and exposure to endocrine-disrupting chemicals further explain the observed associations between these hormone-dependent diseases.

title: Personalized Low-Invasive Approach to Chronic Endometritis Evaluation in Premenopausal Women: Machine Learning-Based Modeling
summary:
# Summary

This study develops machine learning models to identify chronic endometritis (CE) in premenopausal women using non-invasive biomarkers and clinical parameters. Chronic endometritis is strongly associated with recurrent implantation failure, recurrent pregnancy loss, and unexplained infertility, with CD138 immunohistochemistry serving as the diagnostic gold standard. The research identifies independent risk factors for CE including prolonged menstrual bleeding, previous abortions, fallopian tube obstruction, endometrial polyps, and uterine fibroids. Multiple predictive models reveal that CE risk involves dysregulation of hormonal and inflammatory markers, including low testosterone, reduced leptin-to-adiponectin ratio, elevated IL-1 with low TNFα, and abnormal estradiol and FSH levels. The final simplified model incorporating elevated sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG), low FSH, reduced C-reactive protein and leptin-to-adiponectin ratio, and increased endometrial thickness provides a practical non-invasive approach for CE assessment. These findings underscore the complex interplay between sex hormones, metabolic factors, and immune dysregulation in CE pathogenesis and suggest personalized diagnostic algorithms may improve detection of this condition in women with fertility problems.

title: Combined Oral Contraceptives and the Risk of Thrombosis
summary: Combined oral contraceptives substantially increase venous and arterial thrombosis risk through multiple prothrombotic mechanisms. The estrogen component elevates synthesis of several clotting factors, including fibrinogen, prothrombin, factors VII, VIII, and X, while simultaneously decreasing natural anticoagulants such as antithrombin, protein S, and tissue factor pathway inhibitor. This altered coagulation balance is compounded by reduced endogenous fibrinolysis, acquired resistance to activated protein C, increased platelet adhesiveness and aggregation, and elevated plasminogen activator inhibitor-1 levels. Additionally, combined oral contraceptives reduce nitric oxide bioavailability through homocysteine accumulation caused by decreased folate and vitamin B-12 reabsorption, promote inflammatory responses marked by elevated C-reactive protein and pro-inflammatory cytokines, and induce metabolic changes including insulin resistance and water retention. Third-generation progestins containing desogestrel, along with drospirenone, dienogest, cyproterone acetate, and norgestimate, produce particularly pronounced prothrombotic alterations. The cumulative effect of these coagulation, fibrinolytic, endothelial, and inflammatory disturbances explains the three- to seven-fold increased thrombosis risk associated with oral contraceptive use.

title: Menopausal Hormone Therapy—Risks, Benefits and Emerging Options: A Narrative Review
summary: Menopausal hormone therapy (MHT) is the most effective treatment for moderate-to-severe vasomotor symptoms and genitourinary syndrome of menopause, while also preventing postmenopausal bone loss through estrogen's suppression of osteoclastic activity. Estrogen deficiency in menopause triggers multiple systemic effects, including vasomotor symptoms, vaginal atrophy, bone loss, central adiposity, and insulin resistance, all of which MHT can effectively counteract. The cardiovascular and thrombotic safety profile of MHT is highly dependent on formulation and route of administration—oral conjugated equine estrogens carry substantially higher risks of venous thromboembolism and stroke, whereas transdermal 17β-estradiol at doses ≤50 μg/day combined with micronized progesterone offers a more favorable safety profile. Early MHT initiation within ten years of menopause is associated with reduced all-cause mortality and cardiovascular events, with improved endothelial function and cardiometabolic outcomes. Beyond vasomotor and bone benefits, MHT provides additional clinical value by improving sleep quality, reducing depressive symptoms, and enhancing sexual function. Emerging options such as estetrol (E4) demonstrate promising anti-vasomotor efficacy with favorable pharmacology, potentially expanding treatment choices beyond conventional hormone regimens.

title: Hormonal Crosstalk in Melasma: Unraveling the Dual Roles of Estrogen and Progesterone in Melanogenesis
summary:
# Summary

This article elucidates the hormonal regulation of melanogenesis and melasma pathogenesis through multiple estrogen and progesterone signaling pathways. Estradiol, estrone, and progesterone upregulate key melanogenic enzymes (tyrosinase, TRP-1, TRP-2) and activate intracellular signaling cascades including cAMP/PKA and MAPK pathways, while also promoting melanocyte proliferation and migration at physiological concentrations. The estrogen receptors (ER-α, ER-β) and G-protein-coupled estrogen receptor (GPER) are significantly elevated in melasma lesions, facilitating enhanced hormonal responsiveness of affected melanocytes. In contrast, estriol exhibits protective effects by upregulating antioxidant enzymes (superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase) and suppressing reactive oxygen species production, which otherwise trigger melanogenesis. Epidemiological findings demonstrate that melasma prevalence markedly increases during pregnancy (14.5–56%) and oral contraceptive use (11.3–46%), underscoring the critical role of estrogen and progesterone in disease development and exacerbation alongside environmental UV exposure.

title: Mapping the Nitric Oxide Axis in IVF: Genotype Associations in Antagonist Cycles
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how genetic variation in the eNOS gene (NOS3 rs1799983 polymorphism) influences nitric oxide signaling and reproductive outcomes during IVF with gonadotropin antagonist stimulation. Endothelial nitric oxide synthase regulates critical ovarian processes including steroidogenesis, vascular tone, and redox signaling by producing nitric oxide, which activates soluble guanylyl cyclase to increase cGMP levels and modulate kinase cascades (PI3K/Akt, PKG) that control follicular growth and hormone production. The common Glu298Asp polymorphism showed an allele-dose association with baseline estradiol levels (GG < GT < TT genotypes) and β-hCG concentrations in follicular fluid, with heterozygous GT carriers displaying substantially elevated β-hCG, suggesting enhanced peri-ovulatory luteinization efficiency. Mechanistically, the Asp allele can promote eNOS uncoupling under inflammatory conditions (elevated cytokines and ROS), generating superoxide instead of nitric oxide and leading to nitrosative stress that impairs granulosa cell function and steroidogenesis. These findings highlight the nitric oxide axis as a critical modulator of ovarian endocrine activity during controlled stimulation, with genetic polymorphisms potentially explaining variable responses to gonadotropin therapy among IVF patients.

title: Cryptotanshinone Suppresses BVDV Propagation by Suppressing Cell Apoptosis and Restoring Hormone Secretion in Bovine Granulosa Cells
summary: Bovine viral diarrhea virus (BVDV) causes severe reproductive dysfunction in cattle by inducing granulosa cell apoptosis, arresting follicular development, suppressing estradiol and progesterone secretion, and provoking ovarian inflammation. Cryptotanshinone (CRY), a diterpenoid compound from Salvia miltiorrhiza, inhibits BVDV replication through multiple mechanisms including suppression of viral adsorption and internalization, as well as reduction of viral mRNA and protein expression in bovine ovarian granulosa cells. CRY protects granulosa cells from BVDV-induced apoptosis while simultaneously restoring hormone secretion by promoting both estradiol and progesterone production, thereby countering the virus-induced hormonal deficits. These protective effects align with CRY's established anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anti-apoptotic properties, which have previously demonstrated efficacy in treating other ovarian pathologies including polycystic ovary syndrome and premature ovarian failure. The compound acts through regulation of key enzymes in granulosa cells, including CYP19A1, which is essential for estrogen biosynthesis. These findings suggest CRY represents a promising therapeutic agent for mitigating BVDV-induced reproductive disorders in cattle through dual mechanisms of antiviral activity and hormonal restoration.

title: Advances in using biomaterials for repairing thin endometrium
summary:
# Summary

Thin endometrium represents a major clinical challenge in reproductive medicine, accounting for 15–25% of infertility cases and significantly reducing live birth rates by 30–50% in affected women. The condition is multifactorial, involving abnormal hormone levels, elevated inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1 and IL-6, cellular senescence, and pathological conditions including endometriosis and adhesions. While traditional treatments like high-dose estrogen, vasoactive drugs, and G-CSF provide limited efficacy, biomaterial-based approaches have emerged as promising therapeutic strategies by promoting endometrial regeneration and improving blood flow. Advanced biomaterial technologies, particularly those combining mesenchymal stem cells or decellularized scaffolds with carriers such as collagen and hyaluronic acid hydrogels, demonstrate superior outcomes by promoting cell proliferation, angiogenesis, and upregulating key growth factors including VEGF, IGF-1, and LIF while preventing fibrosis. Composite scaffolds such as hUCMSCs-loaded collagen and collagen-hyaluronic acid hydrogels, combined with endometrial stem cells and compounds like HSYA, have shown remarkable efficacy in increasing endometrial thickness, reducing fibrosis, and promoting myometrial regeneration in preclinical models. These biomaterial innovations represent a paradigm shift toward tissue engineering-based solutions for restoring endometrial function and fertility potential in women with thin endometrium.

title: Longitudinal Analysis of Estrogen Receptor Gene Methylation, Estradiol, and Depressive Symptoms During the Perinatal Period
summary:
# Summary

This longitudinal study reveals that DNA methylation of estrogen receptor genes—particularly ESR1, ESR2, and GPER—regulates estrogen receptor expression and significantly influences perinatal depression susceptibility. Lower ESR1 DNA methylation during pregnancy is associated with higher estrogen receptor alpha expression, increased estrogen sensitivity, and greater depressive symptoms, with women scoring ≥11 on the Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale showing substantially reduced ESR1 methylation. Estrogen modulates key neurotransmitters including serotonin, dopamine, GABA, and glutamate in limbic brain regions through these receptors, providing a mechanistic link between estrogen signaling and mood regulation. ESR1 methylation patterns change across the perinatal period, increasing from pregnancy to postpartum, with specific CpG sites showing dynamic associations with both estradiol levels and depressive symptoms. Genetic haplotypes in ESR1 modify the relationship between methylation status and depression, suggesting individual variation in susceptibility. These findings identify estrogen receptor alpha signaling and its epigenetic regulation as promising molecular targets for understanding and potentially treating perinatal depression.

title: Endometrial estrogen and progesterone receptor activity in unusual chronology of pubertal development: a case report
journal: Revista Brasileira de Ginecologia e Obstetrícia
summary: This case report describes an unusual presentation of isolated vaginal bleeding in a prepubertal girl without secondary sexual characteristics, attributed to endometrial hypersensitivity to low estrogen levels. Histopathological analysis revealed positive estrogen and progesterone receptors in the endometrium, demonstrating that the endometrium can respond to basal estrogen concentrations even in the absence of full hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis activation. The findings suggest that partial and transient activation of the hormonal axis may be sufficient to trigger endometrial proliferation and estrogen withdrawal-induced menstrual bleeding, as estrogen promotes progesterone receptor expression necessary for endometrial cycling. This case emphasizes that prepubertal vaginal bleeding should be considered a manifestation of endometrial responsiveness to minimal hormonal stimulation, distinct from typical menarche where complete pubertal development occurs. The study highlights the importance of endometrial biopsy via hysteroscopy for detecting receptor expression patterns in atypical presentations and suggests that isolated menarche may occur without negative impacts on final height or future fertility.

title: Position Statement on Cardiometabolic Health Across the Woman's Life Course – 2025
journal: Revista Brasileira de Ginecologia e Obstetrícia
summary: This position statement on cardiometabolic health across the female lifespan demonstrates that sexual hormones fundamentally regulate glucose and lipid metabolism, with estrogen exerting broad cardioprotective effects through multiple mechanisms including inhibition of inflammatory pathways, reduction of nuclear factor kappa B activity, and stimulation of endothelial nitric oxide production. Female reproductive events such as menopause, preeclampsia, and menstrual irregularities activate systemic inflammation and endothelial dysfunction, significantly elevating cardiovascular disease risk independent of traditional metabolic risk factors. Chronic low-intensity inflammation—triggered by obesity, insulin resistance, and dyslipidemia—drives heart failure pathogenesis, particularly heart failure with preserved ejection fraction in women, through visceral adipose tissue-derived adipokines that promote cardiac fibrosis and microcirculation inflammation. Preeclampsia and other pregnancy complications establish long-term metabolic dysfunction and increased heart failure risk that manifests decades after the acute event, highlighting the critical role of reproductive health in lifetime cardiovascular outcomes. The loss of estrogen's anti-inflammatory and vasoprotective effects during menopause, combined with the pro-inflammatory state of menstrual irregularity, underscores why women's cardiovascular disease trajectories diverge significantly from men's and require sex-specific prevention and management strategies.

title: Posicionamento sobre a Saúde Cardiometabólica ao Longo do Ciclo de Vida da Mulher – 2025
summary:
# Summary

This Brazilian cardiology position paper examines cardiometabolic health across women's lifespan, with particular emphasis on estrogen's cardioprotective mechanisms and life-stage-specific risk factors. Estrogen exerts potent anti-inflammatory and endothelial protective effects by inhibiting nuclear factor-kappa B, reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines like interleukin-6, stimulating nitric oxide production, and suppressing vascular adhesion molecules, thereby preventing atherosclerosis progression. Conversely, hypoestrogenism during menopause initiates metabolic dysfunction and endothelial impairment, while irregular menstrual cycles promote systemic inflammation that mediates atherosclerotic development. Beyond reproductive hormones, chronic low-grade inflammation from obesity, insulin resistance, diabetes, and dyslipidemia reduces nitric oxide production and triggers cardiac fibrosis through adipokine release, particularly contributing to heart failure with preserved ejection fraction in women. Additional threats include cancer therapy cardiotoxicity from anthracyclines and trastuzumab, autoimmune disease-related myocardial inflammation, and pregnancy complications like preeclampsia that activate sustained inflammatory cascades elevating long-term heart failure risk. These findings underscore inflammation as a central pathophysiological mechanism linking multiple female-specific and metabolic conditions to cardiovascular disease across the lifespan.

title: Interrelationship between the menstrual cycle and the phenomena associated with orthodontic tooth movement in young female patients: a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

Fluctuations in female sex hormones throughout the menstrual cycle significantly affect alveolar bone turnover and orthodontic tooth movement through estrogen-mediated regulation of osteoblast and osteoclast activity. During the menstrual phase when estrogen levels are low, osteoclast function dominates, increasing bone resorption and enabling faster orthodontic tooth movement, whereas high estrogen levels during ovulation promote osteoblast function and bone mass maintenance, decelerating tooth movement. Conversely, orthodontic pain intensity increases during the luteal phase when progesterone levels rise and estrogen levels decline, and this pain is significantly exacerbated in patients with primary dysmenorrhea, which shares common neurobiological pathways with orthodontic pain including calcitonin gene-related peptide signaling. Importantly, orthodontic treatment itself does not alter menstrual cycle length or bleeding duration long-term, though timing of force application can be strategically optimized to reduce patient discomfort and enhance treatment efficiency. These findings suggest that clinicians should consider menstrual cycle phase when scheduling orthodontic adjustments, particularly for patients experiencing severe dysmenorrhea, to minimize pain while potentially accelerating tooth movement during optimal hormonal windows.

title: Bidirectional crosstalk between sleep disorders and gynecological cancers: unraveling molecular synergies and precision therapeutics
summary:
# Summary

Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) and circadian disruption establish bidirectional links with gynecological malignancies through interconnected molecular mechanisms. Intermittent hypoxia from OSA stabilizes hypoxia-inducible factor-1α (HIF-1α), promoting tumor aggressiveness by enhancing angiogenesis, glycolytic metabolism, invasion, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition while suppressing apoptotic pathways through Bcl-2/Survivin upregulation. Circadian disruption impairs endocrine and immune regulation, dysregulates clock genes (PER1-3, BMAL1) that normally suppress carcinogenesis, and perturbs gut and vaginal microbiota, collectively creating a tumor-permissive environment linked to increased incidence and mortality of ovarian, endometrial, and cervical cancers. Female hormones—estrogen and progesterone—exert protective effects by stabilizing upper airway function, reducing ventilatory instability, and modulating HIF-1α signaling, while melatonin offers therapeutic potential through antitumor effects, apoptosis promotion, and circadian gene regulation. This sleep-cancer-endocrine axis reveals precision therapeutic opportunities targeting melatonin signaling, circadian restoration, and HIF-1α inhibition to interrupt the pathogenic crosstalk between sleep disorders and gynecological malignancy progression.

title: Physiology of Ovarian Development in Crustaceans: Interactions Among Hormones, Nutrients, and Environmental Factors From Integrated Perspectives
summary:
# Summary

Crustacean ovarian development is orchestrated by an integrated system of neuroendocrine factors, nutritional substrates, and environmental cues. The eyestalk neuropeptides crustacean hyperglycemic hormone (CHH) and molt-inhibiting hormone (MIH) promote vitellogenesis and oocyte maturation, while vitellogenesis-inhibiting hormone (VIH) suppresses these processes through the GC/cGMP-MAPK pathway and repression of vitellogenin expression. Beyond hormonal regulation, vitellogenesis and oocyte maturation require nutritional support from proteins, amino acids (especially arginine), long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids, cholesterol, and carotenoids, which operate through mTOR and steroidogenic signaling pathways. Environmental factors—including temperature, photoperiod, salinity, and thermal regimes—further modulate ovarian development and reproductive output. CHH and VIH function as metabolic counterbalances, with CHH mobilizing energy resources while VIH initiates vitellogenesis suppression during unfavorable reproductive conditions, creating a coordinated endocrine-metabolic framework that integrates hormonal signaling with physiological status and environmental context.

title: From Mice to Primates: Assessing Hormone-Based Endometriosis Models for Preclinical and Therapeutic Insights
summary: Endometriosis, a complex gynecological disorder affecting over 10% of women, causes chronic pain, pelvic discomfort, and infertility through multiple pathophysiological mechanisms including inflammation, hormonal dysregulation, immunological dysfunction, genetic factors, and retrograde menstruation. The condition is fundamentally dependent on estrogen and sex steroid hormones, which promote ectopic endometrial tissue adherence, invasion, and vascular establishment, while local estrogen production amplifies disease pathogenesis. Multiple developmental pathways contribute to endometriosis, including the classical retrograde menstruation theory and emerging stem cell origin mechanisms involving bone marrow and endometrial stem cells. Non-human primate models, particularly hormone-induced baboon models, demonstrate superior physiological resemblance to human disease and provide valuable platforms for evaluating sex steroid effects, though their use raises significant ethical concerns. Transgenic animal models and genetically modified approaches offer promising tools for examining specific gene functions relevant to endometriosis pathogenesis, complementing traditional hormone-induced models to advance preclinical research and therapeutic development. The field increasingly utilizes molecular markers including hormonal indicators (ER1, PR, CYP17A1) and genetic markers (BDNF, CCL2, MMP9, TIMP2, and others) to characterize endometriotic lesions and evaluate treatment responses across diverse experimental models.

title: Joint Modeling of Geometric Features of Longitudinal Process and Discrete Survival Time Measured on Nested Timescales: An Application to Fecundity Studies
summary: This study develops a joint modeling statistical approach to examine how reproductive hormone profiles predict fertility outcomes in women. Luteinizing hormone (LH) and estrogen, measured through ovulation kits during menstrual cycles, are central regulators of oocyte development and conception; abnormalities in LH surge—including reduced peak values and altered hormonal curve geometry—are strongly associated with infertility and impaired oocyte maturation. The research demonstrates that specific geometric features of the LH profile, particularly the peak value and curvature (sharpness) of the hormonal curve at its peak, are predictive of time-to-pregnancy outcomes. Beyond reproductive function, the study highlights that irregular menstrual cycles associate with systemic health risks including breast cancer, osteoporosis, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Using data from the Oxford Conception Study with women aged 18-40 years, the authors show that hormonal profile characteristics can effectively predict pregnancy timing when combined with behavioral factors like intercourse timing within the fertile window. This methodological advance enables both improved understanding of hormonal mechanisms underlying fertility and practical prediction of reproductive success.

title: Osteoporosis and Fracture Risk in Ovarian Cancer: Beyond the Oncologic Burden
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian cancer survivors face accelerated bone loss and increased fracture risk through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy causes abrupt estrogen deficiency, which normally inhibits osteoclast activation through the RANK/RANKL pathway by upregulating osteoprotegerin and inducing osteoclast apoptosis. Estrogen loss removes this protective effect while simultaneously increasing pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF and IL-1) that stimulate osteoclastogenesis, leading to rapid reduction in bone mineral density and trabecular microarchitecture deterioration. Additionally, tumor-derived factors including parathyroid hormone-related peptide and prostaglandin E2 enhance bone resorption, while chemotherapy (particularly platinum-based regimens) further exacerbates bone loss and reduces muscle mass and strength. Management strategies include antiresorptive agents such as bisphosphonates, which induce osteoclast apoptosis and suppress bone turnover, and denosumab, which blocks RANKL signaling, combined with calcium, vitamin D, and exercise. DXA screening is recommended for ovarian cancer survivors, particularly those with additional osteoporosis risk factors, as low bone mineral density is independently associated with increased mortality.

title: Assisted Reproduction Therapy in Patients with Multiple Sclerosis: Narrative Review and Practical Recommendations
summary:
# Summary: Assisted Reproduction Therapy in Patients with Multiple Sclerosis

Multiple sclerosis, an autoimmune disease predominantly affecting women, involves activation of autoreactive T-lymphocytes and B-lymphocytes that cross the blood-brain barrier after it is disrupted by pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-17, IFN-γ, and TNF-α. Women with MS face reduced fertility due to overlapping risk factors such as obesity, vitamin D deficiency, and endometriosis, combined with MS-specific complications including sexual dysfunction and the ovarian toxicity of immunosuppressive therapies like mitoxanthrone and cyclophosphamide. Hormonal fluctuations significantly modulate MS disease activity, with higher disease activity associated with lower anti-Müllerian hormone levels and reduced ovarian reserve, while pregnancy temporarily alleviates symptoms through a shift toward Th2-mediated immune responses but is followed by postpartum MS relapses. Women with MS consequently require assisted reproductive technology at higher rates than healthy individuals to overcome infertility, though the procedure itself does not exacerbate MS progression. Disease-modifying therapies including interferons, glatiramer acetate, and dimethyl fumarate manage MS activity while preserving reproductive potential, making coordinated care essential for optimizing both immunological control and fertility outcomes.

title: Medical and Social Characteristics of Patients with Endometrial Hyperplasia in a Large City in Kazakhstan: A Retrospective Comparative Study
summary: Endometrial hyperplasia is an excessive proliferation of uterine glands driven by estrogen dominance relative to progesterone, with rising prevalence linked to metabolic disorders including obesity, diabetes mellitus, and thyroid disease, particularly in postmenopausal women. While non-atypical endometrial hyperplasia carries less than 5% risk of progression to endometrial cancer over 20 years, atypical hyperplasia poses a substantially higher risk of up to 28% progression with concurrent carcinoma detected in up to 60% of cases. Diagnostic approaches have shifted from blind curettage toward hysteroscopy with biopsy, enabling more precise detection and characterization of hyperplastic lesions. Metabolically, alterations in lipid metabolism and redox-related processes occur at the hyperplasia stage and appear central to endometrial carcinogenesis. HER2 amplification and overexpression have emerged as important molecular markers in endometrial cancer, particularly in p53-abnormal tumors and serous subtypes, with HER2-targeted therapy using trastuzumab now established as a clinical treatment option. The increasing burden of metabolic disorders in urban populations appears to enhance estrogen-dependent endometrial stimulation, creating an unfavorable background for endometrial hyperplasia and malignant progression.

title: Uncontrolled diabetes may cause a transient decrease in ovarian reserve parameters to a suboptimal level: Recovery with optimized glycemic control - A case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report demonstrates that uncontrolled diabetes transiently suppresses ovarian reserve parameters through mechanisms affecting anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels and follicle counts, with both type 1 and type 2 diabetes strongly associated with diminished ovarian reserve in clinical and animal studies. The key finding is that a patient with type 1 diabetes experienced spuriously decreased AMH and antral follicle count values when glycemic control was poor, but these parameters recovered substantially when hemoglobin A1C was optimized to 6.0% using insulin lispro and insulin degludec therapy. The authors propose that poor glycemic control may reversibly suppress ovarian reserve testing results rather than causing permanent damage, suggesting that optimizing glycemic control prior to fertility assessment is critical in diabetic patients. Multiple other factors are also recognized as reducing ovarian reserve, including aging, endometriosis surgery, cancer treatment, and pelvic infections, as well as combined oral contraceptive use, which suppresses follicle-stimulating hormone and inhibits small follicle development. The report suggests vitamin D and moderate solar radiation exposure may have protective effects on ovarian function, highlighting the multifactorial nature of ovarian reserve regulation.

title: Emerging drug delivery approach using nanomaterials for the treatment of endometrial cancer
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial cancer is a hormone-dependent malignancy primarily driven by prolonged estrogen exposure and genetic mutations in PTEN, POLE, and TP53, with additional risk factors including obesity, diabetes, and hypertension. Type I endometrial cancer exhibits estrogen and progesterone receptor positivity and responds to hormone therapies, while Type II disease shows reduced estrogen association and requires alternative approaches. This article reviews emerging nanomaterial-based drug delivery systems that significantly enhance therapeutic efficacy against endometrial cancer, including paclitaxel-loaded PLGA nanoparticles, realgar quantum dots, and combination therapies that target multiple pathways such as PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling, glycolysis, and autophagy. Novel nanosystems leverage the enhanced permeability and retention effect alongside surface functionalization to improve tumor specificity and reduce systemic toxicity compared to conventional chemotherapy. These approaches are complementary to established treatments including hysterectomy, radiotherapy, hormone therapies, and immunotherapies with checkpoint inhibitors, offering promising strategies to improve outcomes in both early-stage and recurrent endometrial cancer.

title: Maternal serum steroid hormones in vaginal delivery and caesarean section
summary:
# Summary

Maternal serum steroid hormone concentrations are significantly associated with mode of delivery, with aldosterone and DHEAS emerging as the strongest independent predictors of vaginal delivery versus urgent caesarean section. Women requiring urgent caesarean delivery show substantially lower concentrations of multiple steroid hormones including aldosterone, DHEAS, progesterone, cortisol, and corticosterone compared to those delivering vaginally. Aldosterone plays a multifaceted role in facilitating labor through its effects on cervical remodeling, myometrial contractility, placental blood flow regulation, and plasma volume expansion. DHEAS supports vaginal delivery through its involvement in cervical remodeling and immune cell activation, mechanisms linked to increased interleukin-8 production and neutrophil chemotaxis that promote proteolytic enzyme activity. Together with progesterone, estrogen, DHEA, and DHT, these hormones coordinate labor onset, uterine contractility, and fetal-maternal nutrient exchange, with parity-dependent variations in aldosterone and SHBG concentrations also influencing delivery outcomes.

title: Association between hysterectomy, oophorectomy, and risk of breast cancer: a meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis demonstrates that both hysterectomy and bilateral oophorectomy significantly reduce breast cancer risk through estrogen suppression, with standalone hysterectomy reducing risk by 13% and bilateral oophorectomy by 19%. The protective effect of bilateral oophorectomy is mediated by decreased estrogen and progesterone levels, which normally stimulate breast cell proliferation and promote cancer development. Notably, the risk reduction from bilateral oophorectomy is more pronounced when coupled with hormone therapy (20% reduction) and in premenopausal women (13% reduction), while unilateral oophorectomy and postmenopausal bilateral oophorectomy show no significant protective effect. Although these gynecological procedures effectively treat conditions such as endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and ovarian tumors, the analysis reveals important tradeoffs: oophorectomy and hysterectomy substantially increase risks for osteoporosis and cardiovascular disease due to estrogen depletion. These findings highlight that the breast cancer protective benefits must be weighed against other systemic consequences of surgical estrogen reduction.

title: Targeting Androgen Receptor Signaling to Enhance Cancer Immunotherapy
summary: Androgen receptor signaling suppresses adaptive anticancer immunity through multiple mechanisms across innate and adaptive immune lineages, contributing to sex differences in cancer incidence and mortality. Androgens inhibit T cell development and cytotoxic function by reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-2, IFN-γ, TNF-α), promote regulatory T cell differentiation, and suppress B cell proliferation and antibody production, while also impairing macrophage and monocyte activity through increased anti-inflammatory IL-10 production. Single-cell transcriptomic studies demonstrate that androgen exposure reshapes immune composition by suppressing antigen presentation and reducing group 2 innate lymphoid cells, effects validated by evidence that men with higher testosterone levels mount lower antibody responses to influenza vaccination and experience higher COVID-19 and tuberculosis mortality compared to women. Pharmacologic AR inhibition enhances T cell function and sensitizes tumors to immunotherapy, though sustained suppression paradoxically leads to immunosuppressive remodeling of the tumor microenvironment, suggesting a critical window for therapeutic intervention. These findings reveal that sex-based differences in AR signaling fundamentally reshape immune competence, with implications for designing sex-informed immunotherapy strategies in cancer treatment.

title: Protective Effect of S-Methylcysteine Against Toxoplasma gondii-Induced Reproductive Toxicity in Female Albino Rats
summary: Toxoplasma gondii infection causes severe reproductive toxicity in female rats through hormonal disruption and inflammatory damage to reproductive tissues, including elevated estrogen and progesterone with suppressed follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH), along with catarrhal endometritis, endometrial hyperplasia, and follicular atresia. S-Methylcysteine (SMC), a sulfur-containing amino acid from garlic with potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, was evaluated as a protective agent against these toxoplasma-induced effects. Both SMC and spiramycin (a standard antiparasitic) individually reduced infection-related hormonal imbalances and partially mitigated uterine and ovarian pathology, restoring FSH and LH levels while decreasing excessive estrogen and progesterone. The combined treatment of SMC and spiramycin achieved the most significant protective effects, demonstrating near-complete restoration of normal hormonal profiles and ovarian histology with improved folliculogenesis and fully recovered endometrial tissue. These findings suggest that SMC supplementation, leveraging its multifaceted therapeutic properties including anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities, may serve as an adjunctive strategy to enhance reproductive health protection against parasitic-induced reproductive toxicity and hormonal dysregulation.

title: Therapeutic mechanisms of Zi Chong granules against hydroxyurea-induced diminished ovarian reserve based on integrated multi-omics analyses
summary: Hydroxyurea induces diminished ovarian reserve through multiple mechanisms including DNA damage, cell-cycle arrest, and p53-caspase-3-mediated apoptosis in granulosa cells and oocytes, while simultaneously disrupting the gut microbiome by reducing beneficial Lactobacillus species and critical metabolic pathways including steroidogenesis and tryptophan metabolism. Zi Chong granules, a traditional Chinese medicine, counteracts these effects by restoring gut microbiota composition and metabolite production, which in turn promotes ovarian recovery through multiple pathways: upregulating anti-Müllerian hormone and steroid hormone secretion, increasing primordial follicle numbers while reducing follicular atresia, and enhancing mature oocyte production. Beyond ovarian effects, the treatment also improves endometrial health by increasing thickness, glandular density, microvessel density, and reducing inflammatory markers including CD138 expression. The therapeutic mechanism fundamentally depends on restoring the gut-ovarian axis, as the microbiota-derived metabolites regulate both systemic inflammation and reproductive hormone bioavailability. This integrated multi-omics analysis reveals that Zi Chong granules represent a potential therapeutic approach for alleviating hydroxyurea-induced diminished ovarian reserve by simultaneously addressing ovarian dysfunction, endometrial insufficiency, and dysbiosis-driven metabolic disruption.

title: Primary Ovarian Leiomyoma with Torsion in a Postmenopausal Woman: A Rare Case with Progesterone Receptor Positivity
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare primary ovarian leiomyoma with progesterone receptor positivity in a postmenopausal woman, presenting with torsion. Immunohistochemical analysis revealed the tumor expressed strong progesterone receptors while lacking estrogen receptor expression, and demonstrated characteristic smooth muscle differentiation markers (smooth muscle actin and desmin). The presence of progesterone receptors on tumor cells suggests that even minimal circulating progesterone levels in postmenopausal women may stimulate tumor growth through mechanisms involving increased cell proliferation, reduced apoptosis, and growth factor production, particularly transforming growth factor-beta and extracellular matrix expansion. The case is notable because it demonstrates hormonal influences on leiomyoma development can persist after menopause, challenging the assumption that such tumors regress with declining ovarian hormone production. Additionally, ovarian leiomyomas show documented associations with other hormone-dependent conditions including endometriosis and ovarian fibromas, suggesting a common underlying hormonal pathophysiology that warrants further investigation.

title: Impact of night shift work on women's fertility, pregnancy and menopause
summary:
# Summary

Nocturnal and rotating shift work adversely impacts women's reproductive health through disruption of circadian rhythm regulation, a critical process required for hormonal synthesis, folliculogenesis, ovulation, and implantation. Shift work causes irregular sleep-wake cycles and circadian rhythm disturbances, which lead to hormonal imbalance and desynchronization across different endocrine systems, including reduced melatonin levels that normally enhance luteinizing hormone surges and support gonadotropin function. These circadian disruptions are associated with lower progesterone levels, irregular menstrual cycles, reduced ovarian follicles, and pregnancy failure, with night shift workers demonstrating 30-40% higher incidence of menstrual irregularities and endometriosis. Additionally, shift work substantially increases risks for adverse pregnancy outcomes including gestational diabetes (particularly with 75% increased odds for afternoon and night workers), hypertensive disorders of pregnancy, preterm birth, low birth weight, and spontaneous abortion, while reducing fecundability in a dose-dependent manner, particularly among women over 35 years old. Sleep timing variability acts as a key mediator between shift work exposure and adverse outcomes such as gestational diabetes, indicating that standardized sleep patterns may mitigate some reproductive health risks associated with shift work schedules.

title: Sleep in women: a narrative review of hormonal influences, sex differences and health implications
summary:
# Summary

Progesterone and estradiol exert opposing effects on sleep architecture in women across the menstrual cycle, mediated through their receptors in key sleep-wake regulatory brain regions including the hypothalamus, basal forebrain, dorsal raphe nucleus, and locus coeruleus. Progesterone enhances sleep duration and quality by promoting slow wave sleep and exerts hypnotic and sedative properties, yet paradoxically, rapid progesterone elevation during the early luteal phase increases wakefulness and reduces sleep efficiency. Estradiol facilitates wakefulness while reducing REM and non-REM sleep duration and modulates neurochemical transmission by increasing wake-promoting substances and decreasing sleep-promoting neurotransmitters, effects shared by progesterone. The luteal phase, characterized by elevated progesterone and moderate estrogen, is associated with excessive daytime sleepiness, difficulty initiating sleep, and increased wake time after sleep onset, whereas the follicular phase with lower hormone levels yields better subjective sleep quality and objective sleep metrics. Gynecological conditions including endometriosis, dysmenorrhea, and PCOS are associated with sleep disruption, as are hormonal fluctuations underlying premenstrual syndrome and the perimenopause, highlighting the critical role of ovarian hormones in regulating female sleep physiology.

title: Effects of voluntary wheel running on appetite-regulating peptides and neuroinflammation in the hypothalamus of ovariectomized middle-aged mice
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that estrogen deficiency following ovariectomy disrupts hypothalamic appetite regulation and promotes weight gain through dual mechanisms: reduced activation of anorexigenic neurons and increased neuroinflammation. Estrogen normally maintains energy homeostasis by activating satiety-promoting neurons while inhibiting hunger-promoting neurons, and additionally exerts neuroprotective effects by suppressing microglial activation and inflammatory signaling cascades including NLRP3, TLR4, and IL-1β. Voluntary wheel running effectively counteracts these estrogen-deficiency effects by upregulating estrogen receptor beta (ERβ) expression and appetite-regulating receptors (leptin receptor and cholecystokinin receptor), while simultaneously reducing hypothalamic neuroinflammatory markers including caspase-1 and IL-18. The exercise-induced modulation of microglial activation restores central appetite regulation and stabilizes body mass independent of ovarian status, highlighting physical activity as a therapeutic intervention for metabolic and neuroinflammatory dysfunction during menopause. These findings suggest that exercise-mediated restoration of hypothalamic signaling and reduced neuroinflammation may compensate for estrogen's loss of control over energy homeostasis and appetite regulation.

title: Inflammatory and Redox Mediators in Rat and Human Ovulation
summary:
# Summary: Inflammatory and Redox Mediators in Ovulation

The hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis coordinates ovulation through a complex hormonal cascade initiated by gonadotropin-releasing hormone, which stimulates follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone secretion to promote follicle development and maturation. The pre-ovulatory luteinizing hormone surge triggers both inflammatory and redox cascades within the follicle, characterized by controlled production of eicosanoids, reactive oxygen species, and pro-inflammatory cytokines (interleukin-1β, interleukin-6, tumor necrosis factor-α) from granulosa and theca cells. Luteinizing hormone stimulates matrix metalloproteinase release, which fragments the collagenous matrix and facilitates follicular rupture, while inflammatory mediators induce cumulus expansion and recruit infiltrating leukocytes that further amplify the inflammatory response through additional cytokine and eicosanoid production. The delicate balance between reactive oxygen species and antioxidants is fundamental to normal ovulation, as excessive exposure to antioxidants or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs can paradoxically interfere with the ovulatory inflammatory process. These findings demonstrate that physiologically moderate inflammation and redox reactions are essential components of successful ovulation in both rats and humans.

title: Independent Predictors of Ovarian Torsion Laterality: Nulliparity (Virgo) and Cyst Presence
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies nulliparity (VIRGO status) and ovarian cyst presence as independent predictors of ovarian torsion laterality, with both factors significantly reducing the probability of left-sided torsion and conversely increasing right-sided torsion risk. Anatomical differences explain the right ovary's higher susceptibility to torsion, as its longer meso-ovarium permits greater mobility compared to the left ovary, which is more firmly fixed by the sigmoid colon. While hormonal fluctuations theoretically may influence torsion likelihood, specific hormone levels including progesterone, estradiol, FSH, and LH showed no significant association with torsion laterality, likely because 95.3% of hormone measurements were obtained during the follicular phase when hormone levels are naturally low. Ovarian torsion causes ovarian ischemia and tissue necrosis, potentially resulting in loss of fertility if not rapidly diagnosed and treated; however, laparoscopic detorsion can successfully preserve ovarian function rather than requiring oophorectomy. These findings have important clinical implications for risk stratification in patients presenting with acute pelvic pain, particularly in nulliparous women without detectable cysts, who face substantially elevated risk of right-sided torsion.

title: Humanized Mouse Models as a Cellular Platform for Investigating Immune‐Hormonal Crosstalk and Therapeutic Strategies in Menopause
summary: Menopause is a complex biological transition characterized by declining ovarian function and estrogen levels, which triggers both physiological and immunological changes that contribute to multiple adverse health outcomes including hot flashes, mood disturbances, bone loss, cardiovascular dysfunction, and metabolic alterations. Traditional ovariectomized rodent models fail to adequately replicate the complexity of human immune-hormonal interactions during menopause, limiting their utility for understanding disease mechanisms. The authors propose humanized mouse models—which incorporate human immune systems and tissues—as a superior experimental platform for investigating estrogen signaling and immune interactions during menopausal transition. Estrogen plays critical roles in reproductive health, bone density maintenance, and cardiovascular function, and its depletion during menopause increases bone resorption and contributes to osteoporosis and autoimmune disorders. Multiple dietary compounds including phytoestrogens, polyphenols, omega-3 fatty acids, and probiotics demonstrate beneficial effects on menopausal symptoms and improve bone health, mood regulation, and cardiovascular function. This framework positions humanized mouse models as valuable tools for elucidating immune-hormonal crosstalk in menopause and developing more effective therapeutic strategies for hormone-related conditions including endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome.

title: Objective and comprehensive characterization of uterine peristaltic activity throughout the menstrual cycle by means of intracavitary electrohysterography, a cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This study used intracavitary electrohysterography to objectively characterize uterine peristaltic activity throughout the menstrual cycle, revealing that ovarian hormones—estrogen, progesterone, oxytocin, and prostaglandins—orchestrate dynamic changes in myometrial contractility that vary by cycle phase and uterine region. Uterine contractions peaked during the early luteal phase in frequency and amplitude, while the mid-follicular phase showed the highest contractile energy; conversely, the late luteal phase exhibited reduced contractile features, including lower contraction amplitude, basal tone, and contractility index, supporting the physiological quiescence necessary for embryo implantation. The fundus demonstrated higher contractile strength and density of both myocytes and oxytocin receptors compared to the lower segment, though its elevated myocyte density resulted in reduced local coordination of contractions. Mechanistically, estradiol increases myometrial excitability via estrogen receptor alpha signaling, which upregulates connexin-43 expression to enhance gap junction formation and cell-to-cell communication. These peristaltic patterns facilitate critical reproductive functions including sperm transport, oocyte movement, and embryo implantation, and abnormalities in uterine contractility are implicated in pathological conditions such as dysmenorrhea, adenomyosis, implantation failure, and recurrent miscarriage.

title: 卵巢组织冷冻保存和移植的临床研究进展
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian tissue cryopreservation and transplantation represent a clinically significant approach for restoring female fertility and ovarian endocrine function in patients undergoing gonadotoxic treatments. While chemotherapy causes substantial ovarian damage through reduction of primordial follicles, oocyte apoptosis, and fibrosis, the resiliency of primordial follicles to cryogenic injury enables successful cryopreservation, with transplanted tissue achieving 55% five-year survival rates and restoring menstrual cycles in 88.7% of patients. Following transplantation, endocrine function recovery reaches 95%, characterized by suppressed FSH and luteinizing hormone with elevated estrogen levels, effectively providing endogenous hormone replacement. Clinical outcomes demonstrate pregnancy rates of 27.3-43.8% and live birth rates of 18.2-32.3%, with natural conception achieving superior outcomes (~30% live birth rate) compared to in vitro fertilization. Patient age at cryopreservation critically influences success, with women ≤35 years achieving significantly higher pregnancy rates and prolonged ovarian function maintenance, while orthotopic transplantation is preferred over ectopic placement for physiologically optimal follicle development and natural conception potential.

title: Blood pressure reduction and anti-inflammatory macrophage augmentation attenuate uterine immune dysregulation and inflammation in mice with salt-sensitive hypertension
summary: Salt-sensitive hypertension promotes systemic inflammation and pro-inflammatory immune cell infiltration in the uterus, including elevated pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages, dendritic cells, natural killer cells, and CD4+ T cells, while simultaneously suppressing anti-inflammatory M2 macrophages and causing lymphatic vessel expansion. High salt intake directly drives these effects by activating pro-inflammatory macrophages and disrupting regulatory T cell function through elevated sodium levels and associated molecular mediators like isolevuglandins and IL-1β. Both blood pressure reduction via hydralazine and Mas receptor agonism via AVE0991 attenuate this uterine immune dysregulation by decreasing systolic blood pressure, reducing pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages and dendritic cells, and promoting macrophage polarization toward the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype through suppression of NF-κB and MAPK signaling pathways. Beyond hypertension's direct uterine inflammatory effects, the condition is broadly associated with impaired reproductive health, including sexual dysfunction, irregular menstrual cycles, early menopause, fibroid growth, and pregnancy complications such as impaired implantation and fetal growth restriction. These findings suggest that therapeutic targeting of hypertension and macrophage polarization may ameliorate uterine inflammation and potentially improve reproductive outcomes in hypertensive women.

title: Pilot Study of Daily Exemestane in Women with Endometrial Intraepithelial Neoplasia or Low Grade Endometrial Cancer
journal: Clinical cancer research : an official journal of the American Association for Cancer Research
summary: This pilot study evaluated exemestane, an aromatase inhibitor, as a treatment for endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia (EIN) and low-grade endometrial cancer, conditions driven by excess estrogen exposure. Exemestane significantly reduced cell proliferation, as demonstrated by a marked decrease in Ki-67 expression from a median of 40.7% at baseline to 18.1% at surgery, while also downregulating estrogen and progesterone receptor expression in tumor tissue. The aromatase inhibitor blocks estrogen production by inhibiting the conversion of androgens in adipose tissue, thereby reducing the hormonal stimulus for these estrogen-dependent malignancies. Treatment was well-tolerated overall, with the most common adverse events being hot flashes (53%), hypertension (40%), and diarrhea (25%), and serum exemestane was detected in 38 of 39 participants at surgery, confirming compliance. Notably, Ki-67 reduction was greater in the exemestane-treated group compared to a matched historical control cohort, with more pronounced effects in patients with EIN or grade 1 cancer. These findings suggest that aromatase inhibitors warrant further investigation for preventing and treating low-grade endometrial malignancies.

title: Introducing a New Hypothesis in the Genesis of Breast Cancer: An Integrative Review
summary:
# Summary

This integrative review proposes a novel hypothesis linking estrogen-driven pathways to breast cancer development through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Estrogen, produced by ovaries, adrenal glands, and adipose tissue, activates estrogen receptors (ERα and ERβ) to drive breast tumorigenesis via genotoxic and proliferative effects, while also regulating immune checkpoint molecules (PD-L1 and PD-1) and promoting recruitment of immunosuppressive macrophages and regulatory T cells. Beyond direct hormonal effects, the review identifies estrogen metabolites and fibrinogen-related proteins (FREPs) as critical contributors to immune evasion and tumor progression, with elevated fibrinogen levels associated with shorter survival and higher recurrence risk. Additionally, estrogen modulates hemostasis by increasing coagulation factors and fibrinogen while decreasing anticoagulant proteins, creating a prothrombotic state that facilitates tumor progression and metastasis. The review emphasizes that estrogen and progesterone together promote breast DNA mutations through proliferative signaling, particularly when progesterone receptor activity is absent or reduced, suggesting combined hormone therapy may amplify cancer risk through disrupted hormone receptor cross-talk.

title: Menstrual Health in Servicewomen: The Menstrual Cycle, Menstrual Disturbances, and Occupational Consequences
summary:
# Summary

The menstrual cycle is regulated by a coordinated hormonal cascade initiated by the kisspeptinergic system, which stimulates gonadotropin-releasing hormone to trigger luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone secretion from the pituitary. These gonadotropins stimulate 17β-estradiol and progesterone production from the ovaries, with estradiol driving follicle and endometrial proliferation during the follicular phase, while progesterone maintains endometrial stability in the luteal phase. Beyond reproductive functions, both hormones influence bone metabolism, muscle physiology, and nervous system function, with progesterone deficiency associated with premenstrual syndrome and low-grade inflammation affecting quality of life in 90% of women. Multiple occupational and physiological stressors in military servicewomen—including low energy availability, sleep deprivation, environmental hazards, and psychogenic stress—suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis through elevated cortisol, resulting in menstrual disturbances such as secondary amenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, and luteal phase defects. These menstrual disturbances associate with increased musculoskeletal injury risk, impaired physical and academic performance, and underlying endocrine dysfunction, with medical conditions including endometriosis and polycystic ovarian syndrome contributing to menstrual pathology in this population.

title: The Association Between Pre-cycle Maternal Vitamin D Status and Reproductive Outcomes in Frozen-Thawed Embryo Transfer: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that vitamin D plays a critical role in reproductive success during frozen-thawed embryo transfer by regulating endometrial receptivity and implantation capacity. Vitamin D receptors are abundantly expressed in reproductive tissues including the endometrium, placenta, and ovaries, where the active metabolite 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3 regulates implantation-related genes such as HOXA10 and modulates immune responses toward a favorable trophoblast-supporting environment. The analysis reveals that vitamin D deficiency is significantly associated with reduced clinical pregnancy rates and live birth rates in frozen embryo transfer cycles, establishing vitamin D sufficiency as a prerequisite for optimal endometrial competence. Notably, frozen embryo transfer cycles avoid the adverse effects of supraphysiologic estradiol from ovarian stimulation, which can mask vitamin D's immunomodulatory benefits observed in fresh cycles. These findings highlight that pre-cycle maternal vitamin D status substantially influences reproductive outcomes and suggest vitamin D sufficiency should be considered an important factor optimizing success in assisted reproductive technology, particularly for patients with conditions like endometriosis that may compromise endometrial function.

title: The association between birth weight/rapid weight gain and early menarche: a systematic review and meta-analysis based on cohort studies
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that rapid postnatal weight gain, particularly between 0-2 years of age, substantially increases the risk of early menarche (RR=1.41), whereas high birth weight paradoxically reduces this risk. The biological mechanism underlying rapid weight gain's effect involves elevated concentrations of insulin-like growth factor 1, insulin resistance, leptin, and adrenal androgens, which collectively influence gonadotropin-releasing hormone pulsatility and activate the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis to advance puberty onset. Early menarche is subsequently associated with multiple adverse health outcomes including endometriosis, breast cancer, type 2 diabetes, obesity, and psychological disorders such as low self-esteem, anxiety, and eating disorders. Modifiable lifestyle factors protect against early menarche, including prolonged breastfeeding, higher plant protein intake, and yogurt consumption, while animal protein intake accelerates menarche timing. Adipokine signaling from fat tissue emerges as a critical mechanistic pathway linking nutritional status to reproductive timing through leptin, lipocalin, and insulin-like growth factors. These findings underscore the importance of promoting healthy infant growth patterns and appropriate dietary practices as preventive strategies against early menarche and its associated chronic diseases.

title: Endometrial Hyperplasia: Current Insights into Epidemiology, Risk Factors, and Clinical Management
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial hyperplasia is a precancerous endometrial condition driven primarily by unopposed estrogen exposure, particularly in women with obesity, polycystic ovarian syndrome, and metabolic dysfunction. Estrogen actively promotes endometrial proliferation while progesterone normally suppresses this growth; obesity intensifies this imbalance by increasing aromatization of androgens to estrogens and reducing sex hormone-binding globulin, thereby elevating bioavailable estrogen. Beyond hormonal mechanisms, obesity-associated insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia activate mitogenic signaling cascades including IGF-1 and PI3K/AKT pathways that further drive aberrant endometrial growth. Atypical hyperplasia carries a substantially higher malignant progression risk (27.5% over 20 years) compared to non-atypical forms and exhibits molecular alterations in PTEN, KRAS, and PI3K signaling that overlap with early endometrial cancer. Progestin therapy effectively treats endometrial hyperplasia, while protective factors including multiparity and combined oral contraceptives work through cyclic progesterone dominance. Diagnosis relies on transvaginal ultrasonography followed by tissue sampling via hysteroscopic biopsy, with emerging biomarkers including p53, Ki-67, and mismatch repair proteins under investigation to better stratify cancer risk.

title: An Overview of Essential Nutritional Strategies and Products in the Treatment of Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This review examines nutritional strategies for managing endometriosis, emphasizing the critical role of diet-driven inflammation and hormonal regulation. Anti-inflammatory dietary approaches, particularly the Mediterranean and MIND diets, are highlighted as first-line interventions that reduce endometriosis-related pain. The mechanisms underlying dietary efficacy involve bioactive fatty acids regulating inflammation, cellular metabolism, and endogenous estrogen levels, with omega-3 fatty acids exerting anti-inflammatory effects while omega-6 and high-fat diets promote inflammatory pathways and oxidative stress. The review identifies multiple dietary triggers including red meat consumption, which elevates estrogen and heme iron that stimulate inflammation, and establishes connections between dysbiosis, immune activation, and impaired estrogen metabolism in endometriosis pathogenesis. Specialized dietary modifications—including low-FODMAP, gluten-free, and low-nickel diets—address comorbid gastrointestinal conditions and reduce visceral symptoms, pelvic pain, and dyspareunia through distinct mechanistic pathways, with gluten elimination reducing mast cell activation and nickel restriction addressing documented allergies that correlate with endometriosis severity.

title: Insights into Singaporean Physicians’ Knowledge and Attitudes on Menopause Hormone Therapy
summary: This study reveals significant knowledge gaps among Singaporean physicians regarding menopause hormone therapy (MHT), despite well-documented clinical benefits. MHT effectively treats menopausal symptoms including vasomotor and genitourinary symptoms while preventing bone loss and postmenopausal osteoporosis, and may provide cardiovascular protection in premature ovarian insufficiency. However, substantial proportions of physicians incorrectly identified MHT as increasing endometrial cancer risk (75%), were unaware of gallbladder disease and stroke risks, and mistakenly considered endometriosis and fatty liver as contraindications. A critical understanding deficit concerns progesterone's protective role against endometrial cancer when combined with estrogen, as unopposed estrogen alone elevates this risk. Notably, obstetrician-gynecologists demonstrated significantly greater prescribing confidence (59% lacking confidence) compared to family medicine physicians (84% lacking confidence). These findings highlight urgent educational needs to optimize MHT utilization and improve patient outcomes in menopausal management across different physician specialties.

title: Endocrine Dyscrasia in the Etiology and Therapy of Alzheimer’s Disease
summary:
# Summary

Age-related endocrine dyscrasia, particularly dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis during menopause and andropause, causes cognitive decline and Alzheimer's disease through elevated circulating gonadotropins. Luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone directly promote Alzheimer's neuropathology by upregulating γ-secretase and δ-secretase pathways, inducing amyloid-β production, tau phosphorylation, and aberrant neuronal cell cycle re-entry, while also compromising blood-brain barrier integrity. Therapeutic strategies including gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues (such as leuprolide acetate), hormone replacement therapy, and 17β-estradiol treatment lower circulating gonadotropins and reverse AD protein deposition, hippocampal dysfunction, and cognitive deficits across multiple domains. Clinical evidence supports these findings, showing that earlier menopause and chemical castration increase dementia risk while later menopause, oral contraceptive use, and GnRH agonist treatment reduce cognitive decline, with a Phase II trial demonstrating that leuprolide acetate improves mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease. The mechanism by which gonadotropins drive neurodegeneration appears distinct from testosterone deficiency, as serum LH concentration rather than reduced free testosterone correlates with amyloid-β accumulation.

title: Ethnopharmacological insights into uterine fibroids: a review of etiology, and therapeutic potential of natural products
summary:
# Summary: Ethnopharmacological Insights into Uterine Fibroids

Uterine fibroids develop through multiple interconnected mechanisms involving hormonal dysregulation, genetic mutations, epigenetic alterations, and inflammatory pathways. Estrogen promotes cellular proliferation while progesterone enhances extracellular matrix production and suppresses apoptosis, with prolonged estrogen dominance driving fibroid formation. Somatic mutations in the MED12 gene disrupt transcriptional regulation and cell cycle control, while epigenetic modifications including DNA methylation, histone modifications, and microRNA dysregulation contribute to disease progression. Growth factors such as insulin-like growth factors, transforming growth factor-beta, epidermal growth factor, and platelet-derived growth factor promote fibroid development, with insulin-like growth factors amplifying effects through interactions with estrogen and insulin signaling pathways. Fibroids are characterized by excessive extracellular matrix accumulation rich in collagen, fibronectin, and laminin, alongside chronic low-grade inflammation involving tumor necrosis factor-alpha and interleukin-6, as well as oxidative stress and reactive oxygen species-mediated DNA damage. Current therapeutic approaches range from surgical interventions including hysterectomy and myomectomy to pharmaceutical options such as selective progesterone receptor modulators, gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists, and levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine devices, with emerging evidence supporting natural products including turmeric, green tea, and phytochemicals exhibiting anti-estrogenic and antiproliferative activities.

title: Biomaterials for Reproductive Restoration: Translating Engineering Innovations
summary:
# Summary

This article reviews engineered biomaterial platforms for restoring reproductive function in conditions including endometriosis, premature ovarian insufficiency, and intrauterine adhesions. Three-dimensional hydrogel scaffolds composed of alginate, collagen, polyethylene glycol, and decellularized extracellular matrices support folliculogenesis and endometrial regeneration while mitigating ischemic injury that complicates conventional cryopreservation and transplantation approaches. Growth factors including VEGF, GDF-9, IGF, EGF, and kit ligands embedded within these matrices promote granulosa cell proliferation and oocyte maturation, with optimal follicle development requiring intermediate stiffness (~0.5–1 kPa) that replicates native ovarian tissue mechanics. Key innovations include collagen-alginate bilayer scaffolds with stiffness gradients that enable in vitro ovulation and hormone production, matrix metalloproteinase-sensitive linkers that allow dynamic remodeling, and coencapsulation of follicles with stem cells to enhance survival and endocrine function. While conventional treatments such as hormone replacement therapy and surgical adhesion removal fail to restore native tissue architecture, these biomaterial-based strategies offer unprecedented opportunities for fertility preservation and restoration in reproductive-age patients facing gonadotoxic therapies or age-related decline.

title: Delayed onset of HPV-associated uterine malignancies: seven pathogenetic factors in contrast to cervical cancer
journal: Romanian Journal of Morphology and Embryology
summary:
# Summary

This article identifies seven distinct pathogenetic factors explaining why HPV-associated endometrial malignancies develop with delayed onset compared to cervical cancer, despite HPV's well-established role in cervical tumorigenesis. Anatomical barriers including the cervical canal, cervical mucus (containing secretory IgA and antimicrobial peptides), and distance significantly reduce direct viral access to the endometrium, while cyclical shedding of the endometrial functional layer during menstruation prevents long-term viral persistence—a prerequisite for HPV-driven malignant transformation. The endometrial immune microenvironment, adapted for reproductive tolerance, creates cyclical windows of reduced antiviral surveillance enhanced by progesterone-driven immunosuppression, allowing HPV to evade immune detection through downregulation of interferon pathways and antigen-presenting machinery. Critical additional factors include unopposed estrogen exposure (which promotes continuous endometrial proliferation), and pre-existing genetic alterations in PTEN, PIK3CA, and mismatch repair genes that create a permissive background for HPV oncoproteins E6/E7 to act as late-stage accelerants rather than primary initiators. Emerging evidence suggests endometriosis and other ectopic endometrial proliferations may serve as viral reservoirs, while co-infections and microbial dysbiosis facilitate viral ascent and chronic inflammation. Overall, HPV functions as a multifactorial co-factor requiring the convergence of anatomical, immunological, hormonal, and genetic dysregulation to promote endometrial malignancy.

title: Pituitary neuroendocrine tumors in patients with prior extrapituitary cancer – clinical and immunohistochemical insights into a complex association. Evidence from a retrospective series
journal: Romanian Journal of Morphology and Embryology
summary: This retrospective study examines the association between pituitary neuroendocrine tumors (PitNETs) and prior extrapituitary cancers, revealing a bidirectional link mediated by hormonal dysregulation. The most frequently associated malignancies were breast carcinoma and renal cell carcinoma (33.3% each), followed by thyroid carcinoma and hepatocellular carcinoma, with somatotroph tumors predominating in GH and PRL immunopositivity across these cancer types. The GH/IGF-1 axis emerges as a central mechanism, whereby these hormones exert mitogenic, pro-angiogenic, and anti-apoptotic effects that stimulate cell proliferation and survival, with acromegaly patients showing increased risk for multiple malignancies including colorectal, thyroid, renal, breast, and prostate cancers. Notably, normalization of GH and IGF-1 levels correlates with reduced neoplastic risk, suggesting a dose-dependent relationship between hormonal excess and tumorigenesis. Additionally, ACTH-secreting tumors promote oncogenesis indirectly through immunosuppression, insulin resistance, and chronic inflammation. Immunohistochemical analysis showed low Ki67 and absent p53 expression in most PitNETs, indicating generally low-grade behavior, while characteristic transcription factors (Pit1, Tpit, SF-1, GATA3) and neuroendocrine markers facilitated tumor classification and identified hormone-secreting subtypes associated with specific extrapituitary malignancies.

title: Sex Matters: Hormonal and Chromosomal Determinants of Autoimmunity and Anti‐Cancer Immunity Across the Lifespan
summary:
# Summary

Sex hormones profoundly shape both anti-cancer immunity and autoimmunity throughout the lifespan by acting through distinct receptor pathways and epigenetic mechanisms. Estrogens, which bind estrogen receptors (ERα and ERβ) and GPER1, promote thymic involution and enhance Th1 immune responses while also increasing apoptosis in developing thymocytes, paradoxically driving autoimmunity in women who are disproportionately affected by autoimmune diseases.
