In contrast, androgens protect against autoimmunity by increasing AIRE expression in medullary thymic epithelial cells to enhance negative selection, and reducing thymic output through suppression of DLL4-mediated T cell commitment; consequently, hypogonadism in men is associated with increased risk of rheumatoid arthritis and systemic lupus erythematosus. During puberty, hormonal changes including elevated estrogens and androgens trigger epigenetic modifications and immune cell programming that establish sex-specific immune trajectories, with females showing greater methylation changes near estrogen-responsive elements. The divergent effects of estrogen receptor subtypes—ERα deletion decreasing pro-inflammatory Th1 responses while ERβ activation increasing IL-17 and IL-21 production through NF-κB engagement—illustrate how sex hormone signaling complexity underlies the striking female bias in autoimmunity observed across conditions including multiple sclerosis and lupus.

title: Molecular Insights into Widespread Pseudouridine RNA Modifications: Implications for Women’s Health and Disease
summary: Pseudouridine (Ψ), the most abundant chemical modification in RNA, plays critical roles in maintaining RNA structure, stability, and translational efficiency through its involvement in proper RNA folding and ribosome function. The enzymes responsible for pseudouridylation—including PUS1, PUS7, and dyskerin—are tightly regulated by cellular stress conditions such as oxidative stress and nutrient deprivation, and their aberrant activity directly contributes to the development and progression of gynecological cancers including breast, ovarian, endometrial, and cervical cancers. Dysregulation of pseudouridine and other RNA modification pathways has been implicated not only in cancer but also in neurological disorders, metabolic syndromes, cardiovascular disease, and reproductive dysfunction, indicating broad implications for women's health. Elevated pseudouridine levels in tissues, blood, and urine correlate with tumor burden and metastatic potential, suggesting pseudouridine as a potential biomarker for cancer severity and therapeutic response. The article highlights that sex hormones such as estrogen and progesterone regulate gene expression at multiple levels, and recent evidence shows that the m6A methyltransferase METTL14 binds to estrogen receptor alpha, suggesting an intimate link between hormonal signaling and epitranscriptomic regulation relevant to endometriosis and reproductive diseases. These findings underscore the importance of understanding RNA modification pathways as fundamental mechanisms underlying both normal reproductive physiology and disease pathogenesis in women.

title: Immunological Causes of Infertility: Diagnostic Perspectives
summary:
# Summary

The immune system plays a critical role across all stages of reproduction, affecting fertilization, implantation, and pregnancy maintenance, with endometriosis emerging as a disorder with a strong immunological basis associated with infertility. Successful implantation requires a delicate balance between pro-inflammatory T cells (Th1 and Th17) and anti-inflammatory regulatory T cells (Tregs), a balance modulated by progesterone that protects the developing embryo while maintaining immunological tolerance to the semi-allogeneic fetus. Diagnostic evaluation of infertility should assess lymphocyte subpopulation balance, cytokine profiles, and Th1/Th2 immune responses, as recurrent implantation failure and pregnancy loss often involve immune system dysfunction caused by infections, subclinical inflammation, and microbial colonization. Maintenance of an appropriate Treg/Th17 ratio is critical for sustaining pregnancy and preventing miscarriage, preeclampsia, and preterm birth, with deficiency in Tregs linked to unexplained infertility and pathological pregnancy conditions. Multiple factors including metabolic disturbances (hyperglycaemia and insulin resistance), pathogens, vitamin D3 deficiency, and obesity disrupt Treg generation and function through mitochondrial oxidative stress and chronic low-grade systemic inflammation, thereby compromising fertility outcomes.

title: Sex Hormone Supplementation and Cardiovascular Disease Risk
summary:
# Summary

Sex hormones profoundly regulate cardiovascular health through multiple mechanisms including lipid and calcium metabolism, coagulation, and protein synthesis, with men showing higher baseline cardiovascular disease risk despite having lower hormone levels. Both hormonal deficiency and excess can be detrimental to the cardiovascular system; testosterone deficiency specifically increases risk for stroke, myocardial infarction, and heart failure, while testosterone excess and anabolic androgenic steroid abuse promote hypertension, dyslipidemia, and premature atherosclerosis. Testosterone replacement therapy in hypogonadal men provides cardioprotective benefits by reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines, endothelin-1, lipid levels, carotid intima-media thickness, and body adiposity while improving insulin sensitivity and exercise capacity. These findings suggest that maintaining physiologically appropriate hormone levels is critical for cardiovascular health, though the relationship between testosterone and cardiovascular outcomes remains complex and requires careful clinical consideration during hormone supplementation.

title: Vitamin D in Endocrine Disorders: A Broad Overview of Evidence in Musculoskeletal, Thyroid, Parathyroid, and Reproductive Disorders
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review establishes vitamin D as a multifunctional hormone with significant roles across endocrine disorders, particularly in reproductive health and endometriosis. The literature consistently reports lower vitamin D levels in endometriosis, along with infertility and polycystic ovarian syndrome, suggesting vitamin D deficiency as a common feature in reproductive disorders. Vitamin D exerts its effects through the vitamin D receptor (VDR), which regulates immune tolerance by downregulating pro-inflammatory Th1 and Th17 cells while stimulating regulatory T cells, and suppresses inflammatory mediators like TNF-alpha while promoting anti-inflammatory IL-10 production. Additionally, VDR signaling demonstrates anti-tumorigenic properties through cell cycle arrest, apoptosis induction, and inhibition of angiogenic factors like vascular endothelial growth factor and transforming growth factor-beta. The review emphasizes that vitamin D supplementation effectively treats established disorders including osteoporosis, hypoparathyroidism, and osteomalacia, while preliminary evidence suggests benefits in PCOS management. These immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory mechanisms position vitamin D as a potential disease-modifying agent in endometriosis and other endocrine-inflammatory conditions.

title: Smooth Muscle Mechanosensitivity Generates and Maintains Pressure Gradients Across the Intestine
summary: This study demonstrates that intestinal smooth muscle mechanosensitivity, primarily mediated by L-type calcium channels (~80% of response) and IP3 receptors, generates and maintains pressure gradients essential for peristaltic transport. Smooth muscle cells respond to mechanical stretch by increasing contractile amplitude through calcium-induced calcium release mechanisms, and this positive-feedback mechanosensitive behavior spontaneously generates pressure differentials across the gut lumen. The authors show that sufficient pressure gradients can reverse the normal caudal direction of peristaltic wave propagation, suggesting that mechanosensitivity acts as a stabilizing mechanism for applied pressures. These findings are proposed to have broader implications for pathophysiology in peristaltic organs beyond the intestine, including mechanisms underlying endometriosis, where abnormal intrauterine pressure gradients (reaching 50 mmHg compared to peritoneal pressure of 7-12 mmHg) may promote retrograde menstruation and ectopic endometrial tissue implantation. The study identifies L-type channel blockers like nifedipine and IP3 receptor inhibitors like 2-APB as potential pharmacological tools for modulating pathological mechanosensitive responses in these organs.

title: Nr4a1 β-cell specific deletion impairs glucose tolerance in female mice
journal: American journal of physiology. Endocrinology and metabolism
summary:
# Summary

The Nr4a1 transcription factor plays a critical role in pancreatic β-cell function and glucose homeostasis, particularly in female mice through estrogen signaling. Nr4a1 regulates expression of genes essential for fuel metabolism, mitochondrial respiration, ATP production, and β-cell proliferation; its deletion impairs these processes and reduces glucose-stimulated insulin secretion. Estrogen induces Nr4a1 expression in β-cells, and β-cell-specific Nr4a1 deletion in female mice fed a high-fat diet results in impaired glucose tolerance, decreased β-cell area, and reduced expression of metabolic genes including Sdhb and Eno1. The mechanism involves Nr4a1-dependent regulation of glycolytic and mitochondrial respiration genes, as Sdhb knockdown alone impairs insulin secretion, demonstrating the importance of these metabolic pathways. These findings suggest that Nr4a1 mediates estrogen's protective effects on β-cell function under metabolic stress, and may implicate this pathway in sex-specific differences in diabetes susceptibility.

title: Burden, causes, and treatment approaches of recurrent pregnancy loss: a scoping review
summary:
# Summary

Recurrent pregnancy loss results from a multifactorial pathophysiology involving genetic, anatomical, immunological, and hormonal mechanisms. Genetic contributions include parental chromosomal abnormalities and balanced translocations, while anatomical factors encompass uterine malformations such as septate uterus and conditions like Asherman's syndrome that impair embryo implantation. Immunological dysregulation, particularly involving elevated Natural Killer cells and T cells whose interactions with HLA molecules regulate placentation, represents a major etiologic pathway alongside autoimmune conditions including antiphospholipid antibody syndrome and systemic lupus erythematosus. Hormonal imbalances affecting thyroid hormones, progesterone, estrogen, and insulin—manifesting in conditions such as polycystic ovarian syndrome, diabetes, and hyperprolactinemia—significantly contribute to pregnancy loss. Maternal and paternal lifestyle factors including smoking, alcohol consumption, poor nutrition, and paternal sperm abnormalities increase risk, while advanced maternal age and obesity create additional susceptibility through increased aneuploidy and chronic inflammation respectively. Infectious agents including Chlamydia, cytomegalovirus, and other organisms, as well as endometriosis-associated decreased endometrial receptivity, further compromise pregnancy establishment and maintenance.

title: Research trends in estrogen and rheumatoid arthritis: a systematic bibliometric approach
summary: This bibliometric analysis reveals that estrogen plays a multifaceted role in rheumatoid arthritis pathogenesis, with women experiencing 2-3 times higher incidence rates than men. Estrogen modulates RA development through multiple interconnected mechanisms, primarily involving dysregulation of autoimmune and inflammatory responses, including alterations in cytokine production, macrophage function, and apoptosis pathways. The research landscape demonstrates evolving focus areas, with early studies emphasizing pregnancy and postmenopausal states, while recent investigations (since 2020) increasingly highlight disease activity, immune responses, and oxidative stress as key mechanistic themes. Notably, menopause emerges as a critical risk period for RA onset, likely reflecting the sharp decline in estrogen levels and its immunomodulatory consequences. The analysis also identifies estradiol and hormone replacement therapy as important clinical considerations, alongside associations with related autoimmune conditions such as systemic lupus erythematosus, suggesting shared hormonal mechanisms across autoimmune diseases. Emerging research areas including gut microbiota suggest future investigation into how estrogen-microbiota interactions may influence RA pathogenesis.

title: Comparative effects of estradiol and daidzein on the expression of endometrial cancer-related genes and histopathological parameters in the uterus of ovariectomized rats
journal: Iranian Journal of Basic Medical Sciences
summary:
# Summary

This study compared the effects of estradiol and daidzein on endometrial gene expression and histopathology in ovariectomized rats to understand mechanisms relevant to endometrial cancer prevention. Ovariectomy induced endometrial atrophy and substantially downregulated key protective genes including estrogen receptors (ERα and ERβ), the tumor suppressor PTEN, the proliferation marker Ki67, and antioxidant enzymes (SOD and catalase), while increasing oxidative stress markers. While estradiol effectively reversed these changes and restored endometrial volume and glandular structure, it also induced hyperplastic effects characteristic of estrogen-dependent endometrial cancer risk. Daidzein, a phytoestrogen that binds estrogen receptors with weaker affinity, successfully prevented uterine atrophy by upregulating ERβ, antioxidant enzymes, and Ki67 expression while avoiding the hyperplastic effects of estradiol, resulting in more modest but therapeutically beneficial restoration of endometrial architecture. These findings suggest that selective estrogen receptor modulation via phytoestrogens may offer a safer alternative to hormone replacement therapy by maintaining endometrial health without promoting cancer-associated hyperplasia.

title: Animal models of postpartum hemorrhage
summary: Postpartum hemorrhage results from failure of the myometrium to contract and compress spiral arteries after placental delivery, a process called uterine atony. The regulation of uterine contractility involves complex hormonal signaling: estrogen stimulates endometrial growth and promotes prostaglandin production, which facilitates contractions, while progesterone opposes these effects and maintains pregnancy until labor initiation through progesterone withdrawal and increased oxytocin signaling. Oxytocin, which opposes progesterone actions, is the primary treatment for uterine atony, though sustained exposure can desensitize oxytocin receptors and reduce therapeutic effectiveness. Because small animal models (mice, rats, rabbits, guinea pigs) have bicornuate uteri, shorter gestations, minimal myometrial smooth muscle, and lack menstruation, they poorly replicate human postpartum hemorrhage physiology. Guinea pigs most closely approximate human conditions with hemomonochorial placentation, sustained high progesterone through late pregnancy, and preterm labor susceptibility, while rabbits offer the most similar placental architecture. Larger animal models including sheep, cows, and nonhuman primates more accurately recapitulate human uterine anatomy and oxytocin receptor dynamics, making them superior for studying postpartum hemorrhage mechanisms.

title: Microbiota-dependent IFN-L controls uterine and placental immunity
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that the cervicovaginal microbiota promotes interferon lambda (IFN-L) production, which plays a critical regulatory role in controlling uterine immunity and protecting against bacterial infection. IFN-L constrains type 17 immune responses (Th17, γδT17, and ILC3 cells) in a microbiota-dependent manner by signaling through receptors on uterine epithelial cells and upregulating interferon-stimulated genes involved in antibacterial defense. Sex hormones modulate this system, with progesterone promoting higher frequencies of lymphoid cells compared to estrogen, though the microbiota dependency of IFN-L's effects distinguishes its role from hormone-driven immunity. During pregnancy with Streptococcus agalactiae infection, IFN-L enhanced antibacterial responses and protected the decidua and fetus from ascending bacterial transmission, while loss of IFN-L signaling increased bacterial burden in maternal and fetal tissues and enhanced neonatal infection. These findings suggest that dysregulation of the microbiota-IFN-L axis contributes to pregnancy complications and endometriosis, both conditions associated with vaginal dysbiosis and dysregulated type 17 immunity.

title: Leukemia in users of contemporary hormonal contraception: A nationwide registry-based cohort study among premenopausal women in Denmark
summary:
# Summary

Although estrogen and progesterone have been mechanistically implicated in leukemogenesis through their promotion of hematopoietic stem cell self-renewal and proliferation, and receptors for these sex hormones are present on leukemic cells, this large Danish registry-based cohort study of nearly two million premenopausal women found no increased leukemia risk associated with contemporary hormonal contraceptive use. Neither combined estrogen-progestin contraceptives nor progestin-only formulations showed associations with any leukemia subtype, including acute lymphoblastic leukemia, acute myeloid leukemia, chronic lymphocytic leukemia, and chronic myeloid leukemia, regardless of current, recent, or previous use patterns. While hormonal contraceptives suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and reduce gonadotropin levels (FSH and LH), which can stimulate malignant cell proliferation through receptors expressed on hematopoietic cells, the epidemiologic findings do not support a clinically meaningful leukemia risk in reproductive-aged women using modern contraceptive formulations. These reassuring results remained consistent across sensitivity analyses and after adjusting for relevant confounders including endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome.

title: The estrogen-progestogen-oxidative stress network in uterine fibroids: mechanistic insights and therapeutic opportunities
journal: Redox Report : Communications in Free Radical Research
summary:
# Summary

Uterine fibroids result from a complex interplay between hormonal imbalance and oxidative stress, termed the estrogen-progesterone-oxidative stress (E-P-OS) network. Estrogen dominance drives fibroid development through elevated estrogen receptor expression and increased local estradiol production via upregulated aromatase and CYP1B1 enzymes, while progesterone paradoxically promotes fibroid cell proliferation and collagen deposition despite being traditionally viewed as protective. Both hormones amplify reactive oxygen species (ROS) production—estradiol through activation of NADPH oxidase (NOX4) and generation of highly reactive estradiol quinones, and progesterone through NADPH production—creating a localized oxidative stress microenvironment that further accelerates fibroid growth through abnormal leiomyoma cell proliferation. Estradiol activates multiple downstream signaling pathways including Wnt/β-catenin, PI3K/Akt/mTOR, and MAPK/ERK, while also driving expression of growth factors such as IGF-1 and TGF-β that synergize with progesterone-induced effects. Current hormonal therapies including GnRH analogues and selective progesterone receptor modulators target this network, though the dual role of progesterone in promoting both proliferation and differentiation presents therapeutic challenges. Understanding the mechanistic crosstalk between hormonal and oxidative stress pathways offers opportunities for developing novel antioxidant-based therapies to complement existing hormonal treatments.

title: Ovarian cancer risk and survival according to tumor sex hormone receptor expression: An ovarian Cancer association consortium and ovarian tumor tissue analysis consortium pooled analysis
summary:
# Summary

This pooled analysis demonstrates that sex hormone receptor expression (androgen, estrogen, and progesterone receptors) significantly influences both epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) risk and survival outcomes across histological subtypes. The study confirms the hormone hypothesis that estrogen and androgen stimulation promote ovarian epithelial transformation, while progesterone exerts protective effects, with oral contraceptives reducing risk through suppression of androgens and estrogens. Key clinical and demographic factors differentially influence EOC risk based on receptor status: obesity associates with estrogen receptor-negative tumors, hysterectomy with estrogen receptor-positive tumors, and postmenopausal status with progesterone receptor-negative tumors. Progesterone receptor-positive tumors and estrogen receptor-negative tumors demonstrate superior survival compared to their receptor-negative or receptor-positive counterparts, respectively, with particularly favorable outcomes in high-grade serous ovarian cancer expressing both ER-negative and PR-positive phenotypes. The findings suggest that estrogen and progesterone receptor expression may serve as prognostic biomarkers for patient stratification and survival prediction across EOC histotypes including high-grade serous, endometrioid, and clear cell cancers.

title: From Abnormal Uterine Bleeding to Malignancy: The Role of Hormonal Profiling and Histopathology in Early Detection of Endometrial Carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This study examines how hormonal imbalances and abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) contribute to endometrial carcinoma development, with particular emphasis on estrogen-driven pathways and obesity-related mechanisms. Abnormal uterine bleeding serves as a critical early warning sign of endometrioid carcinoma and often reflects underlying hormonal alterations, endometrial hyperplasia, or progressive malignancy. The normal menstrual cycle maintains endometrial homeostasis through balanced estrogen and progesterone signaling, where progesterone prevents pathological endometrial thickening; when this balance is disrupted, endometrial hyperplasia develops and significantly increases endometrioid carcinoma risk. Obesity emerges as a major modifiable risk factor, promoting endometrial cancer through multiple pathways including increased estrogen production by adipose tissue, adipokine-mediated endometrial growth stimulation, insulin resistance, and chronic inflammation. Additionally, thyroid dysfunction and elevated prolactin levels independently contribute to endometrial pathology by disrupting gonadotropin-releasing hormone signaling and inhibiting ovulation, while elevated LH/FSH ratios correlate with abnormal endometrial thickening. The findings underscore that comprehensive hormonal profiling combined with histopathological evaluation is essential for early detection of endometrial carcinoma, particularly in postmenopausal women presenting with AUB.

title: Understanding Bone Metabolism Biomarker Variability Across the Menstrual Cycle: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Sex hormones, particularly oestradiol and progesterone, play a central regulatory role in bone metabolism by modulating osteoclast and osteoblast activity across the menstrual cycle. Oestradiol suppresses bone resorption through multiple mechanisms: it downregulates RANKL while upregulating its inhibitor OPG, suppresses PTH secretion, inhibits sclerostin to enhance the bone-anabolic Wnt/β-catenin pathway, and directly inhibits PTH-induced osteoclastogenesis via the cAMP-PKA pathway. Conversely, oestradiol stimulates osteoblast activity and bone formation, while progesterone exhibits osteoanabolic functions during the luteal phase. Bone resorption markers, particularly β-CTX-I, show consistent cyclical variation with higher concentrations during the follicular phase (when oestradiol is low) and lower concentrations during the luteal phase (when progesterone dominates), while bone formation markers including PINP and BALP demonstrate less consistent but generally elevated levels during the late follicular and luteal phases. These findings demonstrate that sex hormone-driven cyclical changes in oestradiol and progesterone create significant variability in bone metabolism biomarkers throughout the menstrual cycle, with bone resorption markers showing more predictable patterns than formation markers.

title: Effect of intrauterine administration of platelet-rich plasma two days before
journal: The Journal of Veterinary Medical Science
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated intrauterine platelet-rich plasma (PRP) administration in dairy cows, focusing on endometrial thickness and reproductive outcomes. Intrauterine PRP significantly increased endometrial thickness through growth factor-mediated mechanisms, including transforming growth factor-β1, epidermal growth factor, insulin-like growth factor, vascular endothelial growth factor, and fibroblast growth factor, which promote cell proliferation and angiogenesis independent of steroid hormone alterations. However, despite these morphological improvements, PRP administration did not significantly improve conception rates, suggesting that endometrial thickness alone is insufficient for pregnancy success. The research demonstrates that PRP exerts tissue-regenerative and anti-inflammatory effects through multiple pathways involving leukocyte-derived matrix metalloproteinases and enhanced vascular permeability, thereby modulating endometrial function. These findings suggest that while PRP may address structural endometrial deficiencies—particularly in conditions like thin endometrium and repeat breeder syndrome—additional factors beyond endometrial thickness are critical for establishing successful pregnancies.

title: Postoperative hormonal modulation in a patient with granulosa cell tumor and myasthenia gravis: a gyneco-oncologic perspective
summary:
# Summary

This case illustrates the critical interaction between hormonal withdrawal and autoimmune disease in a patient with concurrent granulosa cell tumor and myasthenia gravis. Adult-type granulosa cell tumors are rare estrogen-secreting ovarian neoplasms that typically present with abnormal uterine bleeding and endometrial proliferative changes, including hyperplasia and increased carcinoma risk. Following bilateral oophorectomy and estradiol withdrawal, the patient experienced acute postoperative cardiopulmonary instability manifesting as palpitations, chest discomfort, and progressive fatigue within three days of surgery. Estradiol possesses neuroprotective and immunomodulatory properties, suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6 and TNF-α) implicated in autoimmune exacerbation and modulating acetylcholine receptor sensitivity; consequently, abrupt estrogen deprivation exacerbated her underlying myasthenia gravis, which is characterized by antibodies against the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor. Rapid improvement occurred within 48 hours of initiating estradiol valerate replacement therapy, underscoring that hormonal transition states (menopause, postpartum, post-oophorectomy) significantly worsen neuromuscular symptoms in estrogen-sensitive autoimmune patients. This case emphasizes the necessity for specialized perioperative planning in patients with combined hormone-secreting tumors and autoimmune neuromuscular disorders.

title: Decoding menstrual health across the lifespan: a scoping review of digital health tools in research
summary:
# Summary

The menstrual cycle operates through a coordinated hormonal cascade in which follicle-stimulating hormone stimulates ovarian follicles to secrete estrogen, rising estrogen levels trigger a luteinizing hormone surge that initiates ovulation, and the resulting corpus luteum secretes progesterone to sustain the luteal phase before hormonal decline triggers menses. Beyond reproductive function, progesterone has systemic physiological effects including elevation of core body temperature, increased respiratory function and carbon dioxide sensitivity, and increased heart rate during the luteal phase. Contraceptive interventions such as combined oral contraceptives suppress endogenous ovarian hormone production, while hormonal intrauterine devices cause local endometrial thinning, inhibit sperm motility, and may suppress ovulation, though these interventions do not alter natural menstrual cycle length. Menstrual health varies significantly across the lifespan, with body mass index and menarche timing influencing cycle characteristics, rising FSH during perimenopause reflecting declining ovarian reserve, and 20-40% of women experiencing premenstrual syndrome with associated mood swings, bloating, and dysmenorrhea that substantially impair work performance, athletic training, and quality of life. Women with severe premenstrual symptoms or premenstrual dysphoric disorder exhibit lower heart rate variability, while perimenopausal women have 40% higher odds of depressive symptoms, highlighting the substantial clinical and occupational burden of menstrual cycle-related symptoms across reproductive life stages.

title: Oncologic and Reproductive Outcomes of Fertility-Sparing Management in Early-Stage Endometrial Carcinoma: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that fertility-sparing management of early-stage endometrial carcinoma combines hormonal therapies with surgical intervention to achieve superior oncologic and reproductive outcomes. Multiple hormonal mechanisms work synergistically to control disease: progestin-based therapy activates progesterone receptors and induces apoptosis while suppressing estrogen signaling, GnRH analogs reduce estrogen production and promote cell-cycle arrest, metformin enhances progesterone receptor expression and inhibits the PI3K/AKT/mTOR proliferation pathway, and levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine devices deliver high local hormone exposure with minimal systemic effects. Hysteroscopic resection mechanically removes visible lesions and improves responsiveness to hormonal treatment, and combination strategies incorporating surgical resection plus hormonal therapy achieve significantly higher complete response rates, lower recurrence, greater remission durability, and more consistent reproductive outcomes (48% pregnancy rate, 36% live birth rate) compared to monotherapy regimens alone. Monotherapy approaches, whether oral progestin or levonorgestrel-IUD alone, demonstrated lower efficacy and higher rates of early treatment failure, while risk factors including obesity and polycystic ovary syndrome increase carcinoma incidence in young women. The evidence supports that multimodal treatment combining hysteroscopic resection with combination hormonal regimens provides the most favorable balance of oncologic control and fertility preservation in this population.

title: Sex Hormones-Mediated Modulation of Immune Checkpoints in Pregnancy and Recurrent Pregnancy Loss
summary:
# Summary

Sex hormones regulate immune checkpoint protein expression during pregnancy through distinct mechanisms that maintain maternal-fetal tolerance and prevent recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL). Progesterone and estradiol upregulate inhibitory checkpoint molecules including LAG-3 and TIM-3 on cytotoxic cells, while dihydrotestosterone modulates VISTA, LAG-3, and TIM-3 expression with cell-type specificity. These hormonal effects coordinate with regulatory T cell expansion and dendritic cell-mediated tryptophan degradation via indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase, creating a tolerogenic microenvironment at the fetal-maternal interface. Additionally, progesterone suppresses pro-inflammatory Th1 and Th17 responses while promoting Th2 polarization and recruiting specialized uterine NK cells, whereas androgen receptor activation upregulates immunosuppressive factors including IL-10, TGF-β, and FOXP3. Notably, lymphocytes from RPL patients express lower levels of immune checkpoint proteins compared to pregnant women, suggesting that dysregulation of hormone-mediated immune tolerance contributes to unexplained pregnancy losses. These findings highlight sex hormone-immune checkpoint axis modulation as a critical mechanism in maintaining pregnancy homeostasis and identify potential therapeutic targets for RPL management.

title: Premenstrual Syndrome and Nutritional Factors: A Narrative Review of Current Evidence and Clinical Implications
summary: Premenstrual syndrome affects nearly half of reproductive-aged women and causes significant physical and psychological symptoms during the luteal phase, substantially reducing quality of life. While cyclical ovarian hormone fluctuations are physiological, symptom development involves complex dysregulation of multiple systems: neurosteroid-GABAergic dysfunction impairs GABA receptor plasticity, serotonergic dysregulation alters mood regulation, and abnormal hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis function dysregulates cortisol and stress responses. Nutritional factors substantially influence symptom severity; diets high in ultra-processed foods, refined carbohydrates, and saturated fats exacerbate symptoms, while supplementation with micronutrients including calcium, vitamin D, zinc, iron, and omega-3 fatty acids shows protective effects. Environmental and lifestyle modifications, particularly dietary optimization and reduced caffeine intake, represent evidence-based complementary approaches for both preventing and alleviating premenstrual syndrome symptoms. The heterogeneous pathophysiology—encompassing hormonal, genetic, inflammatory, and environmental factors—supports multimodal treatment strategies combining nutritional intervention with pharmacological options such as SSRIs in severe cases.

title: Melatonin mitigates hormonal toxicity in cannabis-treated female Wistar rats: involvement of cannabinoid receptor
summary: Cannabis sativa suppresses multiple reproductive hormones by activating cannabinoid receptors, significantly reducing GnRH, FSH, LH, estradiol, progesterone, and prolactin levels in female rats. The cannabinoid receptor 1 (CB1), predominantly expressed in the central nervous system, reproductive tissues, pituitary gland, and ovarian follicles, primarily mediates these hormonal disruptions through direct inhibition of GnRH neurons and the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. While cannabinoid receptor 2 (CB2) also contributes to hormonal imbalance through immune modulation, CB1 activation causes more severe hormonal toxicity. Melatonin effectively ameliorates cannabis-induced hormonal toxicity by binding to and reducing CB1 receptor activity, restoring normal levels of all suppressed reproductive hormones through its free radical scavenging capacity. This antioxidant mechanism also protects against the direct gonadotoxic, ovotoxic effects of delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol and cannabis-associated adverse pregnancy outcomes including pregnancy loss, low birth weight, and prematurity. The findings suggest melatonin supplementation could serve as a protective strategy against cannabis-induced reproductive toxicity in females.

title: Augmented-OvSynch enhances estrus resumption, luteal function, and conception rates in postpartum Zebu crossbred cows under tropical smallholder systems
summary:
# Summary

The Augmented-OvSynch protocol, which incorporates a third GnRH injection on day 5 post-insemination, substantially improved reproductive outcomes in postpartum Zebu crossbred cattle under tropical conditions by promoting accessory corpus luteum formation and elevating progesterone concentrations during the critical early luteal phase. This hormone manipulation enhanced estrus expression (83.94% vs. 37.80% in controls) and markedly increased pregnancy rates per artificial insemination (68.61% vs. 29.27%), with treated cows demonstrating significantly higher progesterone levels from days 5 to 21 post-insemination. The treatment's success was associated with improved redox balance in pregnant animals, as pregnant cows consistently showed lower malondialdehyde (oxidative stress marker) and higher total antioxidant capacity compared to non-pregnant counterparts. The study demonstrates that elevated oxidative stress and compromised antioxidant defense—exacerbated by heat stress, lactational metabolism, and nutritional challenges in tropical environments—impair ovarian cyclicity and conception rates, whereas progesterone and estradiol work synergistically to support endometrial preparation, embryo implantation, and pregnancy maintenance while modulating antioxidant defense mechanisms. These findings underscore the critical interplay between hormonal manipulation, oxidative stress mitigation, and reproductive success in addressing postpartum anestrus in heat-stressed Bos indicus cattle.

title: Digitoxin-induced apoptosis in ovarian granulosa cells disrupts follicular development and impairs reproductive performance
summary: Digitoxin, a cardiac glycoside used therapeutically for heart failure and atrial fibrillation, severely impairs ovarian function through multiple mechanisms targeting granulosa cells and systemic hormone regulation. At the cellular level, digitoxin reduces granulosa cell viability and proliferation while simultaneously increasing apoptosis by downregulating anti-apoptotic proteins (MCL1, CDK4, PCNA) and upregulating pro-apoptotic caspases (CASP3, CASP7, CASP8, CASP9). In vivo, digitoxin administration suppresses circulating estradiol, follicle-stimulating hormone, and luteinizing hormone levels, disrupting the hormonal signals essential for normal follicular development and ovulation. These effects collectively impair reproductive outcomes, causing delayed first estrus, reduced corpus luteum numbers, and significantly decreased litter size despite paradoxically increased antral follicle counts. Transcriptomic analysis revealed widespread gene expression changes affecting steroid biosynthesis, oocyte maturation, and meiotic progression pathways, explaining the broad reproductive toxicity observed. These findings highlight digitoxin's potential to cause reproductive dysfunction as a clinically relevant adverse effect, emphasizing the need for careful monitoring in female patients receiving this cardiac medication.

title: Sex hormone–psoriasis interactions: from sex-specific clinical features to immunoregulatory mechanisms
summary: Sex hormones, particularly estrogen and testosterone, play critical regulatory roles in psoriasis pathogenesis through bidirectional interactions with immune and endocrine axes. Estrogen exerts protective effects primarily through estrogen receptor signaling, which promotes regulatory T cell differentiation and suppresses pro-inflammatory Th17 responses by upregulating anti-inflammatory genes (Foxp3, IL-10, GATA3) while suppressing pro-inflammatory genes (ROR-γt, IL-17, IFN-γ). Additionally, estrogen inhibits IL-1β production by innate immune cells, thereby reducing downstream IL-17A generation and limiting angiogenic processes. In contrast, testosterone deficiency in male psoriasis patients correlates with increased disease severity and comorbidities including erectile dysfunction, visceral adiposity, and insulin resistance, while testosterone supplementation improves skin lesions. The complex interplay between psoriasis-induced inflammation and hormonal axes is evident through systemic inflammation activating the HPA axis and IL-17 signaling disrupting the HPG axis, which clinical observations link to menstrual cycle fluctuations, perimenopause exacerbation, and pregnancy-associated remission in women. Estrogen receptor downregulation in psoriatic skin lesions suggests impaired local hormone signaling contributes to disease pathogenesis, whereas restoring receptor expression correlates with symptom amelioration and relapse prevention.

title: Hormonal Modulation of Keratoconus: A Systematic Review and Screening Strategy for At-Risk Populations
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review establishes a robust association between hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis hormonal fluctuations and keratoconus development, with endogenous shifts during pregnancy and exogenous hormone exposure accounting for approximately 87% of identified cases. Sex hormone receptors for estrogen, progesterone, and testosterone are present in corneal tissue and regulate critical physiological processes including corneal thickness and curvature through modulation of matrix metalloproteinases and collagenolytic enzymes that govern extracellular matrix composition. Quantitative hormonal studies consistently demonstrate elevated dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate and estradiol alongside reduced estrone and estriol in keratoconus patients, with particularly strong associations observed for DHEAS elevation across multiple studies. Clinical risk factors include pregnancy, lactation, menstrual cycle fluctuations, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, in vitro fertilization, hormone replacement therapy, antiandrogen therapy, and male-to-female hormone therapy, each linked to keratoconus onset or progression. The findings suggest that altered levels of DHEAS, estrogens, and gonadotropins are key pathogenic drivers, supporting implementation of targeted screening strategies in at-risk populations undergoing hormonal changes or therapies.

title: Exploring dietary habits strongly associated with pancreatic cancer from the perspective of Mendelian randomization
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study identifies both genetic and dietary factors influencing pancreatic cancer risk. Genetic mutations in KRAS, CDKN2A, TP53, BRCA1, BRCA2, and ATM are major etiological drivers, while environmental factors including alcohol consumption, smoking, obesity, and chronic pancreatitis substantially elevate cancer risk. Notably, alcohol intake frequency and average weekly spirits consumption strongly increase pancreatic cancer incidence, with acetaldehyde accumulation from alcohol metabolism promoting pancreatic inflammation and tumor progression. Conversely, protective dietary interventions are significant: consumption of cooked vegetables, leafy greens, cruciferous vegetables, berries, and citrus fruits substantially reduce pancreatic cancer incidence through phytochemical mechanisms including antioxidant effects, immune stimulation, tumor suppressor gene induction, and apoptosis activation. Metabolic dysfunction—including obesity-induced adipose tissue inflammation, intrapancreatic fat deposition, hyperglycemia, and low high-density lipoprotein cholesterol—collectively promote cancer development through dysregulated inflammatory pathways.

title: Mathematical modeling of bone remodeling after surgical menopause
summary:
# Summary

This mathematical modeling study examines how surgical menopause triggers rapid bone loss through estrogen-dependent mechanisms. Bilateral oophorectomy causes sudden estrogen deficiency, which normally prevents osteocyte apoptosis and suppresses sclerostin production; following surgery, osteocytes undergo apoptosis and increase sclerostin secretion, a signaling molecule that inhibits the Wnt pathway necessary for osteoblast differentiation while simultaneously promoting RANKL-mediated osteoclast activation. The model demonstrates that estrogen withdrawal simultaneously shifts bone remodeling toward resorption by preventing osteoclastogenesis while accelerating osteoclast differentiation, resulting in accelerated bone loss characteristic of surgical menopause. Notably, the temporary increase in osteocyte apoptosis following surgery paradoxically reduces sclerostin production as the osteocyte population declines, which slows the ongoing bone loss despite the initially harmful apoptotic event. This integrated computational framework reveals that osteocytes serve as critical mechanotransducers and signaling hubs that regulate both bone formation and resorption in response to estrogen status, with implications for understanding bone complications in surgical endometriosis treatment.

title: Night-restricted feeding preserves the ovarian circadian rhythm of IL-17 and promotes follicular development in prepubertal and pubertal rabbits
summary: Night-restricted feeding (NRF) synchronizes ovarian circadian rhythms and accelerates reproductive development in prepubertal and pubertal rabbits by establishing a robust nocturnal oscillation of IL-17 signaling that peaks at night. This rhythmic IL-17 stimulation enhances expression of core circadian clock genes (BMAL1, CLOCK) and the cell cycle regulator WEE1, collectively promoting granulosa cell proliferation and follicular development while suppressing apoptosis. NRF establishes distinct temporal patterns of gene expression, with immune and proliferation-associated pathways peaking at night alongside cell cycle and DNA replication activities, while steroid hormone biosynthesis concentrates during daytime hours. Consequently, rabbits under NRF show earlier puberty onset, increased antral follicles, elevated Ki67 expression in granulosa cells, and diurnal oscillations in FSH levels that are absent in daytime-fed controls. The study demonstrates that feeding time acts as a powerful zeitgeber controlling ovarian circadian rhythm entrainment, with misalignment between feeding and the light-dark cycle disrupting metabolic hormone rhythmicity and impairing follicular development. These findings suggest that temporal alignment of nutrient availability with the ovarian circadian clock is fundamental to optimizing reproductive maturation and follicular development.

title: Recent Advances in Anti-Mullerian Hormone (AMH)-Related Osteoporosis Research
summary:
# Summary: AMH and Osteoporosis Research

Anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), a transforming growth factor-β superfamily member secreted by ovarian granulosa cells, plays dual roles in regulating both ovarian function and bone metabolism. As an ovary-specific marker that decreases earlier than FSH or estrogen during reproductive aging, AMH serves as a sensitive indicator of ovarian reserve and is particularly relevant during the menopausal transition when accelerated bone resorption occurs. Mechanistically, AMH inhibits osteoclast differentiation by suppressing the RANKL-NF-κB pathway while simultaneously promoting osteoblast function through SMAD1 signaling, upregulating osteogenic genes (RUNX2 and OSX) and increasing mineralization. AMH receptor type II is expressed on both osteoblasts and hypothalamic GnRH neurons, enabling direct effects on bone cells independent of traditional hormonal pathways. These findings suggest that AMH may serve as a valuable biomarker for identifying women at risk of postmenopausal osteoporosis and represent a potential therapeutic target for preventing bone loss, particularly in conditions characterized by altered AMH levels such as polycystic ovary syndrome or diminished ovarian reserve.

title: DNA Methylation in the Ovary and Uterus of Mammalian Animal Models: Implications for Reproductive Function
summary: DNA methylation is a critical epigenetic mechanism that regulates gene expression and maintains genome stability, with essential roles in mammalian reproductive function. The process is controlled by DNA methyltransferases (DNMT1, DNMT3A, and DNMT3B), which establish and maintain methylation patterns primarily at CpG islands in gene promoters, while ten-eleven translocation enzymes (TET1, TET2, TET3) catalyze active demethylation through sequential oxidation of 5-methylcytosine. In the ovary and uterus, DNA methylation regulates critical processes including folliculogenesis, oocyte maturation, steroidogenesis, ovarian follicle growth, steroid hormone production, and uterine remodeling, with methylation levels dynamically changing during oocyte development and meiosis. Abnormal DNA methylation patterns are associated with multiple reproductive pathologies, including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, endometrial cancer, and infertility, as well as developmental defects when imprinted genes are improperly methylated. The balance between methylation and demethylation is particularly important for preventing gene silencing through hypermethylation while maintaining appropriate tissue-specific gene expression patterns necessary for normal reproductive function.

title: Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome, Menopause and Hormone Replacement Therapy: Clinical Decisions in Times of Uncertainty
summary: Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome (POTS) predominantly affects women of reproductive age, with symptom onset frequently coinciding with menarche, pregnancy, or the postpartum period, and worsens cyclically before or during menstruation in the majority of patients. The condition involves multiple pathophysiologic mechanisms including autoimmune dysfunction (evidenced by elevated antinuclear antibodies and antibodies against autonomic nervous system receptors), mast cell hyperactivity, vascular dysfunction, and preload failure. Estrogen plays a critical modulatory role in POTS pathophysiology by activating mast cells and modulating autonomic nervous system function; the loss of estrogen during menopause is associated with sympathetic overactivity, decreased heart-rate variability, and increased sympathetic dominance. Hormone replacement therapy, particularly transdermal estradiol, suppresses muscle sympathetic nerve activity and improves autonomic control, whereas oral estrogen-progestin formulations carry increased risks of venous thromboembolism and cardiovascular disease. Approximately half of women with POTS experience symptom improvement during pregnancy when estrogen levels are substantially elevated, while postpartum hormonal withdrawal triggers symptom exacerbation in at least half of affected women. The association between POTS and gynecologic disorders including endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome suggests shared hormonal and immune-mediated pathogenic pathways.

title: Prenatal and postnatal atrazine-induced endocrine and ovarian chemical biotransformation protein disruption in offspring
summary:
# Summary

Prenatal and postnatal atrazine (ATZ) exposure disrupts reproductive development and ovarian function through multiple endocrine and biochemical mechanisms. ATZ exposure during gestation and lactation significantly reduced birth weight, body weight at postnatal day 10, and umbilical cord blood progesterone concentrations while tending to increase estradiol levels, creating an unfavorable hormonal environment. The herbicide impaired ovarian biotransformation capacity by reducing critical cytochrome P450 enzymes (CYP1A1, CYP1B1, CYP2E1, CYP11A1) and drug transporter proteins (ABCB1, GSTP1), compromising the ovary's ability to metabolize xenobiotics and steroids. ATZ also suppressed PI3K/AKT signaling and PPARα abundance, which normally regulate ovarian follicle viability, steroidogenesis, and fatty acid metabolism. Additionally, ATZ exposure increased PARP1 abundance while decreasing RAD51, suggesting impaired DNA damage repair capacity that may compromise oocyte genomic integrity. Overall, these findings demonstrate that perinatal ATZ exposure causes reproductive dysfunction and behavioral abnormalities in adulthood through disruption of ovarian steroid metabolism, xenobiotic detoxification, and DNA repair pathways.

title: Mapping the variability of in vivo rat uterine electrical properties throughout the oestrous cycle
summary:
# Summary

The oestrous cycle regulates uterine electrical activity through cyclical variations in oestrogen and progesterone, which modulate the electrical properties essential for reproduction and uterine health. Uterine smooth muscle cells generate action potentials via membrane depolarization that triggers voltage-gated L-type calcium channels, leading to calcium influx and activation of the contractile apparatus; this electrical activity drives distinct contraction patterns critical for sperm transport and menstrual lining expulsion. The cycle directs propagation direction such that ovarian-cervical contractions predominate during menses to expel tissue, while cervico-fundal propagation emerges during the peri-ovulatory phase to facilitate sperm mobility toward the ovaries. Uterine electrical activity varies in frequency and propagation patterns across cycle stages, with the oestrus phase showing the highest frequency of electrical events and shortest intervals between events. Notably, dysfunctional uterine electrical activity is linked to endometriosis, dysmenorrhoea, and infertility, suggesting that abnormalities in hormone-regulated electrical signaling underlie these reproductive disorders. This mapping of normal electrical variability provides a framework for understanding the pathophysiology of uterine dysfunction in endometriosis.

title: Detecting the body’s reproductive hormonal brake against tissue overgrowth: Micrin/SgII-70
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies micrin/SgII-70, a peptide derived from secretogranin II, as a novel reproductive hormonal inhibitor of tissue overgrowth produced by the ovaries. Micrin functions as an antiorganotrophic factor that selectively suppresses pituitary growth while stimulating adrenal development, establishing an "antihypophysiotrophic" hormonal brake against excessive organ enlargement. The peptide counteracts estrogen's strong organotrophic effects across multiple organs including the heart, liver, and uterus, and its activity is enhanced by the anti-estrogen clomiphene. Micrin agonists show therapeutic potential for treating endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and benign prostatic hyperplasia, while antagonists may benefit infertility treatment. The characterization of this ~10 kDa negatively charged, protease-resistant peptide provides mechanistic insight into how the body regulates reproductive hormone-driven tissue proliferation and offers a novel biological target for reproductive and developmental disorders.

title: Spontaneous Regression of Meningiomas: Literature Review and Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This literature review examines spontaneous regression of meningiomas, tumors whose growth is strongly dependent on sex hormones, particularly progesterone. The primary mechanism of regression identified is discontinuation of progesterone agonists and other hormone-containing medications, with documented cases of regression following menopause and pregnancy when circulating sex hormone levels decreased. Beyond hormonal factors, multiple alternative pathways to regression were documented, including immunologically mediated apoptosis, infections, intratumoral hemorrhage, and use of interferon β-1a or α-1 adrenergic receptor antagonists, though these cases were less common. Age emerged as a significant factor, with younger patients showing greater tumor growth potential and older patients demonstrating regression potentially linked to vascular changes and microangiopathy. For clinical management, the authors affirm that surgical excision remains the gold standard for symptomatic meningiomas, while asymptomatic cases warrant conservative approaches with close radiological follow-up given the demonstrated capacity for spontaneous regression.

title: Ovarian Rev-erbα: a central regulator of fertility following chronodisruption
summary:
# Summary

Circadian rhythm disruption caused by shift work and modern life stressors impairs ovarian function and fertility by reducing expression of the circadian nuclear receptor Rev-erbα and associated clock genes, leading to menstrual irregularities, impaired follicle development, and altered hormone secretion. Rev-erbα functions as a central regulator of fertility by promoting folliculogenesis, steroidogenesis, oocyte maturation, and release while stabilizing the molecular clock in ovarian cells. Treatment with SR9011, a Rev-erbα ligand, effectively restored fertility in circadian-disrupted mice by recovering follicular cell proliferation, enhancing progesterone and melatonin secretion, reducing follicular atresia, and promoting normal follicle development. These findings establish Rev-erbα as a key therapeutic target for restoring reproductive function in individuals experiencing chronodisruption-related infertility, with potential applications for managing circadian rhythm-associated reproductive disorders including endometriosis-related fertility complications.

title: Bridging tradition and innovation: electroacupuncture’s impact on premature ovarian insufficiency
summary:
# Summary

Electroacupuncture (EA) emerges as a promising therapeutic approach for premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) through multiple coordinated mechanisms, including upregulation of follicle-stimulating hormone, estrogen, and anti-Müllerian hormone while suppressing granulosa cell apoptosis and inhibiting the mTOR/4E-BP1 signaling pathway. The therapy also improves insulin resistance and enhances gut microbiota diversity, which itself regulates estrogen metabolism and ovarian function. POI involves complex etiological factors spanning genetic abnormalities (X chromosome mutations, FMR1, NR5A1, and MCM9 mutations), autoimmune mechanisms (anti-ovarian antibodies and associated thyroid/rheumatologic diseases), environmental toxicants (phthalates, bisphenol A, cadmium, and air pollution), and infections (mumps, cytomegalovirus, and SARS-CoV-2), all converging on reduced follicle reserves and impaired oocyte maturation. Beyond EA, emerging regenerative therapies including stem cell transplantation, exosome therapy, in vitro activation, and platelet-rich plasma injection represent novel treatment strategies for this condition. POI carries substantial comorbidity risks including osteoporosis, cardiovascular disease, and lipid metabolism abnormalities, making comprehensive management critical for long-term health outcomes.

title: The Hidden Link Between Endometriosis and Obesity: A State-of-the-Art Review
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the paradoxical relationship between endometriosis and body weight, revealing that endometriosis is counterintuitively associated with low BMI, partly because chronic pelvic pain reduces appetite and limits weight gain. The key mechanistic link involves adipose tissue-derived signaling molecules (adipokines) including leptin, adiponectin, and inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-6), which promote inflammation, angiogenesis, and cell proliferation in ectopic endometrial tissue. Elevated leptin levels in the peritoneal fluid of endometriosis patients activate leptin receptors on ectopic endometrium, driving disease progression, while low adiponectin—associated with both obesity and endometriosis—fails to provide its protective anti-inflammatory effects. Importantly, endometriosis and obesity share common pathological pathways involving macrophage infiltration and pro-inflammatory signaling, suggesting that metabolic dysfunction contributes to endometriosis development, particularly in individuals consuming high-fat diets. The review highlights glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) as a promising therapeutic agent that simultaneously treats obesity, reduces inflammation and macrophage infiltration, and possesses anti-fibrotic properties potentially beneficial for endometriosis management.

title: Preclinical research platform for uterine leiomyoma: construction optimization and selection of animal models
summary:
# Summary

Uterine leiomyomas (fibroids) develop through combined hormonal and genetic mechanisms, with abnormal estrogen and progesterone signaling synergistically promoting tumor formation alongside genetic factors such as Tsc2 mutations, which activate the mTOR pathway, and MED12 mutations. The article evaluates multiple preclinical animal models for studying this disease, including spontaneous models (Eker rats, Japanese quail, miniature pigs), hormone-induced models using estrogen and progesterone treatment, genetically engineered models using Cre-loxP technology, and xenograft models with human tissue implantation in immunodeficient mice. Each model type offers distinct advantages: spontaneous and genetically engineered models reveal interactions between genetic background and pathological mechanisms; hormone-induced models enable rapid pharmacological screening; and xenograft models best replicate human tumor pathophysiology. Current therapeutic approaches rely primarily on surgical intervention, which carries significant risks including trauma, fertility impairment, and disease recurrence, making preclinical drug validation platforms essential for developing alternative treatments. The authors highlight critical research gaps, particularly the need to incorporate immune system interactions and extracellular matrix remodeling into model systems, and emphasize species-specific differences in reproductive endocrinology that may limit cross-translational fidelity.

title: Electroacupuncture may improve endometrial receptivity in controlled ovarian hyperstimulation mice by activating AVP neurons
summary:
# Summary

Controlled ovarian hyperstimulation impairs endometrial receptivity by disrupting circadian clock gene expression and endometrial architecture, reducing key markers including Hoxa10, Spp1, and Maoa while elevating progesterone levels. Electroacupuncture ameliorates these COH-induced defects by activating arginine vasopressin neurons in the suprachiasmatic nucleus, which serve as the central pacemaker for circadian rhythms and orchestrate the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis. Through AVP neuron activation, electroacupuncture restores circadian rhythmicity of reproductive axis clock genes, upregulates endometrial receptivity markers, and reduces elevated progesterone levels that suppress secretory transformation. The therapeutic effects of electroacupuncture depend critically on AVP signaling, as demonstrated by the attenuation of beneficial effects when AVP antagonists are co-administered, and by the ability of AVP agonists alone to replicate electroacupuncture's effects. These findings suggest that electroacupuncture enhances implantation outcomes in assisted reproductive settings by restoring the circadian coordination of endometrial receptivity during the implantation window.

title: Immune modulation by sex hormones: unveiling the complex interplay and regulatory networks
summary: Sex hormones—including estrogens, androgens, and progesterone—serve as critical regulators of immune system development and function, establishing sexual dimorphism in disease susceptibility and immune responses. Estrogen promotes pro-inflammatory pathways by enhancing CD4+ and CD8+ T cell activity, elevating autoreactive B cells, and increasing pro-inflammatory cytokine production, effects that contribute to autoimmune conditions such as systemic lupus erythematosus, particularly during pregnancy when placental estrogen production surges. In contrast, androgens exert predominantly immunosuppressive effects by inhibiting airway inflammation through type 2 innate lymphoid cell suppression, promoting regulatory T cell development via Foxp3 upregulation, and reducing macrophage and dendritic cell activation. Progesterone complements androgen-mediated immunosuppression by inhibiting pro-inflammatory macrophage responses and dendritic cell activation while promoting M2 macrophage differentiation and expanding regulatory T cells. These hormonal effects are mediated through sex hormone receptors—estrogen receptors alpha and beta, androgen receptors, and their variants—which are differentially expressed across immune tissues and regulate cellular gene expression and inflammatory signaling pathways. The integrated hormonal control of immunity through steroid hormone synthesis, bioavailability via sex hormone-binding globulin, and tissue-specific receptor signaling provides a mechanistic framework for understanding sex-based differences in immune-mediated diseases including endometriosis.

title: Associations of surgical menopause and hormone replacement therapy with meningioma development
summary:
# Summary

This study examines the hormonal regulation of meningioma development in relation to surgical menopause and hormone replacement therapy. Bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO) alone reduces meningioma risk, likely due to abrupt cessation of estrogen and progesterone signaling, which mediate meningioma growth through estrogen and progesterone receptor expression. However, subsequent hormone replacement therapy significantly increases meningioma risk, with the combined BSO plus HRT approach elevating both overall and cranial meningioma incidence compared to either intervention alone. This elevated risk is particularly pronounced in patients with hormonally-driven surgical indications such as endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and gynecologic malignancy, and persists regardless of HRT initiation timing or method. The findings highlight a critical clinical paradox: while HRT effectively mitigates vasomotor symptoms, osteoporosis, and cardiometabolic complications following surgical menopause, it substantially increases meningioma incidence, requiring careful risk stratification in surgical decision-making for endometriosis and other hormonally-sensitive conditions.

title: Ovarian Escape in Hormone Receptor-Positive Breast Cancer: Mechanisms, Risk Factors, Clinical Consequences, and Management Strategies
summary:
# Summary

Hormone receptor-positive breast cancer, which comprises 70-80% of breast cancers, is primarily driven by estrogen, making estrogen suppression a critical therapeutic strategy in premenopausal women. Ovarian function suppression (OFS) combined with endocrine therapy—particularly gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonists or antagonists—significantly reduces cancer recurrence and improves outcomes. GnRH agonists work through an initial stimulatory phase followed by sustained receptor downregulation that suppresses follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone, thereby reducing estradiol synthesis; however, GnRH antagonists avoid this flare effect by immediately blocking endogenous GnRH signaling. Despite these mechanisms, ovarian escape—the incomplete suppression of ovarian estrogen production—occurs in a subset of patients, particularly younger women (age ≤35 years), those with higher body mass index, and those lacking prior chemotherapy, due to factors including greater ovarian reserve, FSH rebound, adipose tissue aromatase activity, and antibody development against GnRH agonists. Management strategies to overcome ovarian escape include combining OFS with tamoxifen, which prevents FSH recovery and reduces estradiol concentrations, or avoiding aromatase inhibitors that may paradoxically stimulate residual ovarian activity and trigger ovulation.

title: Short-term hormonal modulation with mifepristone does not induce oncogenic changes in the endometrium of BRCA1/2 pathogenic variant carriers
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated the endometrial safety of mifepristone, a progesterone receptor antagonist under investigation for breast cancer prevention in BRCA1/2 pathogenic variant carriers. While progesterone normally acts as a tumor suppressor in the endometrium by counteracting estrogen-driven proliferation, blocking progesterone signaling raises concerns about unopposed estrogen stimulation and potential carcinogenesis. However, three months of mifepristone treatment induced amenorrhea in all participants without increasing epithelial cell proportions or triggering oncogenic molecular changes, as demonstrated by stable DNA methylation and gene expression indices associated with endometrial cancer. Multi-omics analyses revealed no molecular signatures consistent with endometrial carcinogenesis, and ultrasound measurements showed no significant changes in endometrial thickness. These findings suggest that short-term mifepristone exposure does not induce the molecular alterations linked to endometrial cancer development in this high-risk population, providing reassurance regarding its endometrial safety as a potential preventive strategy in BRCA1/2 carriers.

title: Sex hormone-mediated corneal remodeling in keratoconus: a multivariate analysis of tomographic and biomechanical variations across gender, age, and menstrual phases
summary: Sex hormones, particularly estradiol and testosterone, play a significant role in keratoconus progression through effects on corneal morphology and biomechanics. Male keratoconus patients exhibit significantly lower serum estradiol and testosterone levels compared to healthy controls across all age groups, with these hormones showing protective associations—higher estradiol and testosterone levels correlate with improved corneal biomechanical parameters including reduced keratoconus indices and enhanced corneal resistance. The biological mechanism involves sex hormone receptor expression (estrogen receptor α, androgen receptor, and progesterone receptor) in corneal epithelial cells, where estradiol upregulates pro-inflammatory genes while also downregulating matrix metalloproteinase-2, and androgens demonstrate anti-inflammatory effects that may mitigate corneal remodeling. Age and menstrual phase significantly modulate these relationships, with distinct hormone-corneal parameter correlations observed in different age groups and among females during the luteal phase. Keratoconus progression during puberty and pregnancy, coupled with stabilization after menopause, suggests that fluctuating sex hormone levels fundamentally influence corneal biomechanics and inflammation, implicating sex hormone dysregulation as a key pathogenic factor in keratoconus development and progression.

title: Effect of growth hormone adjuvant treatment on oocyte retrieval in patients with poor ovarian response and no viable embryos in previous in vitro fertilization (IVF) cycles
summary: Growth hormone (GH) adjuvant treatment significantly improves reproductive outcomes in patients with poor ovarian response by activating multiple ovarian pathways. GH stimulates insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) production in the ovaries, which enhances steroidogenesis and stimulates granulosa cell function through paracrine and autocrine mechanisms. Additionally, GH directly enhances oocyte maturation by interacting with GH receptors located on oocytes and increases luteinizing hormone (LH) receptor expression on granulosa cells while stimulating ovarian aromatase activity, thereby optimizing gonadotropin sensitivity. In this patient population, GH adjuvant treatment significantly increased the number of oocytes retrieved, bipronuclear embryos, and transferable embryos compared to control cycles, with elevated LH levels on the human chorionic gonadotropin trigger day identified as an independent predictor of oocyte retrieval success. Poor ovarian response is characterized by elevated baseline LH and FSH levels that impair follicular maturity through multiple mechanisms, including excessive androgen synthesis and granulosa cell apoptosis leading to follicle atresia. These findings support GH as an effective adjuvant therapy to improve embryo quality and oocyte yield in patients with poor ovarian response who had no viable embryos in previous IVF cycles.

title: The epidemiology and pathogenesis of uterine fibroids
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Summary

Uterine leiomyomas (fibroids) are benign smooth muscle tumors that cause significant morbidity including abnormal uterine bleeding, infertility, and pressure symptoms, with a complex etiology involving both genetic and environmental factors. Early menarche, nulliparity, obesity with elevated estrogen levels, high red meat consumption, and vitamin D deficiency increase fibroid risk, while increased parity, late menarche, high intake of green vegetables and fruits, and smoking appear protective. The genetic basis involves mutations in MED12 and HMGA2 genes, with MED12 mutations associated with smaller fibroids, while FH gene mutations cause hereditary leiomyomatosis with renal cell carcinoma risk. Pathologically, fibroids accumulate abnormally high levels of type I and III collagen compared to normal myometrium, and vitamin D inhibits leiomyoma cell growth through vitamin D receptors while downregulating estrogen and progesterone receptor expression. Treatment options include depot medroxyprogesterone acetate and continuous progestins, with fibroid regression occurring postmenopausally as estradiol and progesterone decline, and greater shrinkage rates observed in women with BMI less than 25 kg/m². Notably, women of African ancestry experience significantly higher prevalence, earlier onset (10-15 years), and larger fibroids, with vitamin D deficiency particularly prevalent in this population.

title: Optimizing oral contraceptive timing: Daytime intake reduces doses and enhances efficacy
summary:
# Summary

Oral contraceptives treat endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome by inhibiting ovulation through suppression of pituitary hormones, particularly follicle-stimulating hormone and luteinizing hormone, while keeping progesterone levels below the contraceptive threshold of 3 ng/mL. This study demonstrates that oral contraceptive timing significantly impacts efficacy and safety by leveraging circadian rhythms that regulate reproductive hormone secretion, drug metabolism, and the natural peaks of luteinizing hormone production. Daytime dosing at 11:00 AM aligns ethinyl estradiol and dienogest concentration peaks with the circadian luteinizing hormone peak, reducing required ethinyl estradiol doses by approximately 6% and dienogest doses by 52% compared to evening administration at 22:00, while also decreasing nausea and venous thromboembolism risk. A nonconstant dosing regimen optimized for circadian synchronization achieved a remarkable 67% reduction in total hormone exposure and shortened treatment duration to just 8 days, demonstrating how dual-timescale pharmacological optimization can enhance contraceptive efficacy while minimizing adverse effects. The circadian rhythm of luteinizing hormone emerged as the primary driver of dose variation across different administration times, with optimal efficacy consistently achieved in the 10:00-12:00 AM window. These findings establish that chronopharmacology—tailoring medication timing to endogenous biological rhythms—represents a novel strategy for improving hormonal contraceptive management in endometriosis and related reproductive disorders.

title: Mevalonate supplementation to enhance euploid embryo formation in women of advanced maternal age: a study protocol for a randomized controlled trial
summary: This randomized controlled trial investigates mevalonate (MVA) pathway supplementation as a strategy to improve embryo quality in women of advanced maternal age (AMA). Advanced maternal age drives meiotic chromosome segregation errors that result in oocyte aneuploidy, a major cause of pregnancy failure and age-related fertility decline. The study targets the mevalonate pathway, a central metabolic route regulating cholesterol biosynthesis and homeostasis, which supports oocyte maturation through luteinizing hormone receptor and epidermal growth factor signaling. Prior research shows that MVA-related gene activity is markedly suppressed in granulosa cells from AMA patients, and animal studies demonstrate that exogenous MVA compounds can correct meiotic abnormalities and reduce aneuploidy rates. The trial will evaluate whether brief in vitro MVA supplementation prior to fertilization increases euploid embryo yield, with outcomes assessed through biochemical pregnancy confirmation via serum β-hCG and clinical pregnancy confirmation via transvaginal ultrasound detection of fetal cardiac activity. These findings may establish MVA supplementation as an adjunctive treatment to enhance fertility outcomes in AMA populations.

title: Impact of fertility treatments on headache disorders: a systematic review with an overview of treatment modalities
journal: The Journal of Headache and Pain
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review examines the complex interplay between fertility treatments and headache disorders, with particular relevance to women with endometriosis-associated comorbidities. Migraine is highly prevalent during reproductive years and closely associated with hormonal fluctuations, endometriosis, thyroid disorders, and polycystic ovary syndrome, creating a vulnerable population for treatment-related complications. Fertility medications trigger headaches through distinct mechanisms: clomiphene and selective estrogen receptor modulators (SERMs) directly induce migraine in susceptible women, while aromatase inhibitors like letrozole, despite superior live birth rates, cause headaches in 8–13% of users compared to 1–9% with SERMs. Gonadotropins carry additional risks including ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome, which has been linked to severe headache and potentially cerebrovascular events in case reports. Paradoxically, while women with migraine often avoid pregnancy due to symptom concerns, clinical evidence shows migraine improves in approximately two-thirds of pregnant women, suggesting that careful treatment selection and counseling are essential for optimizing both reproductive outcomes and headache management in this population.

title: Nanotechnology for reproductive healthcare: a comprehensive review
summary: Nanotechnology represents a transformative approach to treating reproductive disorders, particularly endometriosis, through engineered nanomaterials that enable targeted drug delivery and hormone regulation. Multiple nanoparticle platforms—including liposomes, polymeric nanoparticles, lipid nanoparticles, and chitosan-based carriers—have demonstrated efficacy in delivering therapeutic agents such as curcumin, dexamethasone, and gonadotropin-releasing hormone to affected tissues. A clinical trial showed that oral curcumin nanomicelles significantly reduced elevated inflammatory markers (TNF-α and IL-8) in endometriosis patients while improving fertility outcomes, highlighting the mechanism by which anti-inflammatory nanotechnology addresses the disease's hormonal dysregulation. Iron-oxide magnetic nanoparticles enable dual functionality through image-guided therapy and hyperthermia, offering both diagnostic and therapeutic capabilities for endometriosis and reproductive cancers. Progesterone and estrogen nanocarriers achieve enhanced bioavailability and controlled release, directly addressing infertility management and broader hormonal regulation in the female reproductive system. This comprehensive nanotechnology arsenal demonstrates clinical potential for treating endometriosis alongside other reproductive conditions through precision medicine approaches.

title: Placental Steroid Hormones in Preeclampsia: Multilayered Regulation of Endocrine Pathogenesis
summary:
# Summary

The placenta, particularly its syncytiotrophoblast layer, functions as the predominant endocrine organ during pregnancy, producing steroid hormones through a complex multilayered regulatory system involving microRNAs, histone acetylation, O-linked β-N-acetylglucosamine, and melatonin. Early-onset preeclampsia is characterized by profound disruption in placental steroid hormone balance, marked by elevated androgen synthesis and suppressed estrogen production, with elevated placental testosterone causally linked to disease pathogenesis. MicroRNA-22 and O-GlcNAcylation of key proteins like GATA3 regulate placental sex hormone balance and androgen receptor-mediated syncytialization, while hypoxic stress and proinflammatory signals impair these processes.
